His saying, the Exalted: سَواءٌ مِنْكُمْ مَنْ أَسَرَّ الْقَوْلَ وَمَنْ جَهَرَ بِهِ وَمَنْ هُوَ مُسْتَخْفٍ بِاللَّيْلِ وَسارِبٌ بِالنَّهارِ — It is the same [to Him] whether any of you speaks secretly or aloud, and whether he hides by night or goes out by day (13:10). Sarab (السرب), with two fathas, and surub (السروب) mean going down a slope, the flowing of tears, or going along any path. It is said: saraba sarban wa suruban, like marra marran wa mururan. Thus it is in al-Mufradat. So, the sarib (السارب) is the one going along the path, making himself manifest.

The verse is like a derivation (تفريع) from the previous verse: if Allah, the Glorified, is the Knower of the Unseen and the sensible equally, then it is the same among you whether one conceals his speech or makes it public—Allah knows the speech of both and hears their discourse without the secret of the one who whispers being hidden from Him. Likewise, it is the same among you whether one is hiding by night (مستخف بالليل), seeking aid from the darkness of the night and the lowering of its veils to hide from the eyes of onlookers, or one is going out by day (سارب بالنهار), walking on his path, manifest and not hiding himself. Allah knows both of them, and the one hiding by night is not hidden from Him by his stratagem.

His saying, the Exalted: لَهُ مُعَقِّباتٌ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ يَحْفَظُونَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ — He has guardian angels [who follow him] before him and behind him, guarding him by Allah’s command (13:11), etc. The apparent context suggests that the four pronouns—"for him" (لَهُ), "before him" (يَدَيْهِ), "behind him" (خَلْفِهِ), and "guarding him" (يَحْفَظُونَهُ)—all refer to the same antecedent. No antecedent is suitable for all of them except the relative pronoun in His saying: مَنْ أَسَرَّ الْقَوْلَ — "whoever speaks secretly," etc. Thus, it is this human being, whom Allah knows in all his states, who has "successive guardians" (معقبات) before him and behind him.

The "succession" (تعقيب) of a thing only occurs by coming after it and approaching from its rear ('aqib). Describing the guardians as being "before him and behind him" (مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ) is only conceivable if he is traveling on a path, with the guardians circling around him. Allah has informed us of man being a traveler in this journey by His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ إِلى رَبِّكَ كَدْحاً فَمُلاقِيهِ — O man! You are laboring toward your Lord laboriously, and you will encounter Him (84:6), and in the meaning of other verses indicating his return to his Lord, such as: وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ — and to Him you will be brought back (36:83), and وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ — and to Him you will be returned (29:21). Thus, for man, as he journeys toward his Lord, there are successive guardians watching him from before him and behind him.

Furthermore, it is known from the Quranic perspective that man is not merely this physical frame and material body; rather, he is an existent composed of a soul (نفس) and a body. The primary aspect regarding his affairs is his soul; it possesses consciousness and will, it is the object of command and prohibition, and through it, reward and punishment, comfort and pain, felicity (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاء) are established. From it proceed righteous and wicked deeds, and to it are attributed faith (الإيمان) and disbelief (الكفر), even if the body is like a tool utilized for its purposes and needs.

Accordingly, the meaning of what is "before" man and "behind" him expands to encompass both physical and spiritual matters. All bodies and corporeal things that surround man's body throughout his life—some situated in front of him and some behind him—are included. Likewise, all the psychological stages that man traverses in his journey toward his Lord, the spiritual states he undergoes and fluctuates within—such as proximity and distance—felicity and wretchedness, righteous and wicked deeds, and the reward and punishment stored for them—all of these are situated behind man or before him. These successive guardians mentioned by Allah have a role in these matters insofar as they are connected to man.

Man, whom Allah described as possessing for himself neither harm nor benefit, neither death nor life nor resurrection, is unable to preserve anything of himself or the traces of himself, whether present with him or absent from him. It is only Allah, the Glorified, who preserves them for him. The Exalted said: اللهُ حَفِيظٌ عَلَيْهِمْ — Allah is watchful over them (42:6), and وَرَبُّكَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ حَفِيظٌ — and your Lord is watchful over all things (34:21). Regarding the intermediaries in this matter, He said: وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكُمْ لَحافِظِينَ — Indeed, there are over you watchers (82:10).

Were it not for His preservation of these things through these intermediaries—whom He called "watchers" (حافظين) at times and "successive guardians" (معقبات) at others—annihilation (الفناء) would encompass man from all sides, and destruction would hasten toward him from before him and behind him. However, just as their preservation is by the command of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, so too is their annihilation, destruction, and corruption by the command of Allah. For the kingdom belongs to Allah; none manages His affair or disposes within it except Him, the Glorified. This is what the Quranic teaching guides toward, and the verses on these meanings are numerous, requiring no further citation.

The angels (الملائكة) also only do what they do by His command. The Exalted said: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ — He sends down the angels with the Spirit by His command (16:2), and لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ — They do not anticipate Him in speech, and they act by His command (21:27).

From here it becomes clear that these successive guardians, just as they preserve what they preserve by Allah’s command, they also preserve him "from Allah’s command" (مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ). For the side of annihilation, destruction, loss, and corruption is by Allah’s command, just as the side of subsistence, uprightness, and health is by Allah’s command. No corporeal compound endures except by Allah’s command, just as its composition does not dissolve except by Allah’s command. No spiritual state, deed, or trace of a deed remains firm except by a command from Allah, just as it is not struck by nullification (الحبط) or cessation except by a command from Allah. The entire command belongs to Allah, and to Him all affairs return.

Accordingly, these successive guardians, just as they preserve him by Allah’s command, they also preserve him "from Allah’s command." Thus should His saying in the verse under discussion be understood: يَحْفَظُونَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ — "guarding him from Allah’s command."

From what has preceded, the connection of His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ — Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot unless they change what is in their souls (13:11) becomes clear. It stands as a justification (تعليل) for His saying: يَحْفَظُونَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ. The meaning is that He only established these successive guardians and entrusted them with man to preserve him by His command from His command—preventing him from perishing or changing in any state he is in—because His sunnah has proceeded that He does not change the conditions of a people until they change the spiritual states within their souls. For instance, if they change gratitude (الشكر) into ingratitude (الكفر), obedience into disobedience, or faith into polytheism (الشرك), then Allah changes blessing (النعمة) into vengeance (النقمة), guidance into misguidance, and felicity into wretchedness, and so on.

The verse—namely His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ —indicates in summary that Allah has decreed a definitive judgment (قضاء حتم) of a type of concomitance (تلازم) between the blessings bestowed from Him upon man and the psychological states belonging to man that proceed according to the uprightness of the primordial nature (الفطرة). If a people proceed according to the uprightness of the primordial nature, believe in Allah, and perform righteous deeds (الصالحات), He follows that with the blessings of this world and the hereafter, as He said: وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلكِنْ كَذَّبُوا — If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth. But they denied (7:96). This condition remains firm and permanent for them as long as they remain in that state within themselves. When they change their internal state, Allah, the Glorified, changes their external state by turning blessings into vengeance.

It is possible to derive a general principle from the verse: that there is a type of concomitance between man's psychological states and external conditions, whether on the side of good or evil. If a people are characterized by faith, obedience, and gratitude for blessings, Allah encompasses them with His manifest and hidden blessings, and that continues for them until they change and disbelieve or act wickedly, whereupon Allah changes His blessings into vengeance. This continues until they change again, believing, obeying, and giving thanks, whereupon Allah changes His vengeance into blessings, and so forth.

However, the apparent context does not support this [latter part], especially what follows it: وَإِذا أَرادَ اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوْءاً فَلا مَرَدَّ لَهُ — And when Allah wishes to visit ill on a people, there is nothing that can avert it (13:11). This is the strongest witness that He is describing the meaning of His changing what is with a people until they change. Since the change is toward "ill" (السوء), the original state—namely "what is with a people"—can only refer to "good" (الحسنة). Understand this well.

Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, says: وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ — Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many (42:30). He mentions that He excuses many evil deeds, thereby erasing their effects. Thus, there is no [absolute] concomitance between man's deeds and states and the external effects on the side of evil, unlike the side of good, as the Exalted said in a similar verse: ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّراً نِعْمَةً أَنْعَمَها عَلى قَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ — That is because Allah never changes a blessing that He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls (8:53).

As for His saying: وَإِذا أَرادَ اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوْءاً فَلا مَرَدَّ لَهُ, it entered the discourse not as the primary intent, but because when He mentioned that everything with Him is by a measure, and that every man has successive guardians preserving him by His command from His command—not letting him perish, change, or be disturbed in his existence or the blessings he has been given—and that they are in their state from Allah, He does not change it for them until they change what is in their souls; it became necessary to mention that this change from felicity to wretchedness and from blessing to vengeance is also among the firm, inevitable matters that no preventer can stop from occurring. Its matter belongs solely to Allah, with no share for anyone else. By this, it is completed that people have no escape from Allah’s judgment in both the aspects of good and evil; they are overtaken and within His grasp.

The meaning is: when Allah intends ill for a people—and He only intends it if they change the marks of servanthood (سمات معبودية) and the requirements of the primordial nature within themselves—there is no averting that ill, whether it be wretchedness, vengeance, or exemplary punishment (نكال).

Then His saying: وَما لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ والٍ — and they have no protector besides Him (13:11)—is an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسيري) to His saying: إِذا أَرادَ اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوْءاً فَلا مَرَدَّ لَهُ, and it provides the meaning of a justification for it. For if they have no protector (وال) who manages their affair except Allah, the Glorified, then there is no one there to avert what Allah has intended for them of ill.

It has become clear from all that preceded that the meaning of the verse—according to what the context provides, and Allah knows best—is that for every person, in whatever state he may be, there are successive guardians who follow him in his journey toward Allah, from before him and behind him—that is, in his present state and his past. They preserve him by Allah’s command from his state being changed by destruction, corruption, or wretchedness through another command from Allah. This "other command" that changes the state only exerts its effect if a people change what is in their souls. At that point, Allah changes the blessing they have and intends ill for them; and when Allah intends ill for a people, there is no averting it, for they have no protector to manage their affair besides Him who could avert what Allah intended for them of ill.

From this, several matters have become clear:

First: The verse is like a detailed explanation (بيان تفصيلي) for what preceded in the previous verses: وَكُلُّ شَيْءٍ عِنْدَهُ بِمِقْدارٍ — "and everything with Him is by a measure." That phrase implies that things have fixed limits they do not exceed and from which they do not deviate before Allah, such that they would escape His knowledge. This verse details the case of man: that he has successive guardians before him and behind him entrusted with him, preserving him and all that pertains to him from perishing or changing from what he is upon; he does not perish or change except by another command from Allah.

Second: There is nothing pertaining to man—his soul, his body, his attributes, his states, his deeds, or his traces—except that there is an angel entrusted with it to preserve it. He remains thus in his journey toward Allah until he changes. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is the Preserver (الحافظ), and He has guardian angels (ملائكة حفظة) over him. This is a Quranic reality.

Third: There is another "command" (أمر) lying in wait for people to change what they have. Allah has mentioned regarding this command that it takes effect when a people change what is in their souls. At that point, Allah changes the blessing they have through this command that lies in wait for them. Among the instances of its effect is the arrival of the "appointed term" (الأجل المسمى) which neither differs nor fails. The Exalted said: ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى — We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with consummate truth and for a stated term (46:3), and إِنَّ أَجَلَ اللهِ إِذا جاءَ لا يُؤَخَّرُ — Indeed, when Allah’s term comes, it cannot be deferred (71:4).

Fourth: His command is the dominant (المهيمن) authority over the cores and peripheries of things in every state. Everything, in its stability and its change, is obedient to His command and submissive to His greatness. Although the divine command may be diverse when comparing parts of it to others—divided into a preserving command and a changing command—it possesses a single, unchanging system (نظام). The Exalted said: إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — Indeed my Lord is on a straight path (11:56), and إِنَّما أَمْرُهُ إِذا أَرادَ شَيْئاً أَنْ يَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ فَسُبْحانَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ مَلَكُوتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ — All His command, when He wills something, is to say to it ‘Be,’ and it is. So immaculate is He in whose hand is the dominion of all things (36:82-83).

Fifth: Among the definitive judgments and the prevalent divine sunnahs is the concomitance between excellence (الإحسان), godwariness (التقوى), and gratitude (الشكر) in any people, and the arrival of manifest and hidden blessings and benedictions (البركات) from Allah to them, and their remaining and staying among them as long as they do not change. This is indicated by His saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلكِنْ كَذَّبُوا فَأَخَذْناهُمْ بِما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ — If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth. But they denied; so We seized them because of what they used to earn (7:96), and لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ وَلَئِنْ كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذابِي لَشَدِيدٌ — If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe (14:7), and هَلْ جَزاءُ الْإِحْسانِ إِلَّا الْإِحْسانُ — Is the recompense of goodness anything but goodness? (55:60).

This is what is apparent from the verse regarding the concomitance between the prevalence of righteousness (الصلاح) in a people and the permanence of blessing upon them. As for the prevalence of corruption (الفساد) among them or its appearance from some of them and the descent of vengeance upon them, the verse is silent regarding the concomitance between them. The most that His saying: لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا provides is the permissibility and possibility of His changing [their state] when they change, not its necessity or actuality. Therefore, the context changed, and He said: وَإِذا أَرادَ اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوْءاً فَلا مَرَدَّ لَهُ, and He did not say: "So Allah intends for them an ill that has no averting."

This meaning is supported by His saying: وَما أَصابَكُمْ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فَبِما كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا عَنْ كَثِيرٍ — Whatever affliction may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many (42:30), as it explicitly indicates that some change [of state] upon [human] change is excused.

As for the individual (الفرد) of the species (النوع), the divine speech indicates the concomitance between the righteousness of his deeds and spiritual blessings, and the change [of state] upon [his] change, without [necessarily] a concomitance between his righteousness and corporeal blessings.

The wisdom (الحكمة) in all of this is manifest. The aforementioned concomitance is required by the judgment of harmony and correspondence between the parts of the system and the driving of species toward their goals. For Allah established goals for the species and equipped them with what drives them toward those goals. Then He spread harmony and correspondence between the parts of this system such that the whole became a single thing with no antagonism or contradiction between its parts. The requirement of their nature is that every species lives in well-being, blessing, and dignity until it reaches its goal. If the human species does not deviate from the requirement of its original primordial nature—and no other species apparently deviates—the cosmos proceeds toward its felicity and blessing and does not lack guidance (رشد). However, if it deviates from that and corruption becomes prevalent within it, that upsets the balance between the parts of the cosmos and necessitates the departure of blessing, the disruption of livelihood, and the appearance of corruption on land and sea because of what people's hands have earned, so that Allah may let them taste some of what they have done, that they may perhaps return.

This meaning, as is not hidden, is only fulfilled in the species, not the individual. Therefore, the concomitance was between the righteousness of the species and the general blessings poured upon them. It does not apply to individuals because the goals may be frustrated in individuals, unlike species; for the frustration of the goals of species in the cosmos would imply "play" (اللعب) in creation. The Exalted said: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ — We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play (44:38). Some discussion on this topic has already preceded in the inquiries on deeds (الأعمال) in the second volume of this book.

From what has preceded, the invalidity of the objection against the verse becomes clear—the objection being that the verse apparently implies that the change of blessings for a people does not occur until a change occurs from them through sins, while this is contrary to what is established in the Sharia regarding the impermissibility of holding the general public accountable for the sins of the elite. This [objection] is entirely foreign to the import of the verse.

This is some of what reflection upon the noble verse yields. The commentators have a severe disagreement regarding its interpretation from various aspects:

Among them is their disagreement regarding the antecedent of the pronoun in His saying: لَهُ مُعَقِّباتٌ — "He has successive guardians." Some say the pronoun refers to "whoever" (مَنْ) in His saying: مَنْ أَسَرَّ الْقَوْلَ — "whoever speaks secretly," etc., as we have presented. Others say it refers to the Exalted—meaning, for Allah there are successive angels before man and behind him guarding him. This is refuted by the fact that it necessitates a discrepancy in the pronouns [within the same sentence]. Furthermore, it necessitates a shift (التفات) in His saying: مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ — "by Allah’s command"—without a manifest subtle point. Others say the pronoun refers to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the verse mentions that the angels guard him. This is refuted like the previous one as it necessitates a discrepancy in pronouns, and the apparent meaning is contrary to it. Moreover, it would result in the verse not being connected to what preceded it, and no mention of the Prophet had occurred.

Others say the pronoun refers to "the one who goes out by day" (سارِبٌ بِالنَّهارِ). This is the most absurd of views, and we shall return to it.

Among them is their disagreement regarding the meaning of "successive guardians" (المعقبات). It was said that its root is al-mu'taqibat (المعتقبات), which became mu'aqqibat through transposition and elision; it is said i'taqabahu when he detained him, or the people i'taqabu over him, meaning they cooperated. This was refuted as being an error. It was also said it is from the form taf'il (التفعيل), and ta'qib (التعقيب) is for one to follow another in his walking as if he is treading on his heel ('aqib), i.e., the back of his foot. Thus, it was said the mu'aqqibat are angels who follow man in his journey toward Allah, never parting from him and guarding him, as preceded. It was also said the mu'aqqibat are the scribes of deeds among the angels of the night and the day who succeed one another; the angels of the night succeed the angels of the day, and they succeed the angels of the night, preserving for man his deeds. This is refuted by its being contrary to the apparent meaning of لَهُ مُعَقِّباتٌ, and it involves making "guarding him" (يَحْفَظُونَهُ) mean "guarding [deeds] against him."

It was also said that the mu'aqqibat are the guards, police, and entourages who follow kings and princes. The meaning would be: for the one who goes out by day—namely kings and princes—there are successive guards and police who surround them and "guard them from Allah’s command," i.e., His judgment and decree, out of their delusion that they are capable of that. This view, despite its absurdity, is a play with the speech of the Exalted.

Among them is their disagreement regarding His saying: مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ — "before him and behind him." It was said it is connected to "successive guardians," meaning they follow him from before and behind. This is refuted by the fact that "succession" (التعقيب) is only realized from behind. It was said it is connected to His saying: يَحْفَظُونَهُ — "guarding him"—and there is a transposition (تقديم وتأخير) in the speech, the order being: "guarding him from before him and behind him from Allah’s command." This is refuted by the lack of evidence for it. It was said it is connected to an implied word like "occurring" or "encompassing" or the like, or through a type of inclusion (تضمين), the meaning being: he has successive guardians encompassing him from before him and behind him, as already presented.

From another perspective, it was said that what is "before him and behind him" refers to the spatial direction, i.e., they surround him from his front and his back, guarding him from destructions and dangers. It was said they refer to his past and future deeds, which the guardian angels preserve against him and record. There is no evidence for the restriction in these two views. It was said they refer to man's physical and spiritual affairs in his present state and what he has left behind him, which is what we have presented.

Among them is their disagreement regarding the meaning of His saying: يَحْفَظُونَهُ — "guarding him." It was said it means "guarding [deeds] against him." It was said it is absolute preservation. It was said it is preservation from harms.

Among them is their disagreement regarding His saying: مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ — "from/by Allah’s command." It was said it is connected to "successive guardians," and that "before him and behind him," "guarding him," and "from Allah’s command" are three attributes of the guardians. This is refuted by its being contrary to the apparent meaning. It was said it is connected to "guarding him," and min (مِنْ) means ba (بـ) for causality (السببية) or accompaniment (المصاحبة), the meaning being: they guard him because of Allah’s command or accompanied by Allah’s command. It was said it is connected to "guarding him" and min is for origin (الابتداء) or arising (النشو), meaning: they guard him, that [guarding] originating or arising from Allah’s command. It was said it is likewise, but min means "on behalf of" ('an), i.e., they guard him on behalf of Allah’s command lest it befall him and overwhelm him. They interpreted "guarding from Allah’s command" as "command" meaning "might/punishment" (البأس), i.e., they guard him from Allah’s punishment by seeking respite whenever he sins and asking Allah, the Glorified, to defer the reckoning and punishment or the execution of his wretchedness, that he might perhaps repent and return. The invalidity of most of these views is manifest and requires no further explanation.

Among them is their disagreement regarding the connection of His saying: لَهُ مُعَقِّباتٌ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ etc. It was said it is connected to His saying: سارِبٌ بِالنَّهارِ — "goes out by day"—and its meaning has preceded. It was said it is connected to His saying: اللهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تَحْمِلُ كُلُّ أُنْثى — "Allah knows what every female carries"—or His saying: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ — "Knower of the Unseen and the sensible"—meaning: just as He knows them, He placed over them guardians to preserve them. It was said it is connected to His saying: إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ — "You are only a warner"—meaning that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is preserved by the angels. The truth is that it is connected to His saying: وَكُلُّ شَيْءٍ عِنْدَهُ بِمِقْدارٍ — "and everything with Him is by a measure"—and is a type of explanation for it, as already mentioned.

Among them is their disagreement regarding the connection of His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ etc. It was said it is connected to His saying: وَيَسْتَعْجِلُونَكَ بِالْعَذابِ — "They ask you to hasten the punishment"—meaning that He does not send down the punishment except upon those from whom He knows a change [of state], such that if He knew there were among them those who would believe in Allah or those in their loins who would be born and live with faith, He would not send the punishment upon them. It was said it is connected to سارِبٌ بِالنَّهارِ, meaning that if he commits sins, he has changed the mark of servanthood upon him, his preservation is nullified, and the punishment descends upon him. Both views—as you see—are far from the context. The truth is that His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ etc., is a justification for what preceded it: يَحْفَظُونَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ, as already explained.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمُ الْبَرْقَ خَوْفاً وَطَمَعاً وَيُنْشِئُ السَّحابَ الثِّقالَ — It is He who shows you the lightning to instill fear and hope, and He produces the heavy clouds (13:12). Sahab (السحاب), with a fatha on the sin, is the collective noun for sahabah (سحابة); therefore, it was described with the plural "heavy" (الثقال).

"Showing" (الإراء) is the manifesting of that which is meant to be perceived by sight to the observer so that he may see it, or making man possess the attribute of seeing and perceiving. The contrast between His saying: يُرِيكُمُ — "He shows you"—and His saying: يُنْشِئُ — "He produces"—supports the first meaning.

His saying: خَوْفاً وَطَمَعاً — "fear and hope"—are objects of purpose (مفعول له), i.e., so that you may fear and hope. It is also possible that they are two verbal nouns (مصدرين) meaning the active participle, acting as circumstantial qualifiers (حالين) for the pronoun in "He shows you," i.e., [showing it to you while you are] fearing and hoping.

The meaning is: He is the one who manifests the lightning to your eyes so that the two attributes of fear and hope appear in you—just as the traveler fears it and the resident hopes for it, or the people of the sea fear it and the people of the land hope for it; one fears its thunderbolt (صاعقة) and hopes for its rain (غيث). And He creates by His production the clouds that are heavy with the waters they carry. In mentioning the sign of lightning with "showing" and the sign of clouds with "producing," there is a manifest subtlety.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيُرْسِلُ الصَّواعِقَ فَيُصِيبُ بِها مَنْ يَشاءُ — He sends the thunderbolts and strikes with them whomever He wishes (13:13), etc. Sawa'iq (الصواعق) is the plural of sa'iqah (صاعقة), which is the fiery piece descending from the sky due to lightning and thunder. Jadal (الجدل) is negotiation and contention in speech for the sake of overcoming [an opponent]; its root is from jadaltu al-habl when you have tightened its twist. Mihal (المحال), with a kasra on the mim, is the verbal noun of mahala (ماحله) when he outwitted him and vied with him in strength to see which of them is stronger, and contended with him to manifest his flaws and defects. Thus, His saying: وَهُمْ يُجادِلُونَ فِي اللهِ وَهو شَدِيدُ الْمِحالِ — while they dispute about Allah, and He is terrible in His stratagems (13:13), means—and Allah knows best—that the idolaters—to whom the speech is directed in presenting these arguments—dispute regarding His Lordship by fabricating arguments for the lordship of their [false] gods, such as clinging to the custom of their forefathers, while Allah, the Glorified, is severe in outwitting [them] because He is knowledgeable of their flaws and defects and powerful over manifesting them and their disgrace.

His saying, the Exalted: لَهُ دَعْوَةُ الْحَقِّ وَالَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ لا يَسْتَجِيبُونَ لَهُمْ بِشَيْءٍ — To Him belongs the true invocation. And those whom they invoke besides Him do not answer them in any way (13:14), until the end of the verse. Du'a (الدعاء) and da'wah (الدعوة) — invocation or calling — is the directing of the called one's attention to the caller; it is usually achieved through a word or a gesture. Istijabah (الاستجابة) and ijabah (الإجابة) — answering or responding — is the turning of the called one toward the caller in response to his call. As for the invocation including a request for a need and the response including its fulfillment, that is a goal that completes the meaning of invocation and response, but is not included in their [basic] concepts.

Indeed, an invocation is only truly an invocation if the one called possesses a "gaze" that can be directed to the caller, and possesses the means and power by which he can respond to the call. As for calling one who does not understand, or understands but does not possess that which would remove the need, it is not a "true invocation" (حق الدعاء), even if it takes its form.

Since the noble verse has established a contrast between His saying: لَهُ دَعْوَةُ الْحَقِ — "To Him belongs the true invocation"—and His saying: وَالَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ — "And those whom they invoke besides Him," etc., which mentions that the invocation of other than Him is devoid of response, and then describes the invocation of the disbelievers as being in "error" (ضلال), we know thereby that the intent of His saying: دَعْوَةُ الْحَقِ is the true invocation, not the false one. It is the invocation that the called one hears and then certainly responds to. This is among the attributes of the Exalted and Sanctified, for He is the Hearer of invocation, Near, and Responsive; He is the All-sufficient (الغني), the Possessor of Mercy. He said: أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ — I answer the solicitor’s invocation when he invokes Me (2:186), and ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ — Call Me, I will answer you (40:60). He spoke absolutely and did not stipulate for the response anything except that the reality of invocation be realized there and that the invocation be directed to the Exalted and none other.

Thus, the phrase "true invocation" (دعوة الحق) is an annexation (إضافة) of the described to the attribute, or a literal annexation with the consideration that Truth (الحق) and Falsehood (الباطل) seemingly divide invocation between them: one part belongs to Truth, which is that which never fails to be answered, and one part belongs to Falsehood, which is that which is not guided to the goal of response, like the invocation of one who does not hear or is not capable of responding.

Since He mentioned in the previous verses that He is knowledgeable of all things and that He possesses wondrous power, He mentioned in this verse that He possesses the reality of invocation and response. He is the Responder to invocation just as He is All-knowing and All-powerful. He mentioned this in the verse through the methods of affirmation and negation—namely, affirming the right of invocation for Himself and negating it for others.

As for the first, it is His saying: لَهُ دَعْوَةُ الْحَقِ, where the advancement of the prepositional phrase implies exclusivity (الحصر), supported by what follows it of negating it for others. As for the second, it is His saying: وَالَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ لا يَسْتَجِيبُونَ لَهُمْ بِشَيْءٍ إِلَّا كَباسِطِ كَفَّيْهِ إِلَى الْماءِ لِيَبْلُغَ فاهُ وَما هُوَ بِبالِغِهِ — And those whom they invoke besides Him do not answer them in any way, like someone who stretches out his hands to water [from a distance] that it may reach his mouth, though it will never reach it (13:14). He has informed therein that those whom the polytheists invoke besides Allah do not respond to them in any way. He has explained this in several places in His speech: these called ones are either idols (أصنام) invoked by their commoners—which are dead bodies possessing neither consciousness nor will—or the "lords" of the idols among the angels, the jinn, the spiritualities of the stars, or humans, as their elite might realize. These possess for themselves neither harm nor benefit, neither death nor life nor resurrection; how then could they for others? The entire kingdom belongs to Allah, and all power belongs to Allah; thus, there is no hope in any besides the Exalted.

Then He excepted from the generality of the negation of response only one case, which is that which resembles the subject of the parable struck by His saying: كَباسِطِ كَفَّيْهِ إِلَى الْماءِ لِيَبْلُغَ فاهُ وَما هُوَ بِبالِغِهِ.

For if a thirsty person wants to drink water, he must approach the water, then stretch out his hands, scoop it up, take it, and reach his mouth to quench his thirst. This is the "true seeking" (حق الطلب) that brings its possessor to his goal in guidance and rectitude. As for the thirsty one far from the water who wants to be quenched but does not take any of its means, except that he stretches out his hands toward it [from a distance] so that it may reach his mouth—it will never reach his mouth. He has nothing from his seeking except its mere form.

The likeness of one who invokes other than Allah, the Glorified, is like this one stretching out his hands to the water so that it may reach his mouth; he has nothing of the invocation except its form, devoid of meaning, and its name without a named reality. These called ones besides Allah do not respond to those who invoke them in any way, nor do they fulfill their need, except as a response is given to the one stretching out his hands to the water so that it may reach his mouth and fulfill his need—that is, they obtain nothing but the form of invocation, just as that stretcher obtains nothing but the form of seeking by stretching out the hands.

From here it is known that this exception—إِلَّا كَباسِطِ كَفَّيْهِ etc.—does not break the generality of the negation in the excepted-from (المستثنى منه); it only contains the form of an exception. It serves to strengthen the judgment in the side of the excepted-from, for its import is that those who invoke besides Allah are not responded to except as the one stretching out his hands to the water is responded to—and he will never be responded to. In other words: they will never attain through their invocation except that they attain nothing; i.e., they will attain nothing at all.

This is among the subtleties of His speech and corresponds from one aspect to His coming saying: قُلْ أَفَاتَّخَذْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ لا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ نَفْعاً وَلا ضَرًّا — Say, ‘Have you then taken guardians besides Him who possess no benefit nor harm for themselves?’ (13:16), and it is even more emphatic, as will come, if Allah wills.

From what has preceded, it has become clear:

First: That His saying: دَعْوَةُ الْحَقِ refers to the "right of invocation," which is that which is responded to and never rejected. As for the saying of some that it refers to the "word of sincerity" (كلمة الإخلاص)—the testimony that there is no god but Allah—there is no evidence for it from the context.

Second: That the implied meaning of His saying: وَالَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ etc., by manifesting the pronouns, is: those whom the polytheists invoke besides Allah, those called ones do not respond to the polytheists in any way.

Third: That the exception is from His saying: لا يَسْتَجِيبُونَ لَهُمْ بِشَيْءٍ, and there is omission (حذف) and brevity (إيجاز) in the speech. The meaning is: they do not respond to them in any way, nor do they grant them anything, except as a response is given to the one stretching out his hands to the water so that it may reach his mouth and he may attain [something] from his stretching. Perhaps "response" (الاستجابة) here includes the meaning of "attaining" (النيل) or the like.

Then the Glorified emphasized the discourse by His saying: وَما دُعاءُ الْكافِرِينَ إِلَّا فِي ضَلالٍ — And the invocation of the faithless is only in error (13:14). This also contains an indication of another fundamental reality: that there is no objective for invocation except Allah, the Glorified, for He is the All-knowing, the All-powerful, the All-sufficient, the Possessor of Mercy. There is no path for it except the path of turning toward the Exalted. Whoever invokes another and makes him the target of his invocation has lost the connection to the objective and the goal, and has thereby exited the path; thus his invocation has gone astray (ضلّ)—for "error" (الضلال) is exiting the path and treading that which does not lead to the sought-after goal.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ طَوْعاً وَكَرْهاً وَظِلالُهُمْ بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصالِ — To Allah prostrates whoever is in the heavens and the earth, willingly or unwillingly, as do their shadows at morning and evening (13:15). Prostration (السجود) is falling to the ground by placing the forehead or the chin upon it. The Exalted said: وَخَرُّوا لَهُ سُجَّداً — and they fell down prostrate before him (12:100), and يَخِرُّونَ لِلْأَذْقانِ سُجَّداً — they fall down on their faces in prostration (17:107). A single instance of it is a sajdah.

Karh (الكره) is an action performed by man with difficulty. If he is compelled to it from without, it is karh with a fatha on the kaf; if he is compelled to it from within himself, it is kurh with a damma. Taw' (الطوع) — willingness — is the opposite of karh absolutely.

Al-Raghib said: Ghudwah and gadat are from the beginning of the day. In the Quran, gudu (الغدو) is contrasted with asal (الآصال), as in His saying: بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصالِ, and gadat is contrasted with ashi (العشي), as He said: بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِ. End quote. Gudu is the plural of gadat, like qina and qanat. It is said in al-Majma': Asal is the plural of usul—with two dammas—and usul is the plural of asil (الأصيل); thus it is a plural of a plural, derived from asl (الأصل) — root/origin. It is as if it is the "root" of the night from which it arises, which is the time between the afternoon ('Asr) and sunset. End quote.

Social acts performed for spiritual purposes—such as "taking the lead" (التصدر) which represents leadership (الرئاسة), "advancing" (التقدم) which represents sovereignty (السيادة), bowing (الركوع) which manifests smallness and insignificance, and prostration (السجود) which manifests the ultimate humility (تذلل) and lowliness of the prostrator before the might and loftiness of the one prostrated to—their goals are called by their names just as the acts themselves are. Just as "advancing" is called advancement, so too is sovereignty called advancement. Just as a specific bending is "bowing," so too is a specific smallness and insignificance "bowing." Just as falling to the ground is "prostration," so too is humility "prostration." All of this is with the consideration that the goal of the act is what is truly sought, rather than the outward form of the act.

This perspective is what the Noble Quran considers in attributing prostration and its counterparts—such as devoutness (القنوت), glorification (التسبيح), praise (الحمد), and asking (السؤال)—to things, as in His saying: كُلٌّ لَهُ قانِتُونَ — all are obedient to Him (2:116), and وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ — There is not a thing but celebrates His praise (17:44), and يَسْئَلُهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him (55:29), and وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ — To Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth (16:49).

The difference between these matters when attributed to cosmic things and when they occur within the sphere of human society is that the goals exist in the first category in their true sense (حقيقة معناها), unlike the second category where they exist through a type of convention and mental construction (الوضع والاعتبار). For the lowliness of created beings and their placement before the threshold of Greatness and Majesty is a true lowliness and placement, unlike falling to the ground and placing the forehead upon it, which is lowliness and placement according to convention and construction, and therefore may sometimes fail to occur.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "To Allah prostrates whoever is in the heavens and the earth" (13:15) — takes up the preceding observation. Perhaps He specifically mentioned those endowed with intellect (أولي العقل) by saying: مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "whoever is in the heavens and the earth" — despite this abasement (الذلة) and lowliness (الضعة) encompassing all existent beings, as seen in the previously mentioned verse of al-Nahl and as suggested by the end of this verse: وَظِلالُهُمْ — "and their shadows" — because the discourse in this Surah is directed at the polytheists (المشركين) as an argument against them. Thus, it served as an exhortation for them to prostrate to Allah willingly (طوعا), just as the intelligent beings of the heavens and the earth besides them prostrate to Him willingly, to the extent that even their shadows prostrate to Him. For this reason, attention was also given to mentioning the prostration of shadows to be more emphatic in rousing them [to action]; so understand this.

Furthermore, this self-abasement (التذلل) and humility (التواضع), which all existent beings exhibit toward the presence of their Lord, Mighty and Majestic is He, is an essential submission (خضوع ذاتي) that is inseparable from them and never fails. Thus, it is absolutely "willing" (بالطوع). How could it be otherwise, when they possess nothing of themselves such that one might imagine them having any dislike (كراهة), refusal, or recalcitrance (جموح)? Indeed, the Exalted has said: فَقالَ لَها وَلِلْأَرْضِ ائْتِيا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْهاً قالَتا أَتَيْنا طائِعِينَ — "Then He said to it and to the earth, ‘Come! willingly or unwillingly.’ They said, ‘We come obediently’" (41:11).

The aforementioned consideration necessitates "willingness" (الطوع) for all existent beings in their prostration to Allah the Exalted, and completely cuts off any trace of "unwillingness" (الكره) from them. However, there is another consideration that may justify attributing "unwillingness" to some of them in a general sense. This is because some of these things exist in a realm of mutual interference (مجتمع التزاحم), equipped with natures (طباع) that might be hindered from reaching their goals and objectives by other causes. These are the things established in this world of ours—the world of matter (عالم المادة)—which may be crowded out in their aims and prevented from reaching the requirements of their natures by various obstacles. There is no doubt that what opposes one's nature is disliked (مكروه), just as what accords with it is sought after.

Thus, these things are prostrating to Allah, submissive to His command in all affairs pertaining to them. However, in matters that oppose their natures—such as death, decay, the cessation of effects, afflictions, defects, and the like—they are prostrating to Him "unwillingly" (كرها). In matters that accord with their natures—such as life, survival, reaching goals, and attaining perfection—they are prostrating to Him "willingly" (طوعا), like the noble angels who do not disobey Allah in what He commands them and do what they are commanded.

From what has preceded, the fallacy of the view held by some becomes apparent—that the intended meaning of "prostration" (السجدة) is the literal one, meaning falling to the ground by placing the forehead upon it, for example. According to them, all are prostrating, but the believer prostrates willingly while the disbeliever prostrates out of fear of the sword. This view has been attributed to al-Hasan.

Similarly, the view of some that prostration means submission (الخضوع), such that everything submits to Him, except that from the believer it is a willing submission and from the disbeliever it is an unwilling submission due to the pains and illnesses that befall him. This has been attributed to al-Jubba'i.

Likewise, the view of others: that the verse refers to the submission of everything in the heavens and the earth, both those with intellect and others, and the use of the term specific to those with intellect (مَنْ) is by way of predominance (التغليب).

As for His saying: وَظِلالُهُمْ بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصالِ — "and their shadows [as well], morning and evening" (13:15) — it involves joining the shadows of dense bodies with the bodies themselves in prostration. For although a shadow is a privative matter (عدميا) resulting from a dense body blocking the penetration of light, it nonetheless possesses external effects (آثارا خارجية). It increases and decreases at the two ends of the day and varies in a manner apparent to the senses. Thus, it possesses a mode of existence (نحو من الوجود) characterized by effects, and it submits in its existence and effects to Allah and prostrates to Him.

Shadows prostrate to Allah, the Glorified, a willing prostration at all times. The morning (الغدو) and evening (الآصال) were specifically mentioned not for the reason some have claimed—that they mean "perpetuity" (الدوام) because such terms are used for eternity—for if perpetual prostration were intended, it would have been more appropriate to say "at the ends of the day" (بأطراف النهار) to encompass everything before and after noon, as occurs in His saying: وَمِنْ آناءِ اللَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْ وَأَطْرافَ النَّهارِ لَعَلَّكَ تَرْضى — "and celebrate His praise during the hours of the night and at the ends of the day, that you may be pleased" (20:130).

Rather, the subtle point (النكتة) in this—and Allah knows best—is that the increase and decrease are constant for shadows in the morning and the evening, thereby representing to the senses the act of falling to the ground and the abasement of prostration. As for the time of noon and the middle of the day, shadows may vanish or diminish and appear stationary, such that the meaning of prostration is not as apparent.

There is no doubt that the falling of shadows upon the ground and their representation of the "falling down" (خرور) of prostration is intended in the attribution of prostration to shadows in their shifting (تفيؤها). The focus is not limited merely to their ontological obedience (طاعتها التكوينية) in all their states and effects. The evidence for this is His saying: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلى ما خَلَقَ اللهُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ يَتَفَيَّؤُا ظِلالُهُ عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَالشَّمائِلِ سُجَّداً لِلَّهِ وَهُمْ داخِرُونَ — "Have they not regarded the things Allah has created? Their shadows shift to the right and the left, prostrating to Allah in utter humility" (16:48). The attention given to this [visual aspect] is manifest therein.

This is not poetic speech (قولا شعريا) or an imaginary depiction (تصويرا تخييليا) used as a device for the True Call in His speech, the Exalted—far be it from Him—for He has explicitly stated that it is not poetry. Rather, these are realities (حقائق) transcendent above delusions, established before the sound intellect (العقل السليم), and by their nature distant from the senses. When they encounter instances where they can manifest to the senses in some form or be represented in some way, it is appropriate to utilize this to instruct simple understandings (الأفهام الساذجة) and basic intellects, moving them from the level of sense and imagination to the stage of the sound intellect that perceives the realities of gnosis (المعارف). For this belongs to the "True Sense and Imagination" (الحس والخيال الحق) which is backed by realities and supported by truth; thus, there is no harm in relying upon it.

It is from this category that He, the Exalted, counts the shifting shadows observed from upright bodies in the morning and evening as prostrating to Allah, the Glorified, because of their falling upon the ground, resembling the "falling down" (خرور) in prostration by those endowed with intellect.

Also from this category is what preceded in His saying: وَيُسَبِّحُ الرَّعْدُ بِحَمْدِهِ — "The thunder celebrates His praise" (13:13), where He applied "glorification" (التسبيح) to the terrifying sound of thunder, which represents a tongue articulating His transcendence (تنزيهه) above any resemblance to created beings and praising Him for His mercy, heralded by the wind, clouds, and lightning. This is despite the fact that all things without exception are glorifying Him with His praise through their existences which subsist by Him, the Exalted, and depend upon Him. This is an essential glorification (تسبيح ذاتي) from them, and its indication is an essential, intellectual indication (دلالة ذاتية عقلية) unrelated to the linguistic indications (الدلالات اللفظية) found in sounds by way of convention and social construct (الوضع والاعتبار). However, thunder, with its intense and terrifying sound, represents this essential glorification to the hearing and the imagination. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, mentioned it for the significance it holds, so that simple minds might transition through it to the meaning of the essential glorification that subsists in the essence of every thing without a striking sound or a conventional word.

Close to this category is what preceded at the opening of the Surah in His saying, the Exalted: رَفَعَ السَّماواتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها — "It is Allah who raised the heavens without any pillars that you see" (13:2) and His saying: وَفِي الْأَرْضِ قِطَعٌ مُتَجاوِراتٌ — "In the earth are neighboring terrains" (13:4). The reliance, in the context of argumentation, upon matters whose causes are unknown to the senses is not because His causality, the Exalted, is limited to this type of existent, or that matters with known causes are independent of Him. For the Noble Quran explicitly states the universality of the law of causality (قانون السببية) and that He, the Exalted, is above all. Rather, it is because matters whose causes do not appear to the senses at first glance alert simple understandings and represent the need for a cause in the best possible way. Thus, they are drawn to search for their causes, and the search inevitably ends at a First Cause, which is Allah, the Glorified. There is much of this in the Noble Quran.

In short, naming the falling of the shadows of things upon the ground in the morning and evening as "prostration" (سجودا) from them to Allah, the Glorified, is based on their representing, in this state, the meaning of the essential prostration (السجدة الذاتية) they possess in their essences through a sensory example (بمثال حسي) that alerts the senses to the meaning of essential prostration and eases the path for simple understanding to transition to that intellectual reality.

This is what reflection upon His speech, the Exalted, yields. As for reducing these meanings to mere poetic metaphor (استعارة شعرية) or making them a trope (مجازا), for instance, intended to mean the compliance of things to His command in the sense that they exist as He willed, or the claim that "shadow" refers to the person himself—since whoever prostrates, his shadow prostrates with him—these are weak interpretations that should not be entertained.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ مَنْ رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ قُلِ اللهُ قُلْ أَفَاتَّخَذْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ لا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ نَفْعاً وَلا ضَرًّا — "Say, ‘Who is the Lord of the heavens and the earth?’ Say, ‘Allah.’ Say, ‘Have you then taken besides Him guardians who have no control over their own benefit or harm?’" (13:16). This verse, including the command to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to argue against the polytheists, serves as a summary (فذلكة) of the preceding verses.

This is because the preceding verses explained with the utmost clarity that the regulation (تدبير) of the heavens and the earth and everything within them belongs to Allah, the Glorified, just as their creation (خلق) is from Him. He possesses the knowledge, power, and mercy required for creation and regulation, while everyone besides Him is a regulated creature who possesses neither benefit nor harm for himself. This results in the conclusion that He is the Lord (الرب) to the exclusion of others.

Thus, the Exalted commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to record against them the result of His previous explanation and to ask them—after reciting the preceding verses that uncover the face of truth to them—by saying: مَنْ رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Who is the Lord of the heavens and the earth?" i.e., who is it that owns the heavens and the earth and what is in them and regulates their affairs? Then He commanded him to answer the question himself and say: اللهُ — "Allah" — because they, being obstinate polytheists, would refuse to acknowledge the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية). In this, there is an allusion that they do not comprehend an argument nor understand a discourse.

Then, with the help of this result, He deduced a second result through which the falsehood of their polytheism becomes manifest with the clearest explanation. This is that the requirement of His Lordship, the Exalted, established by the previous arguments, is that He is the Owner of benefit and harm. Thus, everyone besides Him possesses neither benefit nor harm for himself, so how could they for another? Therefore, taking lords besides Allah—meaning assuming guardians (أولياء) besides Him who take charge of the affairs of the servants and possess benefit and harm for them—is, in reality, assuming guardians who are not guardians, because they do not possess benefit or harm for themselves, so how could they possess it for others?

This is the meaning of His saying, branching off from the previous question: قُلْ أَفَاتَّخَذْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ لا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ نَفْعاً وَلا ضَرًّا — "Say, ‘Have you then taken besides Him guardians who have no control over their own benefit or harm?’" i.e., how then could they possess it for others? Meaning: if Allah, the Glorified, is the Lord of the heavens and the earth, then by your taking guardians as gods besides Him, you have made a claim that belies itself; it is a non-existence in the very essence of their guardianship. This is an explicit contradiction: that they are "guardians who are not guardians" and "lords who have no lordship."

By reflecting on what we have presented—that the verse is like a summary of the previous explanations—the meaning of the verse returns to something like our saying: "If what preceded has become clear, then who is the Lord of the heavens and the earth but Allah? Have you then taken besides Him guardians who possess neither benefit nor harm?" The shift from direct branching to commanding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) by saying "Say such-and-such" and "Say such-and-such," repeating it time after time, is only to refrain from addressing them directly due to the filth of their ignorance and obstinacy. This is part of the subtle composition of the Quran.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمى وَالْبَصِيرُ أَمْ هَلْ تَسْتَوِي الظُّلُماتُ وَالنُّورُ — "Say, ‘Are the blind and the seeing equal? Or are darkness and light equal?’" (13:16). These are two parables struck by Allah, the Glorified, after the argument was completed and perfected against them. He commanded the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to strike them for them. Through the first, He explains the state of the believer and the disbeliever: the one who disbelieves in the true argument and the clear signs, refusing to submit to them, is "blind" (أعمى), while the one who believes in them is "seeing" (بصير). The rational person does not equate the two by the self-evidence of his intellect. Through the second, He explains that disbelief in the truth is "darkness" (ظلمات), just as the disbeliever fallen into it is not seeing; and faith in the truth is "light" (نور), just as the believer who holds to it is seeing. They are not equal at all. Thus, it is incumbent upon the polytheists—if they possess sound intellects as they claim—to submit to the truth, reject falsehood, and believe in Allah alone.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ جَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ خَلَقُوا كَخَلْقِهِ — "Have they set up for Allah partners who have created like His creation..." until His saying: وَهُوَ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "...and He is the One, the All-paramount" (13:16). In the expression جَعَلُوا — "they have set up" — and عَلَيْهِمْ — "to them" — rather than saying "you have set up" and "to you," there is evidence that the discourse is directed away from them toward the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) without him being commanded to deliver it to them.

Then, the return in the answer to this possibility—contained in His saying: أَمْ جَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ خَلَقُوا كَخَلْقِهِ فَتَشابَهَ الْخَلْقُ عَلَيْهِمْ — "Have they set up for Allah partners who have created like His creation, so that the creations seemed similar to them?" — to the command to deliver it to them by saying: قُلِ اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "Say, ‘Allah is the creator of all things, and He is the One, the All-paramount’" — is evidence that the question is only about the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and what is required from delivering the monotheism of the Creator (توحيد الخالق) to them is an initial delivery, not a delivery by way of an answer. This is only because they do not claim a creator besides Allah, the Glorified, as He, the Exalted, said: وَلئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ — "If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah’" (31:25, 39:38), and He, the Exalted, has repeatedly quoted this from them.

These idolaters did not see any partner for Allah, the Glorified, in creation and origination (الخلق والإيجاد). They only disputed with Islam regarding the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية), not the monotheism of Divinity (توحيد الألوهية) in the sense of creation and origination. Their admission of the monotheism of the Originating Creator and limiting that to Allah invalidates their claim of partners in Lordship, and the argument is completed against them. This is because the exclusivity of creation and origination to Allah, the Glorified, negates independent existence, knowledge, and power from anyone besides Him, the Exalted, and there is no Lordship with the negation of these perfect attributes.

Therefore, there remained for them no way to claim the Lordship of their partners alongside Allah, the Glorified, except to deny His uniqueness, the Exalted, in creation and origination, and to establish [creative power] for their gods after creation and origination—which they do not do. This is what necessitated His, the Exalted, mentioning this possibility to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) without addressing them with it or commanding him to address them.

It is as if He, the Exalted, by saying: أَمْ جَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ خَلَقُوا كَخَلْقِهِ فَتَشابَهَ الْخَلْقُ عَلَيْهِمْ — "Have they set up for Allah partners who have created like His creation, so that the creations seemed similar to them?" — is saying to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "The argument has been completed against these people regarding the monotheism of Lordship from the perspective of His exclusivity, the Exalted, in creation and origination. Thus, nothing remains for them except to claim the partnership of their partners in creation and origination. So, are they saying that their partners created a creation like His creation, such that the creations seemed similar to them, and they consequently claimed their Lordship generally alongside Allah?"

Then He commanded the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to deliver to them that which cuts off the root of this possibility, saying: قُلِ اللهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ وَهُوَ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "Say, ‘Allah is the creator of all things, and He is the One, the All-paramount’." The beginning of the sentence is a claim whose proof is its end; i.e., He, the Exalted, is One in His creativity, having no partner therein. How could He have a partner therein when He possesses a Oneness (وحدة) that subdues (تقهر) every number and multiplicity? We have already presented some discussion on the meaning of Him, the Exalted, being "the One, the All-paramount" (الواحد القهار) in the commentary on His saying: أَأَرْبابٌ مُتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ — "Are discrete lords better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount?" (12:39). It was explained there that the combination of these two attributes yields the attribute of Uniqueness (الأحدية).

From what we have mentioned, the reason for the change in style in His saying: أَمْ جَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ خَلَقُوا كَخَلْقِهِ فَتَشابَهَ الْخَلْقُ عَلَيْهِمْ — "Have they set up for Allah partners who have created like His creation, so that the creations seemed similar to them?" — and the turning away from the previous style of address has become clear. Reflect on this, and know that most commentators were confused regarding the arguments established by the Quranic verses to prove His Lordship, the Exalted, and His Oneness therein, and to negate partners from Him; they mixed them with the arguments established to prove the existence of the Maker (الصانع). So take note of this.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abd al-Rahim al-Qasir, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ — "You are only a warner, and there is a guide for every people" (13:7). He said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "أنا المنذر وعلي الهادي" — "I am the warner and Ali is the guide..." [the hadith].

I say: This meaning was narrated by al-Kulayni in al-Kafi, al-Saduq in al-Ma'ani, al-Saffar in al-Basa'ir, al-Ayyashi and al-Qummi in their respective tafsirs, and others through many different chains of narration.

The meaning of his saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "I am the warner and Ali is the guide" is: "I am the instance (مصداق) of the warner—and warning (الإنذار) is guidance (هداية) accompanied by a call (دعوة)—and Ali is the instance of the guide without a call, and he is the Imam." It does not mean that the "warner" is exclusively the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the "guide" is exclusively Ali (peace be upon him), for that would completely contradict the apparent meaning of the verse.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated by Ibn Jarir, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu'aym in al-Ma'rifah, al-Daylami, Ibn Asakir, and Ibn al-Najjar, who said: When إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ — "You are only a warner, and there is a guide for every people" — was revealed, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) placed his hand on his chest and said: "أنا المنذر" — "I am the warner," and he gestured with his hand to the shoulder of Ali and said: "أنت الهادي ـ يا علي بك يهتدي المهتدون من بعدي" — "You are the guide—O Ali, through you the guided shall be guided after me."

I say: It was also narrated by al-Tha'labi in al-Kashf from Ata ibn al-Sa'ib, from Sa'id ibn Jubayr, from Ibn Abbas, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In Mustadrak al-Hakim, with its chain of narration from Ibrahim ibn al-Hakam ibn Zahir, from his father, from al-Hakam ibn Jarir, from Abu Buraydah al-Aslami, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) called for water for purification while Ali ibn Abi Talib was with him. After the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had purified himself, he took Ali's hand and pressed it to his [the Prophet's] chest, then said: "إنما أنت منذر" — "You are only a warner," meaning himself. Then he moved it to Ali's chest and said: "ولكل قوم هاد" — "and there is a guide for every people." Then he said to him: "أنت منار الأنام وغاية الهدى وأمير القراء ـ أشهد على ذلك أنك كذلك" — "You are the lighthouse of mankind, the ultimate goal of guidance, and the leader of the reciters—I bear witness to that, that you are indeed so."

I say: It was also narrated by Ibn Shahrashub from al-Hakim in Shawahid al-Tanzil, and al-Marzubani in Ma Nazala min al-Quran fi Amir al-Mu'minin.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, narrated by Abdullah ibn Ahmad in Zawa'id al-Musnad, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani in al-Awsat, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn Asakir from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ — "You are only a warner, and there is a guide for every people." He said: "رسول الله المنذر وأنا الهادي" — "The Messenger of Allah is the warner and I am the guide." In another wording: "والهادي رجل من بني هاشم" — "And the guide is a man from Banu Hashim," meaning himself.

I say: There are many other narrations from Sunni sources regarding this meaning.

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ — "You are only a warner, and there is a guide for every people." He said: "كل إمام هاد لكل قوم في زمانهم" — "Every Imam is a guide for every people in their time."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Fudayl, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ — "and there is a guide for every people." He said: "كل إمام هاد للقرن الذي هو فيهم" — "Every Imam is a guide for the generation in which he resides."

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir, who said: I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرٌ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ — "You are only a warner, and there is a guide for every people." He said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "أنا المنذر وعلي الهادي" — "I am the warner and Ali is the guide." [Then he said:] "يا با محمد هل من هاد اليوم؟" — "O Abu Muhammad, is there a guide today?" I said: "May I be your ransom, there has never ceased to be a guide from among you after a guide, until it reached you." He said: "رحمك الله يا با محمد لو كانت إذا نزلت آية على رجل ـ ثم مات ذلك الرجل ماتت الآية مات الكتاب ـ ولكنه يجري فيمن بقي كما جرى فيما مضى" — "May Allah have mercy on you, O Abu Muhammad. If it were that when a verse was revealed concerning a man, and then that man died, the verse died and the Book died—but rather, it flows (يجري) for those who remain just as it flowed for those who passed."

I say: The narration testifies to what we previously stated—that the inclusion of Ali (peace be upon him) in the verse is by way of "flow" (الجري), and it similarly flows for the rest of the Imams. This "flow" is what is intended by the reports stating that it was revealed concerning Ali (peace be upon him).

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: اللهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تَحْمِلُ كُلُّ أُنْثى وَما تَغِيضُ الْأَرْحامُ — "Allah knows what every female carries, and what the wombs stint" (13:8). He said: "ما لم يكن حملا" — "That which is not a fetus." [And regarding] وَما تَزْدادُ — "and what they add" — he said: "الذكر والأنثى جميعا" — "The male and female together."

I say: His saying "the male and female together" means what exceeds one [fetus] from a single [conception], as evidenced by the following narration.

In the same source, from Muhammad ibn Muslim and others, from both of them (peace be upon them), they said: ما تَحْمِلُ — "what [every female] carries" — refers to every female or male; وَما تَغِيضُ الْأَرْحامُ — "and what the wombs stint" — he said: "ما لم يكن حملا" — "that which is not a fetus"; وَما تَزْدادُ — "and what they add" — from female or male.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Hariz, from one whom he mentioned, from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them) regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: اللهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تَحْمِلُ كُلُّ أُنْثى وَما تَغِيضُ الْأَرْحامُ وَما تَزْدادُ — "Allah knows what every female carries, and what the wombs stint and what they add." He said: "الغيض كل حمل دون تسعة أشهر" — "The stinting (الغيض) is every pregnancy less than nine months," and وَما تَزْدادُ — "and what they add" — is everything that increases beyond nine months. For whenever a woman sees pure blood during her pregnancy from menstruation, she adds [to the term] the number of days she saw blood during her pregnancy.

I say: This is another meaning, and it has been reported from some of the early commentators.

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of narration from Tha'labah ibn Maymun, from some of our companions, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهادَةِ — "the Knower of the Unseen and the witnessed" (13:9). He said: "الغيب ما لم يكن والشهادة ما قد كان" — "The Unseen (الغيب) is what has not yet been, and the witnessed (الشهادة) is what has already been."

I say: What is meant by "what has not yet been" is not the non-existent (المعدوم) that is nothing, but rather the matter that is in potentiality (بالقوة) as long as it has not entered the realm of actuality (الفعلية). What he (peace be upon him) mentioned is one of the instances (المصاديق), which is apparent.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, narrated by Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani in al-Kabir, Ibn Mardawayh, and Abu Nu'aym in al-Dala'il, through the path of Ata ibn Yasar, from Ibn Abbas: That Arbad ibn Qays and Amir ibn al-Tufayl came to Madinah to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). They reached him while he was sitting and sat before him. Amir said: "What will you give me if I submit?" The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "لك ما للمسلمين وعليك ما عليهم" — "You shall have what the Muslims have and be bound by what they are bound by." He said: "Will you give me the authority after you if I submit?" He said: "ليس لك ولا لقومك ولكن لك أعنة الخيل" — "It is not for you nor for your people, but you shall have the reins of the cavalry." He said: "Then give me the desert (الوبر) and you take the settled land (المدر)." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "لا" — "No." When he turned to leave, he said: "I will surely fill it against you with horses and men." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "يمنعك الله" — "Allah will prevent you."

When Arbad and Amir left, Amir said: "O Arbad, I will distract Muhammad from you with talk, so strike him with the sword. For if you kill Muhammad, the people will do no more than be satisfied with the blood money (الدية) and will dislike war, so we will give them the blood money." Arbad said: "I will do so." They returned, and Amir said: "O Muhammad, stand with me so I may speak to you." He stood with him, and they went aside to a wall. Amir stood talking to him while Arbad drew his sword. When he placed his hand on his sword, it froze upon the hilt, and he could not draw it. Arbad delayed in striking, so the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) turned and saw Arbad and what he was doing, and he walked away from them. Amir said to Arbad: "What is wrong with you, you coward?" He said: "I placed my hand on the hilt of the sword and it froze."

When Amir and Arbad left the presence of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and reached Harrah Raqam, they stopped. Sa'd ibn Mu'adh and Usayd ibn Hudayr came out to them and said: "Be gone, O enemies of Allah! May Allah curse you both," and they rebuked them. Amir said: "Who is this, O Sa'd?" Sa'd said: "This is Usayd ibn Hudayr al-Kata'ib." He said: "By Allah, Hudayr was a friend of mine."

When he was at al-Raqam, Allah sent a thunderbolt upon Arbad and killed him. Amir went on until he was at al-Khurayb, where Allah sent a plague-sore (قرحة) upon him, and death overtook him there. Then Allah revealed: اللهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تَحْمِلُ كُلُّ أُنْثى — "Allah knows what every female carries..." until His saying: لَهُ مُعَقِّباتٌ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ — "He has guardian angels (المعقبات) before him..." (13:8-11). He said: The guardian angels (المعقبات) are by the command of Allah, protecting Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Then He mentioned Arbad and what killed him, saying: هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمُ الْبَرْقَ — "It is He who shows you the lightning..." until His saying: وَهُوَ شَدِيدُ الْمِحالِ — "...and He is terrible in retributive might" (13:12-13).

I say: Something similar in meaning was narrated from al-Tabari and Abu al-Shaykh from Ibn Zayd, and at its end: Labid said concerning his brother Arbad, weeping for him:

I fear the fates for Arbad, yet I do not Dread the stars of the sky nor the lions. The thunder and thunderbolts have bereaved me of the knight On the day of the fierce, valiant battle.

What the narration mentions regarding the revelation of these verses in this story does not align with the context of the Surah's verses, which clearly indicate it is Meccan. Furthermore, it does not suit the context of the verses themselves based on the meaning we have already established.

In al-Durr al-Manthur as well, narrated by Ibn al-Mundhir and Abu al-Shaykh from Ali: لَهُ مُعَقِّباتٌ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ يَحْفَظُونَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ — "He has guardian angels before him and behind him, who guard him by Allah’s command" (13:11). He said: "ليس من عبد إلا ومعه ملائكة ـ يحفظونه من أن يقع عليه حائط أو يتردى في بئر ـ أو يأكله سبع أو غرق أو حرق ـ فإذا جاء القدر خلوا بينه وبين القدر" — "There is no servant except that there are angels with him, guarding him from a wall falling on him, or falling into a well, or being eaten by a predator, or drowning, or burning. But when the decree (القدر) comes, they leave him to the decree."

I say: Something similar in meaning was also narrated from Abu Dawud in al-Qadar, Ibn Abi al-Dunya, and Ibn Asakir from him. Something similar was also narrated from the two Sadiqs (peace be upon them).

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Fudayl ibn Uthman, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): He said: He narrated to us this verse: لَهُ مُعَقِّباتٌ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ — "He has guardian angels before him and behind him." He said: "من المقدمات المؤخرات ـ المعقبات الباقيات الصالحات" — "From the preceding and succeeding ones—the 'successive ones' (المعقبات) are the 'enduring righteous deeds' (الباقيات الصالحات)."

I say: Its apparent meaning is that the enduring righteous deeds (الباقيات الصالحات) are among the instances of the "successive ones" (المعقبات) mentioned in the verse, which protect their performer from an evil decree. They only protect through the angels appointed over them, so its meaning returns to what we presented in the explanation of the verse. It is also possible that the "preceding and succeeding ones" are the enduring righteous deeds themselves, and its return to what we presented is apparent.

In the same source, from Abu Amr al-Mada'ini, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: My father used to say: "إن الله قضى قضاء حتما ـ لا ينعم على عبد بنعمة ـ فسلبها إياه قبل أن يحدث العبد ما ـ يستوجب بذلك الذنب سلب تلك النعمة ـ وذلك قول الله : إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ ـ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ" — "Indeed, Allah has decreed a definitive decree: He does not bestow a blessing upon a servant and then strip it from him before the servant commits that which necessitates the stripping of that blessing due to that sin. That is the saying of Allah: 'Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot, unless they change what is in their souls' (13:11)."

In the same source, from Ahmad ibn Muhammad, from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ ـ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ وَإِذا أَرادَ اللهُ بِقَوْمٍ سُوْءاً ـ فَلا مَرَدَّ لَهُ — "Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot, unless they change what is in their souls. And when Allah wishes to visit ill on a people, there is nothing that can avert it" (13:11). He said: "فصار الأمر إلى الله تعالى" — "Thus the matter rests with Allah the Exalted."

I say: This is an allusion to the meaning of the verse we previously presented.
