In al-Ma'ani, with its chain of narration from Abdullah ibn al-Fadl, from his father, who said: I heard Abu Khalid al-Kabuli say: I heard Zayn al-Abidin Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) say: "الذنوب التي تغير النعم ـ البغي على الناس والزوال عن العادة في الخير ـ واصطناع المعروف وكفران النعم وترك الشكر ، قال الله عز وجل : إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ ـ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ" — "The sins that change blessings are: transgressing against people, departing from the habit of doing good and performing kindness, ingratitude for blessings, and abandoning thanks. Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: 'Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot, unless they change what is in their souls' (13:11)."

In the same source, with its chain of narration from al-Hasan ibn Faddal, from al-Rida (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي يُرِيكُمُ الْبَرْقَ خَوْفاً وَطَمَعاً — "It is He who shows you the lightning, sealing fear and hope" (13:12). He said: "خوفا للمسافر وطمعا للمقيم" — "Fear for the traveler and hope for the resident."

In the Tafsir of al-Nu'mani, from al-Asbagh ibn Nubatah, from Ali (peace be upon him) regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ شَدِيدُ الْمِحالِ — "and He is terrible in retributive might" (13:13). He said: "يريد المكر" — "He means 'the stratagem' (المكر)."

In the Amali of the Shaykh, with its chain of narration from Anas ibn Malik: That the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) sent a man to one of the tyrants (فراعنة) of the Arabs to call him to Allah, Mighty and Majestic. He said to the messenger: "Tell me about this one to whom you call me—is He of silver, or of gold, or of iron?" The messenger returned to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and informed him of his words. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "ارجع إليه فادعه" — "Return to him and call him." He said: "O Prophet of Allah, he was more obstinate than that." He said: "ارجع إليه" — "Return to him." He returned and the man said the same as before. While he was speaking to him, a cloud thundered a single clap and cast a thunderbolt upon his head, taking away the crown of his head. Then Allah, Great is His Praise, revealed: وَيُرْسِلُ الصَّواعِقَ فَيُصِيبُ بِها مَنْ يَشاءُ ـ وَهُمْ يُجادِلُونَ فِي اللهِ وَهُوَ شَدِيدُ الْمِحالِ — "He unleashes the thunderbolts and strikes with them whomever He wishes, while they dispute concerning Allah, and He is terrible in retributive might" (13:13).

I say: The discussion regarding its end is like the discussion regarding the end of what preceded in the story of Amir and Arbad. This report adds that His saying: وَيُرْسِلُ الصَّواعِقَ — "He unleashes the thunderbolts..." etc., is part of a verse, and there is no reason for the verses to be fragmented in their revelation.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, he said: In a narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هادٍ — "and there is a guide for every people" (13:7): "As for those who prostrate from the inhabitants of the heavens willingly, they are the angels—they prostrate to Allah willingly. As for those who prostrate from the inhabitants of the earth from among those born into Islam, they prostrate to Him willingly. As for those who prostrate to Him unwillingly, they are those who were forced into Islam. And as for those who do not prostrate, their shadows prostrate to Him in the morning and evening."

I say: The apparent meaning of the narration contradicts the context of the noble verse. For the verse is intended to explain the universality of His subdual (قهره), the Exalted, through His greatness and loftiness, of whoever is in the heavens and the earth—themselves and their shadows—and it informs of their prostration to Him, the Exalted, with the reality of prostration (حقيقة السجدة). The apparent meaning of the narration is that prostration means "falling down" (الخرور) and placing the forehead, or what resembles prostration, which is universally present either in them or in their shadows. If they prostrate literally, whether willingly or unwillingly, then so be it; otherwise, the falling of their shadows upon the ground resembles prostration. This is a meaning that does not reflect the majesty of Allah, the Great, the All-Exalted.

Furthermore, it does not agree with the universality apparent in His saying: وَظِلالُهُمْ بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصالِ — "and their shadows [as well], morning and evening" — and even clearer than that is the universality in His saying: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلى ما خَلَقَ اللهُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ يَتَفَيَّؤُا ظِلالُهُ عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَالشَّمائِلِ سُجَّداً لِلَّهِ وَهُمْ داخِرُونَ وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ دابَّةٍ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ وَهُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ — "Have they not regarded the things Allah has created? Their shadows shift to the right and the left, prostrating to Allah in utter humility. To Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth of animals and angels, and they are not arrogant" (16:48-49).

Ayahs

أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَسَالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌۢ بِقَدَرِهَا فَٱحْتَمَلَ ٱلسَّيْلُ زَبَدًا رَّابِيًا ۚ وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِى ٱلنَّارِ ٱبْتِغَآءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتَـٰعٍ زَبَدٌ مِّثْلُهُۥ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحَقَّ وَٱلْبَـٰطِلَ ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفَآءً ۖ وَأَمَّا مَا يَنفَعُ ٱلنَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَالَ (١٧)
He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the extent of] their capacity, and the flood carries along a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire for the purpose of [making] ornaments or wares, [there arises] a similar scum. That is how Allah compares truth and falsehood. As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and that which profits the people stays in the earth. That is how Allah draws comparisons. (17)
لِلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لِرَبِّهِمُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَهُۥ لَوْ أَنَّ لَهُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا وَمِثْلَهُۥ مَعَهُۥ لَٱفْتَدَوْا۟ بِهِۦٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلْحِسَابِ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ (١٨)
For those who answer [the summons of] their Lord there shall be the best [of rewards]. But those who do not answer Him, even if they possessed all that is on the earth and as much of it besides, they would surely offer it to redeem themselves with it. For such there shall be an adverse reckoning, and their refuge shall be hell, and it is an evil resting place. (18)
۞ أَفَمَن يَعْلَمُ أَنَّمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ٱلْحَقُّ كَمَنْ هُوَ أَعْمَىٰٓ ۚ إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (١٩)
Is someone who knows that what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth, like someone who is blind? Only those who possess intellect take admonition (19)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَنقُضُونَ ٱلْمِيثَـٰقَ (٢٠)
—those who fulfill Allah’s covenant and do not break the pledge solemnly made, (20)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ مَآ أَمَرَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦٓ أَن يُوصَلَ وَيَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ وَيَخَافُونَ سُوٓءَ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٢١)
and those who join what Allah has commanded to be joined, and fear their Lord, and are afraid of an adverse reckoning (21)
وَٱلَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا۟ ٱبْتِغَآءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَقَامُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأَنفَقُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقْنَـٰهُمْ سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً وَيَدْرَءُونَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ ٱلسَّيِّئَةَ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عُقْبَى ٱلدَّارِ (٢٢)
—those who are patient for the sake of their Lord’s pleasure, maintain the prayer, and spend secretly and openly out of what We have provided them, and repel evil [conduct] with good. For such will be the reward of the [ultimate] abode: (22)
جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَهَا وَمَن صَلَحَ مِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَأَزْوَٰجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ ۖ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَدْخُلُونَ عَلَيْهِم مِّن كُلِّ بَابٍ (٢٣)
the Gardens of Eden, which they will enter along with whoever is righteous from among their forebears, their spouses, and their descendants, and the angels will call on them from every door: (23)
سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُم بِمَا صَبَرْتُمْ ۚ فَنِعْمَ عُقْبَى ٱلدَّارِ (٢٤)
‘Peace be to you, for your patience.’ How excellent is the reward of the [ultimate] abode! (24)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مِيثَـٰقِهِۦ وَيَقْطَعُونَ مَآ أَمَرَ ٱللَّهُ بِهِۦٓ أَن يُوصَلَ وَيُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمُ ٱللَّعْنَةُ وَلَهُمْ سُوٓءُ ٱلدَّارِ (٢٥)
But as for those who break Allah’s compact after having pledged it solemnly, and sever what Allah has commanded to be joined, and cause corruption in the earth—it is such on whom the curse will lie, and for them will be the ills of the [ultimate] abode. (25)
ٱللَّهُ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ وَفَرِحُوا۟ بِٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِلَّا مَتَـٰعٌ (٢٦)
Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it. They boast of the life of this world, but compared with the Hereafter the life of this world is but a [trifling] enjoyment. (26)

(Commentary)

When He completed the proof against the polytheists (المشركين) in the conclusion of the preceding verses, and then clarified for them the manifest difference between truth (الحق) and falsehood (الباطل), and the distinction between those who adopt the former or engage with the latter—by His saying: قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الْأَعْمَىٰ وَالْبَصِيرُ أَمْ هَلْ تَسْتَوِي الظُّلُمَاتُ وَالنُّورُ "Say, ‘Are the blind and the seeing equal? Or are darkness and light equal?’" (13:16)—He began a detailed exposition of the difference between the two paths: the path of truth, which is faith in Allah and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), and the path of falsehood, which is polytheism (الشرك) and evil deeds. He describes their respective adherents—the believers (المؤمنون) and the polytheists—noting that for the former is peace and the ultimate abode, while for the latter is the curse and the evil abode. Allah outspreads provision for whomever He wishes and straitens it. He, the Glorified, began this entire discourse with a parable (مثل) through which He clarifies the state of truth and falsehood and the specific effect of each, then built the discourse upon that in describing the state of the two paths and the two groups.

His saying, the Exalted: أَنزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً "He sends down water from the sky..." (13:17) until the end of the verse. It is stated in Majma‘ al-Bayan: "The valley (الوادي) is the foot of a great, low-lying mountain where rainwater collects. From it is derived the term for blood-money (الدية), because it is a collection of a great amount of wealth paid on behalf of the slain. Measure (القدر) is the conjunction of one thing with another without increase or decrease; whereas weight (الوزن) may increase or decrease, if it is equal, it is measure (القدر). Al-Hasan read bi-qadriha with a sukun on the dal; these are two linguistic variants: one says qadru shibr and qadaru shibr (the measure of a span), though the verbal noun (المصدر) is only with the lightened form (sukun)."

He said: "Carrying (الاحتمال) is lifting something onto the back by the strength of the carrier. It is said: 'His voice rose above so-and-so, so he bore it (احتمله) and did not become angry.' Scum (الزبد) is the dross of boiling, which is the impurity of boiling; from this comes the scum of the pot and the scum of the torrent (زبد السيل)."

"Refuse (الجفاء) is an elongated noun like ghutha’ (driftwood/scum), and its root ends in a hamzah. It is said: 'The valley cast out refuse (جفأ الوادي جفاء).' Abu Zayd said: 'I cast down (جفأت) the man when I wrestled him to the ground,' and 'I cleared the pot of its scum (أجفأت القدر بزبدها) when I threw its scum away from it.' Al-Farra’ said: 'Everything that gathers together is formed on the pattern of fu‘al, such as hutam (debris), qumash (rubbish), ghutha’ (scum), and jafa’ (refuse).'"

"Kindling (الإيقاد) is throwing wood into the fire; the fire was kindled (استوقدت), caught fire (اتقدت), and blazed (توقدت). Provision (المتاع) is that which you enjoy. Staying (المكث) is remaining in a place over the passage of time; it is said makatha and makutha—with the fathah or dammah on the kaf—and tamakkatha, meaning he tarried." End of quote.

Al-Raghib said: "Falsehood (الباطل) is the opposite of truth (الحق); it is that which has no stability (ثبات) when examined. The Exalted said: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ وَأَنَّ مَا يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ الْبَاطِلُ 'That is because Allah is the Real, and what they invoke besides Him is falsehood' (22:62). This may be applied by consideration to speech and actions; it is said batala batulan and batlan and butlanan (it became null), and someone else nullified it (abtalahu). The Mighty and Majestic said: وَبَطَلَ مَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ 'and what they used to do proved to be in vain' (7:118), and He said: لِمَ تَلْبِسُونَ الْحَقَّ بِالْبَاطِلِ 'Why do you confound the truth with falsehood?' (3:71)." End of the relevant portion.

Thus, the falsehood (بطلان) of a thing is when a type of existence (الوجود) is assumed for it, but when applied to external reality (الخارج), it does not remain as assumed and reality does not correspond to it. Truth (الحق) is the opposite. Truth and falsehood are attributed first to belief (الاعتقاد) and then to other things by way of a certain consideration.

A statement such as "the sky is above us and the earth is beneath us" is true (حق) because reality corresponds to it when examined and applied. Our saying "the sky is beneath us and the earth is above us" is false (باطل) because of its lack of stability in reality according to the stability assumed for it. An action is true when it occurs according to the end (الغاية) or command (الأمر) intended for it—such as eating for satiety, striving for provision, or drinking medicine for health—if it produces its effect and reaches its purpose. It is false if it does not occur according to the intended end or command. An existing thing in external reality is true (حق) insofar as it exists as believed, such as the existence of the Truth (الحق), the Exalted. A non-existent thing for which existence is believed is false (باطل). Similarly, if it exists but is assumed to have properties of existence that it does not possess—such as assuming independence (الاستقلال) and permanence (البقاء) for a contingent being (الموجود الممكن)—then the contingent being is false from the perspective of the lack of independence or permanence assumed for it, even if it is true from the perspective of its essential existence. As the poet said:

Behold, everything besides Allah is false (باطل) And every delight must inevitably vanish.

This noble verse is among the most brilliant of Quranic verses; it discusses the nature of truth and falsehood, describing the beginning of their formation, the manner of their appearance, the specific effects of each, and the established way (سنة) of Allah, the Glorified, that runs through them—and you will never find any change in the way of Allah, nor will you find any alteration in the way of Allah.

The Exalted clarified this through a parable He struck for the people. It is not two parables as some have said, nor three as others mentioned—as we shall point out, Allah willing. Rather, it is a single parable that decomposes into multiple examples. The Exalted said: أَنزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً فَسَالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِهَا فَاحْتَمَلَ السَّيْلُ زَبَدًا رَّابِيًا "He sends down water from the sky, and the valleys flow according to their capacity, and the flood carries a swelling scum" (13:17). His saying "He sends down" (أَنزَلَ) is a verb whose subject is Allah, the Glorified; it is not mentioned due to its clarity. The indefiniteness of "water" (مَاءً) indicates the type, which is pure, clear water—meaning the water itself without being mixed with anything or tainted by change. The indefiniteness of "valleys" (أَوْدِيَةٌ) indicates their variation in size, length, and capacity. Attributing "flowing" to the valleys is a metaphorical attribution, similar to our saying "the gutter ran." Describing the scum as "swelling" (رَّابِيًا) is because it floats and always rises above the flood. All of this is indicated by the context. He struck the parable with the flood because the carrying of swelling scum is most apparent in it.

The meaning is: Allah, the Glorified, sent down water from the sky—which is the direction of height—via rain. Consequently, the valleys located in the area of the rainfall, varying in width, narrowness, and size, flowed "according to their capacity" (بِقَدَرِهَا)—meaning each according to its specific measure: the large according to its measure and the small according to its measure. Thus, the flood occurring in each of these different valleys carried a floating, high scum that is apparent to the senses and covers the water.

Then the Exalted said: وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِي النَّارِ ابْتِغَاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتَاعٍ زَبَدٌ مِّثْلُهُ "and from what they smelt in the fire to make ornaments or housewares, there is a scum like it" (13:17). The word "from" (مِن) is partitive. That which they kindle fire upon are types of metals (الفلزات) and earthly materials capable of being melted, from which ornaments and the provisions of life are fashioned. The meaning is: there emerges from the metals and earthly materials upon which they kindle fire in search of adornment—like gold and silver—or in search of provision—like iron and others from which tools and implements are made—a scum like the scum that swells upon the flood, floating upon the molten material and rising above it.

Then the Exalted said: كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْبَاطِلَ "Thus does Allah draw [the comparison between] the true and the false" (13:17). That is, Allah establishes the truth and the falsehood in a manner similar to what He did with the flood and its scum, and what they kindle fire upon and its scum.

The intended meaning of "striking" (الضرب)—and Allah knows best—is a type of establishment (التثبيت), similar to our saying: "I struck the tent," meaning I pitched it; and His saying: "Abasement and destitution were struck (ضُرِبَتْ) upon them," meaning they were cast and established; and "a wall was struck (ضُرِبَ) between them," meaning it was brought into being and built; and "strike (اضْرِبْ) for them a path in the sea," meaning open and establish one. To this meaning also returns the "striking of a parable" (ضرب المثل), because it is the establishment and setting up of that which resembles the thing exemplified so that its state becomes clear. All of these are, in reality, instances of mentioning the concomitant (الملزوم) while intending the consequence (اللازم). For "striking"—which is the forceful and violent impact of one thing upon another—is usually inseparable from the establishment of a matter in that which received the strike, such as the fixing of a peg in the ground by the strike of a hammer, or the occurrence of pain in the body of an animal by striking it. Thus, "striking" (the concomitant) is mentioned, and "establishment" (the consequence) is intended.

From this, it becomes clear that the view of the commentators—that there is an omission (حذف) or metaphor (مجاز) in the sentence, and the implied meaning is "Thus does Allah strike the parable of truth and falsehood"—is misplaced. It is an unnecessary affectation for which there is no evidence.

Furthermore, had that been intended, its appropriate place would be at the end of the discourse, where His saying occurs: كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثَالَ "Thus does Allah draw the parables" (13:17), which would render the earlier one redundant.

Moreover, the meaning they mentioned ultimately returns to what we have stated. For the fact that the account of the flood and scum, or the smelting and scum, is a parable for truth and falsehood necessitates that the establishment of truth be like the establishment of the flood and the smelted material, and the establishment of falsehood be like the establishment of the scum. Thus, there is no need for an implied omission when the meaning is sound without it.

Then the Exalted said: فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفَاءً ۖ وَأَمَّا مَا يَنفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ "As for the scum, it leaves as refuse; but as for what profits mankind, it stays in the earth" (13:17). He combined the two types of scum—the scum of the flood and the scum of the smelted material—after they had been mentioned separately, because they share the property being mentioned: that it goes away as refuse. For this reason, we stated earlier that the verse contains a single parable, even if it decomposes into more than one example.

He turned from mentioning "water" and other things to saying: "but as for what profits mankind," to indicate a specific property of the truth: that people benefit from it, and it is the desired goal for them.

The meaning is: as for the scum that used to float upon the flood and rise above it, or emerge from what they kindle fire upon, it goes away as refuse and becomes a vanishing falsehood. But as for the pure water or the earthly substance fashioned—in both of which is the benefit of the people and their enjoyment in their livelihood—it stays in the earth for the people to benefit from.

Then the Exalted said: كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثَالَ "Thus does Allah draw the parables" (13:17), concluding the statement with it. That is, the parables struck for the people in the speech of the Exalted resemble the parable struck in this verse in that they distinguish truth from falsehood and clarify for the people what benefits them in their worldly life and their hereafter.

It is not unlikely that the indication in His saying "Thus" (كَذَٰلِكَ) refers to what He mentioned regarding the descent of rain, the flowing of valleys with their scummy floods, and the kindling of earthly materials and the emergence of their scum. That is, the indication is to these external events and concrete formations themselves, not just the speech. This would indicate that these cosmic realities and events occurring in the world of the seen (عالم الشهادة) are parables struck that guide the possessors of intellect and insight to the realities in the world of the Unseen (عالم الغيب), just as what is in the world of the seen are signs (آيات) pointing to what is in the world of the Unseen, as is repeatedly mentioned in the Quran. There is no great difference between these witnessed things being "parables struck" or "pointing signs," as is evident.

Through this parable struck in the verse, several matters that are among the universals of divine knowledge (المعارف الإلهية) have become clear:

First: That the existence (الوجود) descending from Him, the Exalted, upon the existents—which is like the heavenly mercy and the rain descending from the clouds upon the earth—is in itself free from forms (الصور) and measures (الأقدار). Rather, it becomes measured from the side of the things themselves, just like the rainwater that takes on the measure, form, and accidents from the molds of the various valleys. Thus, things receive from the divine gift only according to their capacity (قابلية) and predispositions (استعدادات), and they vary according to the variation of predispositions, circumstances, and vessels.

This is a great principle indicated or alluded to by many verses of His speech, such as His saying: وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ "There is not a thing but its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (15:21), and His saying: وَأَنزَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ الْأَنْعَامِ ثَمَانِيَةَ أَزْوَاجٍ "and He sent down for you eight mates of the livestock" (39:6). All the verses concerning measure (القدر) serve as evidence for this.

Furthermore, these matters called "measures" (الأقدار), even if they are external to the heavenly effusion (الإفاضة) and serve as its limiters, are not outside the ownership and authority of Allah, the Glorified, nor do they occur without His permission. The Exalted has said: إِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُ الْأَمْرُ كُلُّهُ "and to Him all matters are returned" (11:123), and He said: بَل لِّلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ جَمِيعًا "Rather, all command belongs to Allah" (13:31). By joining these verses to the preceding ones, another principle appears, more subtle in meaning and broader in application.

Second: That the dispersal of this heavenly mercy into the valleys of the world and its limitation by the measures accompanying it is inseparable from impurities and dross that rise above it and appear from it. However, these are false (باطلة)—meaning vanishing and unstable—unlike that descending mercy limited by measures, for it is permanent and stable—meaning true (حقة). At that point, what exists is divided into truth (الحق), which is the stable and permanent, and falsehood (الباطل), which is the vanishing and unstable.

Truth is from Allah, the Glorified, while falsehood is not attributed to Him, even if it exists by His permission. The Exalted said: الْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ "The truth is from your Lord" (3:60), and He said: وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَاطِلًا "We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain [falsely]" (38:27). Thus, each of these existents contains a portion of truth that is stable and non-vanishing, which will return to Him upon the nullification of what is false within them, as He said: مَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with the truth and for a stated term" (46:3), and He said: وَيُحِقُّ اللَّهُ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِمَاتِهِ "and Allah confirms the truth by His words" (10:82), and He said: إِنَّ الْبَاطِلَ كَانَ زَهُوقًا "Indeed falsehood is bound to vanish" (17:81), and He said: بَلْ نَقْذِفُ بِالْحَقِّ عَلَى الْبَاطِلِ فَيَدْمَغُهُ فَإِذَا هُوَ زَاهِقٌ "Rather We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it crushes its head, and behold, it vanishes!" (21:18).

Third: That it is a law of the truth that it does not oppose another truth, nor does it crowd it out; rather, it supports it and benefits it on its path to perfection, driving it toward the felicity it seeks. This is indicated by His linking permanence and staying in the verse to the truth "which profits mankind."

The intended meaning of the negation of opposition is not the removal of conflict and crowding among things in our witnessed world; for this world is the abode of conflict and crowding, where one sees only fire extinguished by water, water evaporated by fire, earth consumed by plants, plants consumed by animals, then animals consuming one another, and finally the earth consuming them all. Rather, the meaning is that these things, despite their preying upon and biting one another, cooperate in achieving divine purposes and serve as means for one another to reach their specific species-goals. Their likeness is that of the adze and the wood: despite their conflict, they cooperate in the service of the carpenter to fashion a door, for example. Or like the two pans of a scale: in their opposition and struggle, they obey the one who holds the tongue of the scale to determine the weight. This is unlike falsehood—such as the bluntness of the adze or a deficiency in the weight—for it opposes the true purpose and frustrates the effort, corrupting without reforming and harming without benefiting.

To this category belong most of the verses of subjugation (التسخير) in the Quran, such as His saying: وَسَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا مِّنْهُ "He has disposed for you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth; all is from Him" (45:13). Each thing acts according to its nature, yet in doing so, it follows a path toward achieving what Allah, the Glorified, intended of the matter.

These principles derived from the noble verse are what produce the details of the laws of creation and origination. If you were to reflect upon the Quranic verses that mention truth and falsehood and ponder them deeply, you would see wonders.

Know that just as these principles apply to concrete matters and external realities, they also apply to sciences and beliefs. The likeness of true beliefs in the soul of the believer is like the water descending from the sky, flowing in the valleys according to their varying capacities; people benefit from it, their hearts are given life, and goodness and blessing remain within them. The likeness of false belief in the soul of the disbeliever is like the scum that swells upon the flood; it does not tarry before it goes away as refuse and becomes naught. The Exalted said: يُثَبِّتُ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِالْقَوْلِ الثَّابِتِ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ ۖ وَيُضِلُّ اللَّهُ الظَّالِمِينَ ۚ وَيَفْعَلُ اللَّهُ مَا يَشَاءُ "Allah fortifies those who have faith with a firm word in the life of this world and in the Hereafter, and Allah leads the wrongdoers astray, and Allah does whatever He wishes" (14:27).

His saying, the Exalted: لِلَّذِينَ اسْتَجَابُوا لِرَبِّهِمُ الْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ وَالَّذِينَ لَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَهُ "For those who answer their Lord shall be the best [reward]. And those who do not answer Him..." (13:18) until the end of the verse. The "cradle" (المهاد) is the bedding spread out for its owner and the prepared, trodden place. Hell is called a cradle (مهاد) because it is prepared for their settling therein due to their disbelief and evil deeds.

This verse and the nine verses following it are branched from the parable struck in the preceding verse—as previously indicated. In them, Allah, the Glorified, clarifies the effects of true belief, faith in Him, and answering His call, as well as the effects of false belief, disbelief in Him, and failing to answer His call. The context of the verses bears witness to this, as the discourse revolves around the outcome of faith and disbelief, and that the praised outcome belonging to faith cannot be replaced by anything, even if it were twice the blessings of the world.

Accordingly, it is most apparent that the "best" (الحسنى) refers to the "best outcome" (العاقبة الحسنى). What some have mentioned—that it refers to the "best reward" or "Paradise"—is true in terms of the ultimate result, for the praised outcome of faith and righteous deeds is the divine reward and Paradise. However, reward or Paradise is not intended here insofar as it is a reward or Paradise, but insofar as it is the outcome of their affair and the end toward which their striving leads.

This is supported, indeed proven, by His saying regarding them in the following verses after defining them by their specific attributes: أُولَٰئِكَ لَهُمْ عُقْبَى الدَّارِ جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَهَا "For such the outcome of the [ultimate] abode will be: the Gardens of Eden, which they will enter..." (13:22-23).

On this basis also, His saying: لَوْ أَنَّ لَهُم مَّا فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا وَمِثْلَهُ مَعَهُ لَافْتَدَوْا بِهِ "even if they possessed all that is on the earth and as much of it besides, they would surely offer it as ransom" (13:18) is placed in the position of a deleted consequence to indicate the magnitude of its affair and its reaching the ultimate limit of horror, shock, evil, and wretchedness that goes unmentioned.

The meaning is: those who did not answer their Lord will be struck by a matter—or will miss a matter, which is the result of answering and its best outcome—whose description is such that if they possessed all the blessings on earth that the human soul delights in—which is the ultimate of what a human can hope for and desire—and then its like were added to it—which is beyond human desire—in short, if they owned the ultimate of their desires in life and what is beyond that, they would agree to offer all that they supposedly own as a ransom for what they missed of the "best outcome." In some words of Ali (peace be upon him) describing it: "That which has descended upon them is beyond description."

Then the Exalted informed of this indescribable outcome of their affair, saying: أُولَٰئِكَ لَهُمْ سُوءُ الْحِسَابِ وَمَأْوَاهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ "For such there will be an adverse reckoning, and their abode will be hell" (13:18). The "adverse reckoning" (سوء الحساب) is the reckoning that grieves them and does not please them; it is the addition of the attribute to the described. Then the Exalted censured that, pointing to the evil outcome by His saying: وَبِئْسَ الْمِهادُ "and an evil resting place it is!" (13:18)—meaning, evil is the cradle that was prepared for them and in which they settle. The totality of His saying: "For such there will be an adverse reckoning..." etc., is in the position of a justification (تعليل) for the mentioned ransom. Justification by indication is frequent in speech; one says: "Do such and such to so-and-so, for he is the one whose attribute is such and such."

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: for those who answered the true call of their Lord is the best outcome; and those who did not answer Him have an outcome such that they would agree to pay as ransom to escape it more than they could ever desire, because the evil outcome that befalls them includes or is accompanied by an adverse reckoning and the settling in "and an evil resting place it is!"

In the verse, "answering" and "not answering" have been placed in the stead of faith and disbelief to suit the parable struck in the preceding verse regarding the descent of water from the sky and the acceptance of it by the valleys, each according to its capacity. Answering (الاستجابة) is the acceptance of the call.

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَن يَعْلَمُ أَنَّمَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ الْحَقُّ كَمَنْ هُوَ أَعْمَىٰ ۚ إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ "Is he who knows that what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth like him who is blind? Only those who possess intellect take admonition" (13:19). This is a rhetorical question of denial (استفهام إنكاري), and it is in the position of a justification for what the preceding verse contained, and a detailed exposition of the state of the two groups in terms of answering the call of truth or not.

The summary of the exposition: Truth settles in the hearts of those who answered their Lord, so their hearts become "intellects" (ألباب) and real hearts that have their effects and blessings, which are "taking admonition" (التذكر) and "gaining insight" (التبصر). Among the properties of these hearts by which their possessors are known is that the "possessors of intellect" (أولو الألباب) remain firm in fulfilling the covenant of Allah (عهد الله) taken from them by their primordial nature (الفطرة), so they do not break the pledge (الميثاق) of their Lord. They remain firm in honoring what Allah has commanded to be joined—which is the kinship (الرحم) through which Allah has channeled creation—so they join it while they are in awe and fear. They remain firm through patience (الصبر) during calamities, against sin, and upon obedience. They proceed by turning to their Lord, which is prayer (الصلاة); by reforming society, which is spending (الإنفاق); and by repelling evil with good.

These are the ones for whom is the praised outcome of the abode, which is Paradise; they enter it, and the rewards of their aforementioned righteous deeds are reflected back to them therein. Thus, they are accompanied therein by the righteous among their fathers, spouses, and progeny, just as they joined the kinship in the world. The angels enter upon them from every gate, greeting them with peace because of what they endured with patience, just as they opened the gates of various acts of worship and obedience in the world. This is the effect of the truth.

His saying: "Is he who knows that what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth like him who is blind?"—the question is for denial, as mentioned. It negates equality between the one in whose heart the knowledge of the truth has settled and the one who is ignorant of the truth. In describing the one ignorant of the truth as "blind," there is an allusion to the fact that the one who knows it becomes "seeing" (بصير), as He named them "the blind and the seeing" in His earlier saying: "Say, ‘Are the blind and the seeing equal?’" Knowledge of the truth is insight (بصيرة), and ignorance of it is blindness (عمى). Gaining insight yields "taking admonition" (التذكر), which is why He counted it among the properties of the possessors of knowledge by His saying: "Only those... take admonition."

His saying: "Only those who possess intellect take admonition" is in the position of a justification for what preceded it—namely, His saying: "Is he who knows..." etc. That is, the two are not equal because the possessors of knowledge have a "taking of admonition" that the possessors of blindness and ignorance do not have. He placed "possessors of intellect" (أولو الألباب) in the stead of "possessors of knowledge," thereby indicating another claim that serves the purpose of justification, as if it were said: they are not equal because one of the two groups has a "taking of admonition" that the other does not, and this "taking of admonition" is specific to them because they have "intellects" (ألباب) and hearts, which others do not possess.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِ اللَّهِ وَلَا يَنقُضُونَ الْمِيثَاقَ "Those who fulfill Allah’s covenant and do not break the pledge" (13:20). The apparent context is that the second sentence is an explanatory apposition (عطف تفسيري) to the first. Thus, the "pledge" (الميثاق) they do not break is the "covenant of Allah" (عهد الله) they fulfill. The intended meaning of this covenant and pledge—by the evidence of what was mentioned in the preceding verse regarding their "taking admonition"—is that which they covenanted with their Lord and pledged by the tongue of their primordial nature (الفطرة): that they would profess His oneness and act according to the requirements of His monotheism (التوحيد). For the human being is fashioned upon His monotheism and that which His monotheism calls for. This is a covenant made by the primordial nature and a knot tied by it.

As for the covenants and pledges taken by means of the prophets and messengers by a command from Allah, and the laws and statutes, all of these are branches of the primordial pledge, for the religion (الدين) is primordial.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ مَا أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَن يُوصَلَ "and those who join what Allah has commanded to be joined..." (13:21) etc. It is apparent that the "command" (الأمر) is the legislative command (الأمر التشريعي) sent down, as evidenced by the conclusion of the verse: "and fear an adverse reckoning." For the reckoning is apparently based on the laws sent down in the Sharia, even if they are perceived by the primordial nature—such as the ugliness of injustice and the goodness of justice. For the "oppressed/weak" (المستضعف) who was not reached by the divine command and was not negligent is not reckoned for it as others are. It has been mentioned in our previous discussions that the proof (الحجة) is not completed against the human being by mere primordial perception unless the path of revelation (الوحي) is joined to it. The Exalted said: لِّئَلَّا يَكُونَ لِلنَّاسِ عَلَى اللَّهِ حُجَّةٌ بَعْدَ الرُّسُلِ "so that mankind may not have any argument against Allah after the apostles" (4:165).

The verse is absolute; thus, it refers to every connection that Allah, the Glorified, has commanded. Among its most famous instances is the joining of kinship (صلة الرحم) which Allah commanded and emphasized the obligation of. The Exalted said: وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ الَّذِي تَسَاءَلُونَ بِهِ وَالْأَرْحَامَ "and be wary of Allah by whom you adjure one another, and the ties of kinship" (4:1).

He emphasized the statement regarding it by what is at the end of the verse: وَيَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ وَيَخَافُونَ سُوءَ الْحِسَابِ "and are in awe of their Lord and fear an adverse reckoning" (13:21). He pointed out that in abandoning the connection is a violation of Allah's command—so one should be in awe of Allah in that regard—and an evil deed written in the record of deeds, preserved against the person, whose adverse reckoning one must fear.

It appears that the difference between "awe" (الخشية) and "fear" (الخوف) is that "awe" is the heart's being affected by the approach of evil or what is in its status, while "fear" is being affected in action—meaning proceeding to prepare that which guards against the dreaded thing, even if the heart is not affected. For this reason, He, the Glorified, said in describing His prophets: وَلَا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَدًا إِلَّا اللَّهَ "and they do not fear [are not in awe of] anyone except Allah" (33:39). He negated "awe" from them toward anyone else, while He affirmed "fear" (الخوف) for them toward others in several places of His speech, such as His saying: فَأَوْجَسَ فِي نَفْسِهِ خِيفَةً مُّوسَىٰ "So Moses felt a fear within himself" (20:67), and His saying: وَإِمَّا تَخَافَنَّ مِن قَوْمٍ خِيَانَةً "If you fear treachery from a people..." (8:58).

Perhaps to this returns what Al-Raghib mentioned regarding the difference between them: that "awe" (الخشية) is a fear mixed with veneration (تعظيم), and it most often arises from knowledge. For this reason, the scholars were specifically associated with it in His saying: إِنَّمَا يَخْشَى اللَّهَ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ الْعُلَمَاءُ "Only those of Allah’s servants who are possessors of knowledge fear [are in awe of] Him" (35:28). Similarly, the statement of some: that "awe" is more intense than "fear" because it is derived from their saying "a tree is khashiyah" meaning dry. Also, the statement of some: that "fear" (الخوف) relates to the disliked thing and its source—one says "I feared the disease" and "I feared Zayd"—unlike "awe" (الخشية), which relates to the source rather than the disliked thing itself—one says "I was in awe of Allah."

Were it not for their return to what we have presented, they would be clearly contradicted. Some mentioned that the difference is predominant rather than universal, and others that there is no difference between them at all—which is rejected by the verses we have presented.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ صَبَرُوا ابْتِغَاءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَقَامُوا الصَّلاةَ وَأَنفَقُوا "and those who are patient for the sake of their Lord’s pleasure, and maintain the prayer, and spend..." (13:22) until the end of the verse. The absolute mention of "patience" (الصبر) indicates their being characterized by all its branches and types: patience during calamity, patience upon obedience, and patience against sin. However, it is nevertheless restricted by His saying: "for the sake of their Lord’s pleasure" (ابْتِغَاءَ وَجْهِ رَبِّهِمْ)—meaning seeking the "face" (وجه) of their Lord. Thus, the attribute for which they are praised is that their patience be for the sake of Allah, because the discourse is about their attributes that arise and grow within them from their answering their Lord and their knowledge of the truth of what was sent down to them from their Lord—not every attribute that people praise among themselves even if it is not linked to their servitude (العبودية) and faith in their Lord, such as patience during hardship out of defiance and self-admiration or seeking beautiful praise and the like, as was said:

And my saying whenever [my soul] heaved and surged: "Stay in your place; you shall be praised or find rest."

The intended meaning of the "Face of the Lord" (وجه الرب), the Exalted, is the aspect attributed to Him, the Exalted, of the action and the like—which is the aspect upon which the action appears and settles with Him, the Exalted. That is, the reward (المثوبة) that is with Him, which remains by His permanence. The Exalted has said: وَاللَّهُ عِندَهُ حُسْنُ الثَّوَابِ "and with Allah is the best of rewards" (3:195), and He said: وَمَا عِندَ اللَّهِ بَاقٍ "and what is with Allah is lasting" (16:96), and He said: كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هَالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ "Everything is to perish except His Face" (28:88).

His saying: "and maintain the prayer" (وَأَقَامُوا الصَّلاةَ)—meaning they made it "standing" (قائمة), not falling through neglect of its parts and conditions or by belittling its affair. The conjunction of prayer and what follows it to "patience" is the conjunction of the specific to the general, out of concern for its status and veneration of its affair.

His saying: "and spend of what We have provided them, secretly and openly" (وَأَنفَقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقْنَاهُمْ سِرًّا وَعَلانِيَةً). The intended meaning is absolute spending, more general than the obligatory (الواجب) and others; the verse is Meccan, and the obligation of Zakat had not yet been sent down at its revelation. Restricting the spending by His saying "secretly and openly" is to indicate their fulfillment of its right. For among spending is that in which secrecy is better, and among it is that in which openness is better. Thus, whoever believes in what Allah has sent down with the truth must fulfill the right of each: so he keeps the spending secret if its openness is a likely cause of showing off (الرياء), seeking reputation, or causing insult or loss of face; and he makes it open in that which its openness encourages people toward righteousness and virtue, repels accusation, and the like.

His saying: وَيَدْرَءُونَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ السَّيِّئَةَ "and repel evil with good" (13:22). Repelling (الدرء) is pushing away. The meaning is: if they encounter an evil, they bring a good that exceeds it or equals it, thereby repelling the evil. This is more general than it being an evil that issued from themselves—which they repelled with a good they performed, for "good deeds take away evil deeds"—or repelled it with repentance (التوبة) to their Lord, for "the one who repents from sin is like one who has no sin." Or it may be an evil committed by others against them—such as one who wronged them, whom they repelled with forgiveness or by doing good to him; or one who treated them harshly, whom they met with good character and a cheerful face, as when "the ignorant address them, they say: 'Peace'"; or one who committed a vice, which they forbade; or one who abandoned a virtue, which they commanded.

All of that is from "repelling evil with good," and there is no evidence from the side of the wording that indicates restriction to some of these aspects at all.

The expression has varied in these attributes mentioned for the "possessors of intellect": "those who fulfill, and do not break, and join, and are in awe, and fear, and were patient, and maintained, and spent, and repel." Six were brought in the present/future tense (المضارع), and three in the past tense (الاضي).

It has been narrated from some regarding the reason for this that the expression in His saying: "and those who were patient for the sake of their Lord’s pleasure, and maintained the prayer, and spent of what We have provided them..." etc., is in the past tense, while what preceded was in the present/future tense, by way of stylistic variation (التفنن) in eloquence. This is because these verbs occurred as the relative clause (صلة) for the relative pronoun (الموصول)—namely "those who" (الَّذِينَ)—and the relative pronoun and its clause are in the meaning of a conditional noun with a conditional sentence. The past and present/future are equal in meaning in a conditional sentence, such as "If you struck, I struck" and "If you strike, I strike"; thus it is with what is in its meaning.

For this reason, the grammarians said: if the past tense occurs as a relative clause or an attribute for a general indefinite noun, it is possible that the past is intended or that the future is intended. Of the first is: "those to whom the people said..." and of the second is: "except those who repent before you have power over them."

In this view is the point that the cancellation of the specific time of the verb—past or future—in a condition and what is in its meaning does not necessitate the cancellation of the implications of the tenses, such as "realization" (التحقق) in the past and "occurrence and continuity" (الجريان والاستمرار) and the like in the present/future. For in the past, for example, there is a concern for realization even if the time is cancelled. Thus, the validity of questioning the point of the variation in expression remains.

It is understood from the words of others regarding its reason that the intended meaning of the preceding attributes—namely fulfilling the covenant, joining, awe, and fear—is accompaniment (استصحاب) and continuity. However, since patience (الصبر) was that upon which the engagement with those attributes depended, its status was emphasized; thus it was expressed in the past tense indicating realization. Similarly, in prayer and spending, the past tense was used out of concern for their status.

In this view is the point that some of the preceding attributes are no less important than patience, prayer, and spending—such as fulfilling the covenant of Allah, which was intended as faith in Allah by answering the call of the primordial nature. If concern for status were the reason, it would have been necessary to express it in the past tense like patience, prayer, and spending.

What I believe—and Allah knows best—is that the totality of His saying: "and those who were patient for the sake of their Lord’s pleasure, and maintained the prayer, and spent of what We have provided them, secretly and openly, and repel evil with good" is driven to clarify a single meaning: the performance of righteous deeds (العمل الصالح)—meaning performing the obligations, abandoning the prohibitions, and redressing the flaws that occur therein by way of exception with "good." Thus, the righteous deed is the primary (بالأصالة) intention, and "repelling evil with good"—which is the redressing of the flaw occurring in the deed—is intended secondarily (بالتبع) as a completer of the deficiency.

Had the discourse followed the previous context and it were said: "and those who are patient for the sake of their Lord’s pleasure, and maintain the prayer, and spend of what We have provided them, secretly and openly, and repel evil with good," this specific concern would have been lost, and what was mentioned regarding the primary and secondary nature would have been nullified. But it was said: "and those who were patient for the sake of their Lord’s pleasure," so all the established patience was taken as a single, continuous matter to indicate the occurrence of all patience from them. Then it was said: "and repel..." etc., to indicate the permanence of their vigilance regarding it to redress the flaws that occurred therein. Likewise in prayer and spending. Understand this.

This concern is, in a way, similar to the concern in His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا رَبُّنَا اللَّهُ ثُمَّ اسْتَقَامُوا تَتَنَزَّلُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ "Indeed those who say, ‘Our Lord is Allah!’ and then remain steadfast, the angels descend upon them..." (41:30), where it indicates the branching of the angels' descent upon the realization of their saying "Our Lord is Allah" and their steadfastness, rather than the continuity upon it.

His saying: أُولَٰئِكَ لَهُمْ عُقْبَى الدَّارِ "For such the outcome of the [ultimate] abode will be" (13:22)—meaning its praised outcome. For it is the outcome in reality, because a thing does not end, according to what Allah has fashioned it upon, except in an outcome that suits it and in which its felicity lies. As for the censured, evil outcome, in it is the nullification of the thing's outcome due to a flaw occurring in it. It is only called an "outcome" by a type of extension. For this reason, "the outcome of the abode" was mentioned absolutely in the verse and intended as the praised outcome, and it was contrasted in the opposing verses by His saying: "and for them is the evil of the abode." From here it appears that the intended meaning of "the abode" (الدار) is this worldly abode—meaning the life of the abode—so the outcome is its outcome.

His saying, the Exalted: جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَهَا وَمَن صَلَحَ مِنْ آبَائِهِمْ وَأَزْوَاجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ "the Gardens of Eden, which they will enter along with such as are righteous from among their fathers, their spouses, and their progeny" (13:23). "Eden" (العدن) is stability (الاستقرار); it is said ‘adana in such a place when he settled in it. From it is derived ma‘dan (mine) for the settling place of earthly jewels. "Gardens of Eden" means gardens of a type of stability in which there is eternity (خلود) and peace from every side.

"The Gardens of Eden..." etc., is a substitute (بدل) or an explanatory apposition (عطف بيان) for His saying: "the outcome of the abode." That is, the praised outcome of this abode is the Gardens of Eden and eternity. Thus, this worldly life, according to how Allah has fashioned it, is but a single, connected life: its beginning is toil and trial, and its end is ease, delight, and peace. This promise is that which He, the Exalted, recounts His fulfillment of, narrating from the people of Paradise in His saying: وَقَالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي صَدَقَنَا وَعْدَهُ وَأَوْرَثَنَا الْأَرْضَ نَتَبَوَّأُ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ حَيْثُ نَشَاءُ "They will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has fulfilled His promise to us and made us heirs to the earth, that we may settle in paradise wherever we may wish!’" (39:74).

The verse—as you have heard—parallels His saying: "join what Allah has commanded to be joined," and is a clarification of the outcome of this truth which they adopted and acted upon, and a glad tiding to them that they will accompany the righteous among their kin and families—fathers, mothers, progeny, brothers, sisters, and others. The totality is included in His saying: "their fathers, their spouses, and their progeny," because mothers are the spouses of fathers, and brothers, sisters, paternal uncles, maternal uncles, and their children are the progeny of the fathers and the fathers' fathers. Thus, in the verse is a subtle conciseness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْمَلَائِكَةُ يَدْخُلُونَ عَلَيْهِم مِّن كُلِّ بَابٍ سَلَامٌ عَلَيْكُم بِمَا صَبَرْتُمْ ۚ فَنِعْمَ عُقْبَى الدَّارِ "and the angels will enter upon them from every gate: ‘Peace be to you, for your patience.’ How excellent is the outcome of the [ultimate] abode!" (13:23-24). This is the outcome of their righteous deeds which they maintained in every gate of the gates of life through patience upon obedience, against sin, and during calamity, along with awe and fear.

His saying: "Peace be to you, for your patience. How excellent is the outcome of the [ultimate] abode!" is the speech of the angels as they addressed them with eternal security and peace, and a praised outcome that is never touched by censure or evil.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يَنقُضُونَ عَهْدَ اللَّهِ مِن بَعْدِ مِيثَاقِهِ "But those who break Allah’s covenant after its pledge..." (13:25) until the end of the verse. This is a clarification of the state of the non-believers by way of contrast. He contrasted His saying: وَيُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ "and cause corruption on the earth" (13:25) with the rest of what was mentioned in the preceding verses after fulfilling the covenant of Allah and joining—namely, the righteous deeds. In this is an allusion to the fact that righteous deeds are what guarantee the reform of the earth and the cultivation of the abode in a manner that leads to the felicity of the human species and the growth of human society. This has been previously clarified in the proof for general prophethood.

The Exalted clarified the recompense for their work and the outcome of their affair by His saying: أُولَٰئِكَ لَهُمُ اللَّعْنَةُ وَلَهُمْ سُوءُ الدَّارِ "for such shall be the curse, and for them shall be the evil abode" (13:25). The "curse" (اللعن) is removal from mercy and expulsion from every honor. This is only because of their devotion to falsehood and their rejection of the truth sent down from Allah; and for falsehood, there is nothing but ruin.

His saying, the Exalted: اللَّهُ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَاءُ وَيَقْدِرُ "Allah outspreads provision for whomever He wishes and straitens it..." (13:26) until the end of the verse. This is a clarification that what the two groups were given—of the praised outcome and eternal Paradise, and of the curse and the evil abode—is from the provision (الرزق) that Allah provides to whomever He wishes and however He wishes, without any restriction upon Him or compulsion.

He clarified that His act, the Exalted, continues in accordance with what He has established of the system of truth and falsehood. Thus, true belief and acting upon it leads to being provided with Paradise and peace, while falsehood in belief and acting upon it leads to the curse, the evil abode, and a wretched life.

His saying: وَفَرِحُوا بِالْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَمَا الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا فِي الْآخِرَةِ إِلَّا مَتَاعٌ "They exult in the life of this world, but the life of this world is nothing but a [fleeting] provision compared to the Hereafter" (13:26). He intends by this—as the context suggests—that the real provision is the provision of the Hereafter. However, due to their inclination toward the outward appearance of the worldly life and its adornment, they relied upon it and exulted in it. They were mistaken, for it is a life not intended for itself, nor is it eternal in its permanence; rather, it is intended for something else, which is the life of the Hereafter. Thus, in comparison to the Hereafter, it is a "provision" (متاع) enjoyed for the sake of another and for another, not sought for itself. The worldly life, in comparison to the life of the Hereafter, is only part of the truth if it is taken as a prelude to it through which its provision is earned. But if it is taken as sought independently, it is nothing but part of the falsehood that goes away as refuse and is of no benefit. The Exalted said: وَمَا هَٰذِهِ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ ۚ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوَانُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life (الحيوان), had they known" (29:64).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ihtijaj, narrated from Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him)—in a tradition in which he mentions the states of the disbelievers—regarding His saying: فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفَاءً ۖ وَأَمَّا مَا يَنفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ "As for the scum, it leaves as refuse; but as for what profits mankind, it stays in the earth": "The scum in this place is the speech of the atheists—which they established in the Quran—so it vanishes, becomes null, and dissipates upon investigation. That which profits mankind from it is the Revelation (التنزيل) which falsehood cannot approach—from before it or from behind it—and the hearts accept it. The 'earth' in this place is the location of knowledge and its settling place."

I say: The intended meaning of "the Revelation" is the real intent of His speech, the Exalted. By "the speech of the atheists established in the Quran," he means what they interpreted according to their own opinions. What he (peace be upon him) mentioned are some of the instances, while the verse is broader in its significance, as previously mentioned.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Qatadah regarding His saying: الَّذِينَ يُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِ اللَّهِ وَلَا يَنقُضُونَ الْمِيثَاقَ "Those who fulfill Allah’s covenant and do not break the pledge": "It is incumbent upon you to fulfill the covenant and not break the pledge—for Allah has forbidden it and has given the strongest warning regarding it, mentioning it in some twenty-odd verses as an advice to you—a warning to you and a proof against you. Matters are only magnified by that which Allah has magnified them with among the people of understanding, the people of intellect—and the people of knowledge of Allah. It was mentioned to us that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to say in his sermon: 'There is no faith for one who has no trustworthiness—and no religion for one who has no covenant.'"

I say: The apparent meaning of his words is to carry "the covenant and the pledge" in the noble verse to that which occurs among the people themselves. You have already seen that the apparent context is contrary to this.

In al-Kafi, with its chain to ‘Umar ibn Yazid, who said: "I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: الَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ مَا أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَن يُوصَلَ 'and those who join what Allah has commanded to be joined.' He said: 'Your kinship (قرابتك).'"

Also in it, with another chain from him, he said: "I said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): الَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ مَا أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَن يُوصَلَ. He said: 'It was revealed regarding the kinship (رحم) of the progeny of Muhammad (آل محمد)—and it may be regarding your kinship.' Then he said: 'Do not be of those who say regarding a thing that it is only in one thing.'"

I say: He means: do not restrict the Quran to a single meaning if it admits another meaning, for the Quran has an outward and an inward. Allah has made the love of the near of kin—which is part of the connection—the wage of the message in His saying: قُل لَّا أَسْأَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبَىٰ "Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it except the love of [my] relatives’" (42:23). The following narration points to what we have mentioned.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from ‘Umar ibn Maryam, who said: "I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: الَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ مَا أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَن يُوصَلَ. He said: 'Of that is the joining of kinship—and the ultimate of its interpretation (تأويل) is your joining us.'"

In it, from Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl, who said: "I heard the Righteous Servant (peace be upon him) saying: الَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ مَا أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَن يُوصَلَ. He said: 'It is the kinship of the progeny of Muhammad (آل محمد) suspended from the Throne (العرش), saying: "O Allah, join the one who joins me and cut off the one who cuts me off," and it runs through every kinship.'"

I say: There are other narrations with these meanings. The meaning of the kinship being suspended from the Throne has been previously mentioned in the commentary on the beginning of Surah al-Nisa’ in the fourth volume of this book.

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Sama‘ah ibn Mihran from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Among what Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has also obligated in wealth besides Zakat is His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: الَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ مَا أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَن يُوصَلَ."

I say: Al-‘Ayyashi narrated it in his Tafsir.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Hammad ibn ‘Uthman from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "That he said to a man: 'O so-and-so, what is between you and your brother?' He said: 'May I be your ransom, I had a right against him, so I extracted my right from him in full (استقصيت).' Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: 'Inform me about the saying of Allah: وَيَخَافُونَ سُوءَ الْحِسَابِ "and fear an adverse reckoning." Do you think they feared that He would act unjustly toward them or wrong them? No, by Allah, they feared the full extraction (الاستقصاء) and the meticulous scrutiny (المداقة).'"

I say: It was also narrated in al-Ma‘ani and Tafsir al-Qummi.

In it, from Hisham ibn Salim from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: وَيَخَافُونَ سُوءَ الْحِسَابِ: "He said: 'The full extraction and meticulous scrutiny.' And he said: 'The evil deeds are reckoned against them—and the good deeds are not reckoned for them.'"

I say: The conclusion of the tradition is narrated through various chains from him (peace be upon him). The non-reckoning of good deeds is only because of the meticulous scrutiny and the finding of subtle aspects of flaw, as indicated by the following narration.

In it, from Hisham from him (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "He said: 'The evil deeds are reckoned against them—and the good deeds are not reckoned for them, and that is the full extraction.'"

In it, from Jabir from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Goodness to parents and joining of kinship ease the reckoning.' Then he recited this verse: الَّذِينَ يَصِلُونَ مَا أَمَرَ اللَّهُ بِهِ أَن يُوصَلَ وَيَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ وَيَخَافُونَ سُوءَ الْحِسَابِ."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, regarding His saying: جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ: "Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ali, who said: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "The Garden of Eden is a branch that Allah planted with His hand—then He said to it: 'Be,' and it was."'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain to ‘Amr ibn Shimr al-Yamani, raising the tradition to Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Patience (الصبر) is of three types—patience during calamity, patience upon obedience, and patience against sin. So whoever is patient during calamity until he repels it with beautiful consolation—Allah writes for him three hundred degrees, the distance between each degree being like that between the sky and the earth. And whoever is patient upon obedience, Allah writes for him six hundred degrees—the distance between each degree being like that between the depths of the earth and the Throne. And whoever is patient against sin, Allah writes for him nine hundred degrees—the distance between each degree being like that between the depths of the earth and the end of the Throne.'"

Ayahs

وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُضِلُّ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَنْ أَنَابَ (٢٧)
The faithless say, ‘Why has not some sign been sent down to him from his Lord?’ Say, ‘Indeed Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and guides to Himself those who turn penitently [to Him] (27)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَتَطْمَئِنُّ قُلُوبُهُم بِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ أَلَا بِذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ ٱلْقُلُوبُ (٢٨)
—those who have faith and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of Allah.’ Behold! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance! (28)
ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ طُوبَىٰ لَهُمْ وَحُسْنُ مَـَٔابٍ (٢٩)
Those who have faith and do righteous deeds—happy are they and good is their [ultimate] destination. (29)
كَذَٰلِكَ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ فِىٓ أُمَّةٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهَآ أُمَمٌ لِّتَتْلُوَا۟ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ وَهُمْ يَكْفُرُونَ بِٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۚ قُلْ هُوَ رَبِّى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ مَتَابِ (٣٠)
Thus have We sent you to a nation before which many nations have passed away, that you may recite to them what We have revealed to you. Yet they defy the All-beneficent. Say, ‘He is my Lord; there is no god except Him; in Him I have put my trust, and to Him will be my return.’ (30)
وَلَوْ أَنَّ قُرْءَانًا سُيِّرَتْ بِهِ ٱلْجِبَالُ أَوْ قُطِّعَتْ بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضُ أَوْ كُلِّمَ بِهِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ ۗ بَل لِّلَّهِ ٱلْأَمْرُ جَمِيعًا ۗ أَفَلَمْ يَا۟يْـَٔسِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَن لَّوْ يَشَآءُ ٱللَّهُ لَهَدَى ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا ۗ وَلَا يَزَالُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ تُصِيبُهُم بِمَا صَنَعُوا۟ قَارِعَةٌ أَوْ تَحُلُّ قَرِيبًا مِّن دَارِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِىَ وَعْدُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُخْلِفُ ٱلْمِيعَادَ (٣١)
If only it were a Quran whereby the mountains could be moved, or the earth could be toured, or the dead could be spoken to.... Indeed, all dispensation belongs to Allah. Have not the faithful yet realised that had Allah wished He would have guided mankind all together? The faithless will continue to be visited by catastrophes because of their doings—or they will land near their habitations—until Allah’s promise comes to pass. Indeed Allah does not break His promise. (31)
وَلَقَدِ ٱسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَأَمْلَيْتُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهُمْ ۖ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ عِقَابِ (٣٢)
Apostles were certainly derided before you. But then I gave respite to those who were faithless, then I seized them; so how was My retribution? (32)
أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قَآئِمٌ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ نَفْسٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ ۗ وَجَعَلُوا۟ لِلَّهِ شُرَكَآءَ قُلْ سَمُّوهُمْ ۚ أَمْ تُنَبِّـُٔونَهُۥ بِمَا لَا يَعْلَمُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَم بِظَـٰهِرٍ مِّنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ ۗ بَلْ زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مَكْرُهُمْ وَصُدُّوا۟ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۗ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْ هَادٍ (٣٣)
Is He who sustains every soul in spite of what it earns [comparable to the idols]? Yet they ascribe partners to Allah! Say, ‘Name them!’ Will you inform Him concerning something He does not know about on the earth, or concerning [what are] mere words? Indeed, to the faithless, their scheming is presented as decorous, and they have been barred from the [right] way; and whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide. (33)
لَّهُمْ عَذَابٌ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَشَقُّ ۖ وَمَا لَهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَاقٍ (٣٤)
There is a punishment for them in the life of this world, and the punishment of the Hereafter will surely be harder, and they have no defender against Allah. (34)
۞ مَّثَلُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ٱلَّتِى وُعِدَ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ ۖ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ أُكُلُهَا دَآئِمٌ وَظِلُّهَا ۚ تِلْكَ عُقْبَى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟ ۖ وَّعُقْبَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ ٱلنَّارُ (٣٥)
A description of the paradise promised to the Godwary: streams run in it, its fruits and shade are everlasting. Such is the requital of those who are Godwary; and the requital of the faithless is the Fire. (35)

(Commentary)

This is a second return to the statement of the disbelievers (الكفار): لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ "Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?"—meaning, a sign that we might see and thereby be guided, turning from polytheism (الشرك) to faith (الإيمان). The Exalted responds to this by stating that neither guidance (الهدى) nor misguidance (الضلال) depends upon the sending of a sign; rather, that belongs entirely to Him, the Exalted: He leads astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills. The divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — the established way of Allah in His creation — has proceeded to guide those who turn to Him in repentance (أناب) and possess a heart that finds rest in His remembrance; for such people is a beautiful destination and the ultimate abode. Conversely, it proceeds to misguide those who disbelieve in His clear signs; for them is a punishment in the life of this world, and the punishment of the Hereafter is more severe, and they have no protector against Allah.

Furthermore, even if Allah were to send down a miraculous sign like the Quran (القرآن), if it were possible for anyone to be guided without the will of Allah, it would be through it. However, the matter rests with Allah, and He, the Glorified, does not will the guidance of those for whom misguidance has been decreed among the people of disbelief and deception; and whomever Allah leads astray, for him there is no guide.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ أَنابَ The faithless say, ‘Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?’ Say, ‘Indeed Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and He guides to Himself whomever turns [to Him] in penitence’ (13:27). This is a return to the statement of the disbelievers: لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ "Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?" They intended by this that if a sign from his Lord were sent down to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, they would be guided by it and respond to him, as they do not consider the Quran revealed to him to be a sign.

The evidence for their intending this meaning is His saying immediately after: قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشاءُ "Say, ‘Indeed Allah leads astray whomever He wishes...’" etc.

And His saying later: وَلَوْ أَنَّ قُرْآناً سُيِّرَتْ بِهِ الْجِبالُ "And if there were a Quran by which the mountains were moved..." up to His saying: بَلْ لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ جَمِيعاً "Rather, all dispensation belongs to Allah" (13:31). And His saying later: وَصُدُّوا عَنِ السَّبِيلِ "and they are barred from the way" (13:33) until the end of the verse.

Thus, the Exalted answered their statement by commanding His Prophet to convey to them: قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ أَنابَ "Say, ‘Indeed Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and He guides to Himself whomever turns [to Him] in penitence.’" This conveys that the matter does not depend on the sign such that they would be guided by its revelation or misguided by its absence; rather, the matter of misguidance and guidance belongs to Allah, the Glorified—He leads astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills.

Since it was not safe from the possibility that they might imagine the matter revolves around an arbitrary, unorganized will, He alluded to the rejection of this by substituting "and He guides to Himself whom He wills" with His saying: وَيَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ أَنابَ "and He guides to Himself whomever turns [to Him] in penitence." Thus, He clarified that the matter rests upon the will of Allah, the Exalted, which proceeds according to a permanent sunnah (سنة) and a precise, continuous system (نظام). That is, He wills the guidance of those who turn and return to Him, and He misguides those who turn away and do not repent. So, whoever clothes himself with the attribute of penitence (الإنابة) and return to the Truth, and is not shackled by the chains of whims (الأهواء), Allah guides him through this true call. But whoever is otherwise, he strays from the path even if it is straight, and signs do not benefit him even if they are miraculous; and signs and warners do not avail a people who do not believe.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ يُضِلُ "Indeed Allah leads astray..." etc., is based on the premise: that Allah misguides by His will those who do not turn to Him, and guides to Himself by His will those who turn to Him in penitence.

It also becomes clear that the pronoun "to Him" (إِلَيْهِ) in "guides to Himself" (يَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ) refers to Him, the Exalted. The view mentioned by some that it refers to the Quran, and by others that it refers to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, is not well-founded.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَتَطْمَئِنُّ قُلُوبُهُمْ بِذِكْرِ اللهِ أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ —those who have faith and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of Allah.’ Behold! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance! (13:28). Tranquility (الاطمئنان) is stillness (السكون) and stability (الاستقرار), and finding rest in something is to be still toward it.

The apparent context (السياق) suggests that the beginning of the verse is an explanation of His saying at the end of the previous verse: مَنْ أَنابَ "whomever turns [to Him] in penitence." Thus, faith (الإيمان) and the heart's tranquility through the remembrance of Allah constitute penitence (الإنابة). This represents a preparation and readiness (تهيؤ واستعداد) on the part of the servant that is followed by the gift of divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية), just as transgression (الفسق) and deviation (الزيغ) in the context of misguidance are a preparation and readiness followed by misguidance from Allah, as He said: وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ "and He misleads by it only the transgressors" (2:26), and He said: فَلَمَّا زاغُوا أَزاغَ اللهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ "So when they swerved, Allah made their hearts swerve, and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot" (61:5).

Faith in Allah, the Exalted, for instance, is not merely the perception (إدراك) that He is the Truth; for mere perception may coexist with arrogance (الاستكبار) and denial (الجحود), as the Exalted said: وَجَحَدُوا بِها وَاستَيْقَنَتْها أَنْفُسُهُمْ "They impugned them—though they were convinced of them in their hearts" (27:14). Yet faith cannot coexist with denial. Thus, faith in something is not merely the perception that it is true, but rather a specific compliance (مطاوعة) and acceptance (قبول) by the soul regarding what it has perceived, which necessitates its submission (تسليم) to it and to the effects it entails. Its sign is the compliance of all other faculties and limbs and their acceptance of it, just as the soul complied and accepted. You may see one accustomed to certain blameworthy actions perceiving their ugliness or evil, yet he does not desist because his soul does not believe in this [perception] nor submit to it. But sometimes the soul complies and submits after perceiving it, and he desists immediately; that is faith.

This is what appears from His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللهُ أَنْ يَهْدِيَهُ يَشْرَحْ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ وَمَنْ يُرِدْ أَنْ يُضِلَّهُ يَجْعَلْ صَدْرَهُ ضَيِّقاً حَرَجاً كَأَنَّما يَصَّعَّدُ فِي السَّماءِ "Whomever Allah desires to guide, He opens his breast to Islam, and whomever He desires to lead astray, He makes his breast narrow and straitened as if he were climbing into the sky" (6:125). Thus, guidance from Allah, the Glorified, requires from the servant's heart or breast—and ultimately from his soul—something whose relation to it is that of acceptance and compliance to the thing accepted and complied with. This was expressed in the verse of al-An'am as the "opening of the breast" (شرح الصدر) and its expansion, and in the verse under discussion as "faith" and "tranquility of the heart" (اطمئنان القلب), which is for a person to see himself secure in his acceptance and compliance, and for his heart to be still toward it and for it to be settled in his heart without being agitated by it or uprooted from it.

From this, it becomes clear that His saying: وَتَطْمَئِنُّ قُلُوبُهُمْ بِذِكْرِ اللهِ "and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of Allah" is an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسيري) to His saying: آمَنُوا "those who have faith." Thus, faith in Allah is inseparable from the heart's tranquility through the remembrance of Allah, the Exalted.

This does not contradict what is in His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ الَّذِينَ إِذا ذُكِرَ اللهُ وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ "The faithful are only those whose hearts tremble when Allah is mentioned" (8:2). For the trembling (الوجل) mentioned therein is a state of the heart that precedes the tranquility mentioned in the verse under discussion, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ نَزَّلَ أَحْسَنَ الْحَدِيثِ كِتاباً مُتَشابِهاً مَثانِيَ تَقْشَعِرُّ مِنْهُ جُلُودُ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ ثُمَّ تَلِينُ جُلُودُهُمْ وَقُلُوبُهُمْ إِلى ذِكْرِ اللهِ ذلِكَ هُدَى اللهِ يَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ "Allah has sent down the best of discourses, a scripture [with parts] resembling each other, [and] paired, whereat quiver the skins of those who fear their Lord, then their skins and hearts soften to Allah’s remembrance. That is Allah’s guidance: He guides with it whomever He wishes" (39:23).
