This is because blessing (النعمة) is what descends from Him, the Glorified, whereas affliction (النقمة), whatever it may be, is in reality His withholding (إمساك) from bestowing blessing and sending down mercy; it is not a positive act (فعل ثبوتي) originating from Him, the Exalted, according to what is conveyed by His saying: ما يَفْتَحِ اللهُ لِلنَّاسِ مِنْ رَحْمَةٍ فَلا مُمْسِكَ لَها وَما يُمْسِكْ فَلا مُرْسِلَ لَهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ "Whatever mercy Allah unfolds for the people, no one can withhold it; and whatever He withholds, no one can release it after Him" (35:2).

Since fear (الخوف) and awe (الخشية) are only regarding an expected evil, and there is no evil with Him, the Glorified, the reality of the fear of Allah is a person's fear of his own evil deeds which necessitate the withholding of mercy and the cessation of the good bestowed from Him. When the human soul is struck by the remembrance of Allah, the Glorified, it first turns to the marks of deficiency (القصور) and shortcoming (التقصير) that surround it, so it is seized by a quivering of the skin and trembling in the heart. Then it turns secondly to its Lord, who is the ultimate goal of its primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — so it becomes still toward Him and finds rest in His remembrance.

It is said in Majma' al-Bayan: "Allah has described the believer here as one whose heart finds rest in the remembrance of Allah, and described him in another place as one whose heart trembles when Allah is mentioned. This is because the first refers to his remembering His reward, His favors, His countless blessings, and His incomparable bounties, so he becomes still toward Him. The second refers to his remembering His punishment and His retribution, so he fears Him and his heart trembles." (End of quote). This view is more consistent with the interpretation of those who interpret "remembrance" (الذكر) in the verse as the Noble Quran, which Allah, the Exalted, has called "remembrance" in many places of His speech, such as His saying: وَهذا ذِكْرٌ مُبارَكٌ "And this is a blessed Reminder" (21:50) and His saying: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ "Indeed We have sent down the Reminder" (15:9), and others.

However, it appears that "remembrance" (الذكر) is intended to be more general than verbal remembrance; I mean by it the absolute transition of the mind and the occurrence [of the thought] in the heart, whether it be by witnessing a sign (آية), discovering a proof (حجة), or hearing a word. A witness to this is His saying immediately after: أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ "Behold! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!" for it is like the establishment of a rule (قاعدة) that includes every remembrance, whether verbal or otherwise, and whether it be the Quran or otherwise.

His saying: أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ "Behold! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!" contains an admonition for people to turn toward Him and find relief for their hearts through His remembrance. For man has no concern in his life except attaining happiness (السعادة) and blessing (النعمة), and no fear except that wretchedness (الشقوة) and affliction (النقمة) might overtake him. Allah, the Glorified, is the sole Cause (السبب الوحيد) in whose hand are the reins of good, and to whom all matters return. He is the All-Dominant (القاهر) over His servants and the Doer of what He wills. He is the Guardian (الولي) of His servants who believe in Him and take refuge in Him. Thus, His remembrance for the soul—which is a captive in the hand of events, seeking a firm pillar that guarantees its happiness, bewildered in its affairs, not knowing where it intends nor where it is being intended for—is like the prescription of an antidote (الترياق) for the one bitten [by a snake]; his spirit expands by it and his soul finds relief with it. Relying upon Him, depending on Him, and connecting with Him is like that bitten person taking that antidote, finding within himself the vigor of health and well-being moment after moment.

Thus, every heart—according to what the plural defined by the article (الجمع المحلى باللام) implies of universality (العموم)—finds rest in the remembrance of Allah, and the anxiety and agitation within it are stilled by it. Yes, this is only in the heart that deserves to be called a heart, which is the heart that remains upon its insight (بصيرة) and right-mindedness (رشد). As for the one deviated from its origin, which neither sees nor understands, it is diverted from remembrance and deprived of tranquility and stillness. The Exalted said: فَإِنَّها لا تَعْمَى الْأَبْصارُ وَلكِنْ تَعْمَى الْقُلُوبُ الَّتِي فِي الصُّدُورِ "For indeed it is not the eyes that turn blind, but the hearts turn blind—those that are in the breasts" (22:46), and He said: لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ لا يَفْقَهُونَ بِها "they have hearts with which they do not understand" (7:179), and He said: نَسُوا اللهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ "They forgot Allah, so He forgot them" (9:67).

In the wording of the verse, there is that which indicates exclusivity (الحصر), as the object of the verb—namely His saying: بِذِكْرِ اللهِ "in Allah’s remembrance"—precedes it. This implies that hearts do not find rest in anything other than the remembrance of Allah, the Glorified. The clarification we have provided illuminates this exclusivity, for the heart of man—which is his perceiving self—has no concern but attaining its happiness and security from its wretchedness. In this, it clings to the hem of causes (الأسباب), and there is no cause but that it is dominant in one aspect and dominated in another, except for Allah, the Glorified; for He is the Dominant who is never dominated, the All-Sufficient (الغني), the Possessor of Mercy. Thus, by His remembrance—that is, by Him, the Glorified, alone—do hearts find rest. The heart does not find rest in anything other than Him except through heedlessness (غفلة) of its true state; were it to be reminded of it, it would be seized by trembling and anxiety.

Among what has been said regarding the noble verse, namely His saying: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَتَطْمَئِنُّ قُلُوبُهُمْ بِذِكْرِ اللهِ "those who have faith and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of Allah," etc., is that it is a new sentence (استئناف), and that الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا "those who have faith" is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is His saying in the following verse: طُوبى لَهُمْ وَحُسْنُ مَآبٍ "for them is a goodly state and a beautiful destination," and that His saying: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ "those who have faith and do righteous deeds" is an appositive (بدل) for the subject, and His saying: أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ "Behold! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!" is a parenthetical statement (اعتراض) between the subject and its predicate. This is a forced interpretation (تكلف) far removed from the context.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ طُوبى لَهُمْ وَحُسْنُ مَآبٍ Those who have faith and do righteous deeds—for them is a goodly state and a beautiful destination (13:29). Tuba (طوبى) is on the pattern of fu'la (فعلى) with a damma on the fa, the feminine of atyab (more pleasant). It is an attribute for a deleted noun which—as inferred from the context—is "life" (الحياة) or "livelihood" (المعيشة). This is because a blessing, whatever it may be, is only envied and enjoyed when it is pleasant (طابت) for the person, and it is not pleasant unless the heart finds rest in it, is still, and not agitated. This is not found except for one who believes in Allah and performs righteous deeds (العمل الصالح); he is the one whose heart finds rest and for whom life is pleasant, for he is secure from evil and loss, and at peace regarding what he will face and encounter. He has taken refuge in a pillar that does not crumble and has settled in the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah; his Lord does not direct toward him except that which contains his happiness—if He gives him something, it is good for him, and if He withholds, it is good for him.

He has said in describing the pleasantness of this life: مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ بِأَحْسَنِ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life and pay them their reward by the best of what they used to do" (16:97). And He said in describing one who is not granted the heart's tranquility through the remembrance of Allah: وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ أَعْمى "But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall raise him blind" (20:124). Perhaps the description of life or livelihood in the verse we are in with an increase in pleasantness (طيب) is an allusion to the fact that it is a blessing that is not devoid of pleasantness in any case, but it is more pleasant for the one whose heart finds rest in the remembrance of Allah due to its being pure from the impurities of disturbances.

Thus, His saying: طُوبى لَهُمْ "for them is a goodly state" is implicitly "for them is a life or livelihood that is most pleasant (tuba)." So tuba is the subject and "for them" (لهم) is its predicate. The indefinite subject was placed before the adverbial phrase because the speech is in the position of congratulation (التهنئة), and in such cases, that which constitutes the congratulation is brought forward to hasten the mention of what pleases the listener to hear, similar to their saying in good tidings: "Good news for you" (بشرى لك).

In short, the verse is a congratulation to those who have faith and do righteous deeds—who are those who believe and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of Allah with a continuous tranquility—of the most pleasant life or livelihood and a beautiful return. By this, its connection to what preceded it becomes clear, for the pleasantness of life is among the effects of the heart's tranquility, as previously mentioned.

It is said in Majma' al-Bayan: "طُوبى لَهُمْ (for them is a goodly state), and regarding it there are several views:

First: That its meaning is joy for them and a delight of the eye. Narrated from Ibn Abbas.

Second: Envy (غبطة) for them. Narrated from al-Dahhak.

Third: Good for them and honor. Narrated from Ibrahim al-Nakha'i.

Fourth: Paradise is for them. Narrated from Mujahid.

Fifth: Its meaning is the pleasant life for them. Narrated from al-Zajjaj. And the pleasant state for them. Narrated from Ibn al-Anbari, because it is fu'la from al-tib (pleasantness). And it is said: the most pleasant of things for them, which is Paradise. Narrated from al-Jubba'i.

Sixth: Congratulations, may life be pleasant for them.

Seventh: The best (حسنى) for them. Narrated from Qatadah.

Eighth: Excellent is what they have. Narrated from Ikrimah.

Ninth: Tuba for them is the permanence of good for them.

Tenth: That Tuba is a tree in Paradise whose root is in the house of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and in the house of every believer there is a branch from it. Narrated from Ubayd ibn Umayr, Wahb, Abu Hurayrah, and Shahr ibn Hawshab, and it is narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri in a marfu' (attributed to the Prophet) tradition." (End of the relevant portion).

Most of these meanings are by way of application (الانطباق) and are outside the literal indication of the word.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذلِكَ أَرْسَلْناكَ فِي أُمَّةٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِها أُمَمٌ Thus have We sent you to a nation before which many nations have passed away... (13:30) until the end of the verse. Matab (متاب) is a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي) for tawbah (توبة), which is "return." The indication in His saying: كَذلِكَ "Thus" is to what He, the Exalted, mentioned of His ongoing sunnah (سنة) of calling nations to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — then misguiding whom He wills and guiding whom He wills according to the system of returning to Allah, believing in Him, the heart's stillness through His remembrance, and the lack of return to Him.

The meaning is: And We have sent you to a nation before which nations have passed away, a sending that resembles this ongoing sunnah and proceeds in its affair according to this system, so that you may recite to them what We have revealed to you and convey to them what this Book contains, while they disbelieve in the All-Beneficent (الرحمن). It was said "in the All-Beneficent" rather than saying "in Us," as the apparent context would require, as an allusion to the fact that in their rejection of this revelation which the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recites to them—which is the Quran—and their lack of regard for its affair, where they say despite its revelation: لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ "Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?", they are disbelieving in a general divine mercy (رحمة إلهية عامة) that guarantees for them the happiness of their world and their Hereafter if they were to take it and act upon it.

Then He, the Exalted, commanded that he declare the word of monotheism to them, saying: قُلْ هُوَ رَبِّي لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ مَتابِ Say, ‘He is my Lord; there is no god except Him; in Him I have put my trust and to Him is my return’ (13:30). That is, He alone is my Lord without any partner as you claim, and for His Lordship over me alone, I take Him as the One who stands over all my affairs and through it, and I return to Him in my needs. By this, it becomes clear that His saying: عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ مَتابِ "in Him I have put my trust and to Him is my return" is among the effects of Lordship (الربوبية) branching from it; for the Lord is the Owner and Disposer (المالك المدبر). The sum of the meaning is: He is my Trustee (وكيلي) and to Him I return.

It is said that what is intended by matab (return) is repentance from sins, because in the first meaning, "to Him is my return" would necessarily be a confirmation (تأكيد) of His saying: "in Him I have put my trust," which is contrary to the apparent meaning.

In this, there is a denial of its returning to confirmation, and then a denial of its being contrary to the apparent meaning, which is obvious.

Some mentioned that the meaning is "to Him is my return and your return." In this, there is the necessity of deletion and estimation (حذف وتقدير) for which there is no evidence, and the mere fact that their return is to Him in reality does not necessitate estimation without there being something in the speech that necessitates it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّ قُرْآناً سُيِّرَتْ بِهِ الْجِبالُ أَوْ قُطِّعَتْ بِهِ الْأَرْضُ أَوْ كُلِّمَ بِهِ الْمَوْتى بَلْ لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ جَمِيعاً If there were a Quran by which the mountains were moved, or the earth were laid open, or the dead were made to speak... Rather, all dispensation belongs to Allah (13:31). What is intended by "moving the mountains" is uprooting them from their foundations and moving them from place to place; by "laying open the earth" is splitting it and making it piece by piece; and by "making the dead speak" is reviving them to inquire of them what happened to them after death to prove the truth of the Abode of the Hereafter, for this is what they were suggesting.

These are great matters that break the norm (خارقة للعادة), assumed as effects of a Quran that Allah, the Glorified, assumed by His saying: وَلَوْ أَنَّ قُرْآناً "And if there were a Quran..." etc. The apodosis (جزاء) of "if" (law) is deleted because the speech indicates it, for the speech is followed by His saying: بَلْ لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ جَمِيعاً "Rather, all dispensation belongs to Allah." The verses—as you have known—are directed toward clarifying that the matter of guidance does not return to the sign they suggest by their saying: لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ "Why has not a sign been sent down to him," but rather the matter belongs to Allah; He leads astray whom He wills as He led them astray, and guides to Himself whom He wills.

Upon this proceeds the context of the verses, such as His saying later: بَلْ زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مَكْرُهُمْ وَصُدُّوا عَنِ السَّبِيلِ وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ هادٍ "Rather, their plotting has been made to seem fair to the faithless, and they have been barred from the way; and whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide" (13:33), and His saying: وَكَذلِكَ أَنْزَلْناهُ حُكْماً عَرَبِيًّا وَلَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتَ أَهْواءَهُمْ "Thus have We sent it down as a code in Arabic. If you follow their desires..." (13:37), and His saying: وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ "No apostle may bring a sign except by Allah’s leave" (13:38), among others. The context of the previous verses proceeded similarly.

Thus, the deleted apodosis of law is something like our saying: "they would not have been guided by it unless Allah willed." The meaning is: Even if it were assumed that there was a Quran of such a nature that mountains were moved by it, or the earth were laid open by it, or the dead were revived and spoke by it, they would not have been guided by it unless Allah willed. Rather, the entire matter belongs to Allah; none of it belongs to anyone else, such that one might imagine that if a great, formidable, and astonishing sign were revealed, it could guide them. No, rather the matter belongs to Allah entirely, and guidance returns to His will.

Accordingly, the verse is close in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّنا نَزَّلْنا إِلَيْهِمُ الْمَلائِكَةَ وَكَلَّمَهُمُ الْمَوْتى وَحَشَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ قُبُلاً ما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ "Even if We had sent down the angels to them, and the dead had spoken to them, and We had mustered all things before them, they would not have believed unless Allah willed" (6:111).

As for what has been said: that the deleted apodosis of law is something like our saying: "it would have been this Quran," and the intention is to clarify the greatness of the Quran's status and its reaching the furthest limit in the power of expression and the influence of command, and the ignorance of the disbelievers who turned away from it and suggested a sign other than it. The meaning would be: the Quran is of such high rank and greatness of status that if it were assumed that there was a Quran by which mountains were moved, or the earth were laid open, or the dead were made to speak—the "or" in both places being for the exclusion of emptiness (منع خلو) not for the exclusion of combination (منع جمع)—it would have been this Quran. But Allah did not reveal a Quran like that. Thus, the verse is in one way similar to His saying: لَوْ أَنْزَلْنا هذَا الْقُرْآنَ عَلى جَبَلٍ لَرَأَيْتَهُ خاشِعاً مُتَصَدِّعاً مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللهِ "Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have surely seen it humbled [and] go to pieces with the fear of Allah" (59:21).

In this [view], the context of the verses, as you have known, does not support this estimation, nor is it suited by His saying after it: بَلْ لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ جَمِيعاً "Rather, all dispensation belongs to Allah," nor His saying after it: أَفَلَمْ يَيْأَسِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ لَوْ يَشاءُ اللهُ لَهَدَى النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً "Have not the faithful yet known that had Allah wished He would have guided all mankind?" as we shall point out, if Allah wills. Therefore, they were forced into interpretations regarding His saying: بَلْ لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ جَمِيعاً that are not free from strain.

It was said: the meaning is that if a Quran were to do that, it would be this Quran, but Allah, the Glorified, did not do so; rather, He did what is the case now because all dispensation belongs to Him alone.

It was said: the result of the digression (الإضراب) is that these great, miraculous matters do not occur through a Quran, but rather occur through something else that Allah willed, for all dispensation belongs to Him, the Exalted, alone.

It was said: it is better that His saying: بَلْ لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ جَمِيعاً be conjoined to a deleted phrase, the estimation being: "You have no part in the matter; rather, all dispensation belongs to Allah."

You see that the context does not support any of these meanings, and that the true meaning supported by the context is that it is a digression from the previous conditional statement itself, based on the estimation of the apodosis being something like: "they would not have been guided by it unless Allah willed."

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَلَمْ يَيْأَسِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ لَوْ يَشاءُ اللهُ لَهَدَى النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً Have not the faithful yet known that had Allah wished He would have guided all mankind? (13:31). This is a derivation (تفريع) from what preceded it.

Some mentioned that "despair" (اليأس) here means "knowledge" (العلم), which is the dialect of the Hawazin, and it is said to be the dialect of a tribe from Nakha'. They cited as evidence the saying of Suhaym ibn Wathil al-Riyahi:

I say to them at the mountain pass when they capture me, Have you not known (ألم تيأسوا) that I am the son of the rider of Zahdam?

And the saying of Rabah ibn Adi:

Have the peoples not known (ألم ييأس) that I am his son, Even if I am far from the land of the kinsfolk?

The result of the derivation based on this is: since causes (الأسباب) possess nothing of their guidance, even a Quran by which mountains were moved, or the earth were laid open, or the dead were made to speak, and that the matter belongs entirely to Allah, then it is necessary for those who believe to know that Allah did not will the guidance of those who disbelieve; had Allah willed, He would have guided all people—those who believe and those who disbelieve—but He did not guide those who disbelieve, so they were not guided and will never be guided.

Some mentioned that "despair" (اليأس) is in its well-known meaning of hopelessness (القنوط), except that His saying: أَفَلَمْ يَيْأَسِ "Have they not yet despaired" implies the meaning of knowledge. The intention is to clarify the necessity of their knowing that Allah did not will their guidance, and had He willed that, He would have guided all people, and the necessity of their despairing of their being guided and believing.

The estimation of the speech in reality is: Have those who believe not known that Allah did not will their guidance, and had He willed, He would have guided all people? Or have they not despaired of their being guided and believing? Then "despair" was made to imply the meaning of "knowledge," and only the conditional sentence was attributed to it from the objects of knowledge—namely His saying: لَوْ يَشاءُ اللهُ لَهَدَى النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً "had Allah wished He would have guided all mankind"—for the sake of conciseness and preference for brevity.

Some mentioned: that His saying أَفَلَمْ يَيْأَسِ is upon its apparent meaning without implication, and His saying: أَنْ لَوْ يَشاءُ اللهُ etc., is connected to His saying: آمَنُوا by the estimation of the preposition ba, and the object of يَيْأَسِ is deleted. The estimation of the speech is: Have those who believe not despaired—by [knowing] that had Allah willed He would have guided all people—of their faith? This is because those who believe see that all dispensation belongs to Allah, and they believe that He, the Exalted, had He willed, would have guided all people, and had He not willed, He would not guide. Since He did not guide and they did not believe, let them know that He did not will, and it is not within the power of any cause to guide them and grant them success in faith; so let them despair of their faith.

These are three views, perhaps the most balanced of which is the middle one. The verse, in any case, is not devoid of an indication that the believers were wishing for the disbelievers to believe. Perhaps due to their wishing for that, when they heard the statement of the disbelievers: لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ "Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?", they coveted their faith and hoped for their guidance if Allah were to send down another sign to them besides the Quran. So they asked the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to respond to them regarding that, and Allah made them despair of their faith in these verses, and in other verses of His speech, both Meccan and Medinan, such as His saying in Surah Ya-Sin, which is Meccan: وَسَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ "It is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith" (36:10), and His saying in Surah al-Baqarah, which is Medinan: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ "As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith" (2:6).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَزالُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا تُصِيبُهُمْ بِما صَنَعُوا قارِعَةٌ أَوْ تَحُلُّ قَرِيباً مِنْ دارِهِمْ حَتَّى يَأْتِيَ وَعْدُ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُخْلِفُ الْمِيعادَ The faithless will continue to be visited by catastrophes because of their doings, or they will land near their homes, until Allah’s promise comes to pass. Indeed Allah does not break His promise (13:31). The context of the verses bears witness that what is intended by His saying: بِما صَنَعُوا "because of their doings" is their disbelief in the All-Beneficent in the face of the true call. The catastrophe (القارعة) is the calamity that strikes (تقرع) a person with a blow, as if it were announcing something more severe than itself. In the verse is a threat and a definitive warning to those who disbelieve of a punishment that cannot be averted, and the mention of signs and conditions for it that strike them time after time until the promised punishment comes to them.

The meaning is: These who disbelieve in your true call will continue to be visited, because of the disbelief in the All-Beneficent they have committed, by a striking calamity, or that striking calamity will land near their homes. They will continue to be so until what Allah promised them of punishment comes, for Allah does not break His promise nor change His word.

Reflection upon the fact that the Surah is Meccan—as evidenced by the contents of its verses—and then upon the events occurring after the start of the mission and before the Migration, and after it until the Conquest of Mecca, suggests that what is intended by "those who disbelieve" are the disbelievers of the Arabs from the people of Mecca and others who rejected the beginning of the call, went to extremes in denial and stubbornness, and persisted in sedition and corruption.

What is intended by those whom the catastrophe visits are those who were outside the Sanctuary (الحرم) among them, whom the catastrophes of wars and the launching of raids visit. And by those near whose homes the catastrophe lands are the people of the Sanctuary from the Quraysh; evil events occur near their homes, so its harm reaches them and its terror, anxiety, and other evil effects touch them. What is intended by what He promised them is the punishment of the sword that took them in the Battle of Badr and others.

Know that this promised punishment for those who disbelieve in these verses is other than the promised punishment mentioned previously in Surah Yunus in His saying, the Exalted: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ "There is an apostle for every nation: when their apostle comes, judgment is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged" (10:47) until His saying a second time: وَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ "and judgment will be made between them with justice, and they will not be wronged" (10:54). For that which is in Surah Yunus is a general warning to the nation (الأمة), while that which is in these verses is a specific warning to those who disbelieved in the beginning of the Prophetic call from the Quraysh and others. It has already been mentioned regarding His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ "As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith" (2:6) in the first part of the book, that what is intended by "those who disbelieve" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) in the Quran when used absolutely are the stubborn ones among the polytheists of the Arabs at the beginning of the call, just as what is intended by "those who believe" (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا) when used absolutely are those who preceded in faith at the beginning of the call.

Know also that the commentators have various views regarding the verse which we have omitted, as there is no benefit in most of them. In the aspect we have mentioned is sufficiency for the reflecting researcher, and some of them will reach you in the following traditional discussion, if Allah wills.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدِ اسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فَأَمْلَيْتُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهُمْ فَكَيْفَ كانَ عِقابِ Apostles were certainly derided before you. Then I gave respite to the faithless, then I seized them; so how was My retribution? (13:32). This is a confirmation of the definitive warning in the previous verse by clarifying its counterparts which indicate the possibility of its occurrence. That is, let no one imagine that this which We threaten them with is an empty warning with no evidence of its occurrence, as they said: لَقَدْ وُعِدْنا هذا نَحْنُ وَآباؤُنا مِنْ قَبْلُ إِنْ هذا إِلَّا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ "We and our fathers were certainly promised this before. These are nothing but myths of the ancients" (27:68).

This is because apostles before you were certainly derided with disbelief and the demand for signs, just as these disbelieved in your call and then suggested a sign to you despite the presence of the sign of the Quran. So I gave respite and delay to those who disbelieved, then I seized them with punishment; so how was My retribution? Was it an empty warning with nothing behind it? Or was it a matter they could guard against, or repel, or endure? If that has occurred to them, then let these people—whose actions are like their actions—beware that the like of it should occur to them.

From this, it becomes clear that their saying that the verse is a consolation and comfort for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is out of place.

Derision (الاستهزاء) in the verse was substituted the second time with disbelief (الكفر), as it was said: "to those who disbelieved" (لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) and it was not said "to those who derided," to indicate that their derision was a derision of disbelief, just as their disbelief was a disbelief of derision. Thus, they are the disbelievers who deride the signs of Allah, like those who disbelieved in the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said, deriding the Quran while it is a sign: لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ "Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?"

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قائِمٌ عَلى كُلِّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ Is He who stands over every soul [to observe] what it earns [like those who do not]? And yet they ascribe partners to Allah... (13:33). The one who "stands over" (قائم على) something is the one who is dominant and in authority over it. The one who "stands by" (قائم بـ) something of an affair is the one who disposes of it with some kind of management. Allah, the Glorified, is the One who stands over every soul regarding what it earns. As for His standing over it, it is because He encompasses its essence, is dominant over it, and is a witness to it. As for His standing over what it earns, it is because He disposes of the matter of its deeds, transforming them from the level of motion and stillness into deeds preserved for it in the scrolls of deeds, then transforming them into rewards and punishments in this world and the Hereafter, of proximity and distance, guidance and misguidance, blessing and affliction, Paradise and Fire.

The verse is derived from what preceded it; that is, if Allah, the Glorified, guides whom He wills and thus rewards him with the best reward, and misguides whom He wills and thus punishes him with the most severe punishment, and to Him belongs all dispensation, then He is the One who stands over every soul regarding what it earns, and is the Dominant Disposer of the system of deeds. Does anyone else equal Him such that he should be shared in His divinity (ألوهيته)?

From this, it becomes clear that the predicate (الخبر) in His saying: أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قائِمٌ "Is He who stands..." etc., is deleted, indicated by His saying: وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ "And yet they ascribe partners to Allah." Among the weak statements is what was attributed to al-Dahhak: that what is intended by His saying: أَفَمَنْ هُوَ قائِمٌ عَلى كُلِّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ are the angels, because they are entrusted with the deeds. The meaning would be: Are the angels entrusted with the deeds by His command partners to Him, the Glorified? This is a meaning extremely far from the context.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ سَمُّوهُمْ أَمْ تُنَبِّئُونَهُ بِما لا يَعْلَمُ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ بِظاهِرٍ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ Say, ‘Name them! Will you inform Him of something He does not know about in the earth, or [is it just] a display of words?’ (13:33). When He, the Glorified, mentioned His saying: وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ "And yet they ascribe partners to Allah," He returned to them with a clarification by which He invalidates that statement of theirs, taken from the previous clarification in one way.

So He commanded His Prophet to argue with them with a type of argument that is wondrous in its kind, saying: قُلْ سَمُّوهُمْ "Say, ‘Name them!’"—meaning, describe them. For the attributes of things are those by which their affairs and effects are determined. If these idols were partners to Allah and intercessors (شفعاء) with Him, it would be necessary for them to have attributes that pave the way for this status, just as it is said regarding Him, the Exalted, that He is Living, All-Knowing, All-Powerful, Creator, Owner, and Disposer; thus He is the Lord of everything. But when the idols are mentioned and it is said: Hubal, or al-Lat, or al-Uzza, no attributes are found for them by which it appears that they are partners to Allah or intercessors with Him.

Then He said: أَمْ تُنَبِّئُونَهُ بِما لا يَعْلَمُ فِي الْأَرْضِ "Will you inform Him of something He does not know about in the earth?" The "or" (am) is disjunctive (منقطعة); that is, "rather, do you inform Him of such-and-such?" The meaning is that your taking the idols as partners to Him is in reality an informing of Him of what He does not know. If He had a partner in the earth, He would know of him, because it is impossible for a partner in management (التدبير) for his influence in management to be hidden from his partner. Allah, the Glorified, disposes of the entire matter and does not see for anyone else an effect in that, neither in agreement nor in opposition. The evidence that He does not see for Himself a partner in the matter is that He, the Exalted, is the One who stands over every soul regarding what it earns. In other words, to Him belongs the creation and the command, and He is a witness over everything by the proof that leaves no way for doubt. The verse, in short, is like His saying in another place: قُلْ أَتُنَبِّئُونَ اللهَ بِما لا يَعْلَمُ فِي السَّماواتِ وَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ "Say, ‘Will you inform Allah of something He does not know about in the heavens or the earth?’" (10:18).

Then He said: أَمْ بِظاهِرٍ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ "or [is it just] a display of words?"—meaning, rather, do you inform Him that He has partners by a mere display of words without reality? This is like His saying: إِنْ هيَ إِلَّا أَسْماءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ "These are but names you have coined—you and your fathers" (53:23).

It is narrated from some that what is intended by "a display of words" is the outward appearance of a book revealed from Allah in which the idols are called true gods. The result of the verse would be the negation of both the rational (عقلي) and traditional (سمعي) evidence for their divinity and their being partners to Allah, the Glorified. This is far from the wording.

The aspect of connection between these three arguments is that in their worship of idols and their ascribing partners to Allah, they are wavering between three pitfalls: either they speak of their partnership without proof, since they have no attributes by which it is known that they are partners to Allah; or they claim that they have such attributes which they know but Allah, the Glorified, does not know; or they are merely making a display of the word of their partnership without reality, and they are deceiving Allah by that—Exalted is He far above that.

Al-Zamakhshari said in al-Kashshaf: "This argumentation and its wondrous styles in which it was delivered announce for themselves with an eloquent and fluent tongue that it is not the speech of mortals, for one who knows and is just to himself." (End of his quote).

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ زُيِّنَ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مَكْرُهُمْ وَصُدُّوا عَنِ السَّبِيلِ وَمَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ هادٍ Rather, their plotting has been made to seem fair to the faithless, and they have been barred from the way; and whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide (13:33). This is a digression (إضراب) from the mentioned arguments and their implications. The meaning is: leave these arguments, for they do not ascribe partners to Him for any of these reasons; rather, it is a plot (مكر) that Satan made to seem fair to them, and by that, he barred them from the way of Allah, the Exalted. This is because they know that there is no proof for their partnership and that mere claim does not benefit them, but they want, by promoting the statement of their divinity and directing the hearts of the common people toward them, the gain and adornment of the world. Your call to the way of Allah is a barrier against that. So, in their stubbornness in worshipping them, calling people to them, and urging their adoption, they are plotting against you in one way and against the people in another way. This plot has been made to seem fair to them, and it is the reason for their ascribing them as partners, not any proof or anything else; and by that, they were barred from the way.

Thus, the plot was made to seem fair to them and they were barred by it from the way. The one who made it seem fair to them and barred them is Satan by his seduction. And they were led astray; the one who led them astray is Allah, the Glorified, by withholding the blessing of guidance from them; and whomever Allah leads astray, for him there is no guide.

His saying, the Exalted: لَهُمْ عَذابٌ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَلَعَذابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَشَقُّ وَما لَهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ واقٍ There is a punishment for them in the life of this world, and the punishment of the Hereafter is surely more hard, and they have no protector against Allah (13:34). Ashaqq (أشق) is the elative of mashaqqah (hardship), and waq (واق) is the active participle of wiqayah (protection) in the sense of preservation.

In the verse is a concise statement of what Allah promised those who disbelieve of punishment in the previous verses. In His saying: وَما لَهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ واقٍ "and they have no protector against Allah" is the negation of intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — and its influence in their right at all. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: مَثَلُ الْجَنَّةِ الَّتِي وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ ( تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ ) أُكُلُها دائِمٌ وَظِلُّها تِلْكَ عُقْبَى الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَعُقْبَى الْكافِرِينَ النَّارُ A description of the paradise promised to the God-conscious: streams run in it; its fruits and its shade are everlasting. Such is the outcome of those who are God-conscious, and the outcome of the faithless is the Fire (13:35). Mathal (المثل) is the description that represents the thing.

In His saying: مَثَلُ الْجَنَّةِ "A description of the paradise..." etc., is a clarification of what Allah has specifically promised the God-conscious (المتقون) — those who are wary of Allah's displeasure and maintain piety — of the beautiful promise, in contrast to what He threatened those who disbelieve. This serves as a preparation for what the discourse will conclude with of an allusion to the result of the striving of the two groups in their journey to their Lord and their return to Him. He has contrasted those who disbelieve with the God-conscious, indicating that those who attain this beautiful outcome are those who believe and do righteous deeds, excluding the believers who do not perform righteous deeds, for they believe in Allah but disbelieve in His signs.

Among the subtle allusions in the speech is the contrast between the believers and the polytheists first by the expression "the God-conscious and those who disbelieve" (المتقون والذين كفروا) and finally by His saying "those who were God-conscious and the disbelievers" (الذين اتقوا والكافرون). Perhaps in this is an intimation that the past tense verb and the attribute here are one in meaning, and the sum of their deeds in the world is taken as a single deed. The necessity of this is that the realization of the deed and the occurrence of the act once is the very same as their being characterized by it continuously. Our saying "the disbelievers and the God-conscious" (الكافرون والمتقون), which indicate the stability of the characterization, and our saying "those who disbelieved and those who were God-conscious" (الذين كفروا والذين اتقوا), which indicate the realization of what the verb signifies, would then convey a single meaning, which is the restriction of the described to his attribute. As for the one whose deed changes—such that disbelief or godwariness is realized from him then changes to something else and his deed has not yet been concluded—he is outside the course of the speech; so understand that.

Know that in the previous verses there are various types of shifts (الالتفات), such as His saying: كَذلِكَ أَرْسَلْناكَ "Thus have We sent you," then His saying: بَلْ لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ "Rather, all dispensation belongs to Allah," then His saying: فَأَمْلَيْتُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا "Then I gave respite to the faithless," then His saying: وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ شُرَكاءَ "And yet they ascribe partners to Allah." The reason for this is not hidden: the expression like "We sent you" (أَرْسَلْناكَ) is to indicate that there are intermediaries, such as the angels of revelation, for instance. The expression like "Rather, all dispensation belongs to Allah" (بَلْ لِلَّهِ الْأَمْرُ جَمِيعاً) is to indicate the return of every matter—whether possessing an intermediary or not—to the station of Divinity (الألوهية) which is the Sustainer (القيوم) over everything. And the expression like "Then I gave respite to the faithless, then I seized them" (فَأَمْلَيْتُ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُهُمْ) is to indicate that there is no intermediary in reality who could be a partner or an intercessor as the polytheists claim.

Then His saying, the Exalted: تِلْكَ عُقْبَى الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَعُقْبَى الْكافِرِينَ النَّارُ "Such is the outcome of those who are God-conscious, and the outcome of the faithless is the Fire" is an allusion to the conclusion of the affair of the two groups and their outcomes, as previously mentioned. With this, the discussion regarding the believers and the polytheists from the perspective of the effects of truth and falsehood in their creed and their deeds is concluded. You have known that these nine verses we are in are the completion of the ten previous verses beginning with His saying: أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً "He sends down water from the sky..." (13:17).

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Khalid ibn Najih from Imam Ja'far ibn Muhammad (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ "Behold! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!" He (peace be upon him) said: "Through Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله do hearts find rest, and he is the remembrance (ذكر) of Allah and His veil (حجاب)."

I say: And in His speech, the Exalted: قَدْ أَنْزَلَ اللهُ إِلَيْكُمْ ذِكْراً رَسُولاً "Allah has already sent down to you a reminder, an apostle" (65:10-11).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu al-Shaykh narrated from Anas who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to his companions when this verse was revealed: أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ 'Behold! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!': 'Do you know what the meaning of that is?' They said: 'Allah and His Messenger know best.' He said: 'Whoever loves Allah and His Messenger and loves my companions.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Ibn Abbas: that he said to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَتَطْمَئِنُّ قُلُوبُهُمْ بِذِكْرِ اللهِ ـ أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ "those who have faith and whose hearts find rest in the remembrance of Allah.’ Behold! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!" Then he said to me: "Do you know, O son of Umm Sulaym, who they are?" I said: "Who are they, O Messenger of Allah?" He said: "We, the People of the House (أهل البيت), and our followers (شيعتنا)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ali: that when this verse was revealed: أَلا بِذِكْرِ اللهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ "Behold! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!", the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "That is for whoever loves Allah and His Messenger and loves my People of the House sincerely, without falsehood—and loves the believers, whether present or absent. Behold! Through the remembrance of Allah do they love one another."

I say: The narrations are all by way of application (الانطباق) and flow (الجري), for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, the pure ones from his People of the House, and the elite of the companions and believers are among the instances (مصاديق) of the remembrance of Allah, because Allah is remembered through them. The noble verse is more general in its indication.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, narrated from his father from Muhammad ibn Abi Umayr from Hisham ibn Salim from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) in the narration of the Night Journey (الإسراء) of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he said: "Then there was a tree such that if a bird were sent at its base, it would not circle it for seven hundred years—and there is no station in Paradise except that there is a branch from it in it. I said: 'What is this, O Gabriel?' He said: 'This is the Tuba tree (شجرة طوبى). Allah, the Exalted, said: طُوبى لَهُمْ وَحُسْنُ مَآبٍ "for them is a goodly state and a beautiful destination."'"

I say: This meaning is narrated in many traditions. In several of them: "Gabriel handed me a fruit from it—so I ate it, and Allah transformed that into my loins—so when I descended to the earth, I cohabited with Khadijah and she became pregnant with Fatimah—so I never kissed Fatimah except that I found the scent of the Tuba tree from her."

In the book al-Khara'ij, the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "O Fatimah, indeed good tidings have come to me from my Lord regarding my brother and the son of my uncle—that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has wedded Ali to Fatimah—and He commanded Ridwan, the keeper of Paradise, so he shook the Tuba tree—and it bore scrolls to the number of the lovers of my People of the House. Then He created angels from light—and gave to every angel a script. So when the Resurrection is established with its people—the angels will not meet a lover of ours except that they hand him a document—in which is an acquittal from the Fire."

I say: In Tafsir al-Burhan, narrated from al-Muwaffaq ibn Ahmad in the book al-Manaqib with his chain of narration from Bilal ibn Hamamah from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: the like of it. This meaning was also narrated from Umm Salamah, Salman al-Farsi, and Ali ibn Abi Talib. In them: "When Allah made His angels witness the marriage of Fatimah to Ali ibn Abi Talib, He commanded the Tuba tree to scatter its fruit and what was in it of jewelry and robes—so the tree scattered what was in it and the angels and the wide-eyed houris (الحور العين) gathered it—to give it as gifts and boast of it until the Day of Resurrection." Something close to it was also narrated from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him).

In al-Majma', al-Tha'labi narrated with his chain from al-Kalbi from Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas who said: "Tuba is a tree whose root is in the house of Ali in Paradise—and in the house of every believer there is a branch from it." He said: "And Abu Basir narrated it from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him)."

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Tafsir al-Tha'labi, raising the chain to Jabir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was asked about Tuba, and he said: 'A tree in Paradise whose root is in the house of Ali and its branch is over the people of Paradise.' They said: 'O Messenger of Allah, we asked you and you said: its root is in my house and its branch is over the people of Paradise.' He said: 'My house and the house of Ali are one in Paradise, in one place.'"

I say: It was also narrated in al-Majma' with his chain from al-Hakim Abu al-Qasim al-Haskani with his chain from Musa ibn Ja'far from his father from his ancestors from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: the like of it.

I say: Regarding this meaning, there are many narrations narrated through the paths of the Shi'a and the Sunnis. It appears that the narrations are not looking at the literal interpretation (تفسير) of the verse, but rather they are looking at its inner meaning (بطن) rather than its outer meaning (ظهر). For the reality of the most pleasant life (al-ma'ishah al-tuba) is the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, the Glorified, and Ali (peace be upon him) is its possessor and the first to open its door from this nation, and the believers from the people of guardianship are his followers and partisans. His house (peace be upon him) is in the Paradise of Bliss (جنة النعيم), which is the Paradise of Guardianship, and the house of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is one; there is no difference between them nor crowding. So understand that.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ibn Jurayj regarding His saying: وَهُمْ يَكْفُرُونَ بِالرَّحْمنِ "while they disbelieve in the All-Beneficent": "This was when the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله wrote to the Quraysh at al-Hudaybiyyah—he wrote: 'In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful' (بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم). They said: 'We do not write "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن), and we do not know what "the All-Beneficent" is. We only write "In Your Name, O Allah" (باسمك اللهم).' So Allah revealed: وَهُمْ يَكْفُرُونَ بِالرَّحْمنِ 'while they disbelieve in the All-Beneficent.'"

I say: It was also narrated by Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu al-Shaykh from Qatadah. However, you are aware that the verses (الآيات), according to what their context (سياق) yields, are Meccan (مكية), whereas the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah (صلح الحديبية) is among the events that occurred after the Migration (الهجرة). Furthermore, the context of the verse itself does not support the notion that one of its parts was revealed concerning a specific story while being severed from the rest.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from ‘Atiyyah al-‘Awfi, who said: They said to Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "If only you would move the mountains of Mecca for us so that the land would expand—allowing us to farm therein—or if you would traverse the earth for us—just as the wind used to traverse it for the people of Solomon (سليمان) عليه‌السلام—or if you would revive the dead for us—just as Jesus (عيسى) used to revive the dead for his people." Thereupon, Allah the Exalted revealed: وَلَوْ أَنَّ قُرْآنًا سُيِّرَتْ بِهِ الْجِبَالُ "Had there been a Quran by which mountains were set moving..." [Qarai 13:31] until His saying: أَفَلَمْ يَيْأَسِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا "Have not the faithful yet known..." [Qarai 13:31]. He said: "Have not the faithful yet realized (أفلم يتبين)?"

They asked: "Do you narrate this tradition (الحديث) from any of the companions of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله?" He replied: "From Sa‘id al-Khudri, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله."

I say: There are other narrations (روايات) carrying a similar meaning.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, it is stated: "If any part of the Quran were to be such, it would be this."

In al-Kafi, narrated from Muhammad ibn Yahya, from Ahmad ibn Abi Zahir or someone else, from Muhammad ibn Hammad, from his brother Ahmad ibn Hammad, from Ibrahim, from his father, from Abu al-Hasan the First عليه‌السلام in a tradition: "And indeed Allah says in His Book: وَلَوْ أَنَّ قُرْآنًا سُيِّرَتْ بِهِ الْجِبَالُ أَوْ قُطِّعَتْ بِهِ الْأَرْضُ أَوْ كُلِّمَ بِهِ الْمَوْتَى 'Had there been a Quran by which mountains were set moving, or the earth were spanned, or the dead were spoken to...' [Qarai 13:31]. And we have inherited this Quran, which contains that by which mountains are moved, lands are spanned, and the dead are revived." (The tradition continues).

I say: Both traditions are weak (ضعيفان) in their chain of transmission (سندا).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir narrated from ‘Ali: That he recited: "Have not the faithful yet realized (أفلم يتبين الذين آمنوا)."

I say: This recitation (القراءة) has also been narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas.

In al-Majma‘: ‘Ali عليه‌السلام, Ibn ‘Abbas, ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام, Zayd ibn ‘Ali, Ja‘far ibn Muhammad عليه‌السلام, Ibn Abi Mulaykah, al-Jahdari, and Abu Yazid al-Madani recited: "Have not they realized (أفلم يتبين)," while the well-known recitation (القراءة المشهورة) is: "Have not they despaired (أفلم ييأس)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, it is stated: In the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: وَلَا يَزَالُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا تُصِيبُهُم بِمَا صَنَعُوا قَارِعَةٌ "A catastrophe will continue to strike the faithless because of what they have done..." [Qarai 13:31]—which is the retribution (النقمة)—أَوْ تَحُلُّ قَرِيبًا مِّن دَارِهِمْ "...or land near their habitations..." [Qarai 13:31], meaning it descends upon a people other than them, so they see it and hear of it—while those upon whom it descended are disobedient disbelievers (عصاة كفار) like them—yet they do not take heed from one another. They will continue in this state until the promise of Allah (وعد الله) comes, which He promised the believers (المؤمنين) regarding victory (النصر), and Allah disgraces the disbelievers (الكافرين).

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ ۖ وَمِنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ مَن يُنكِرُ بَعْضَهُۥ ۚ قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱللَّهَ وَلَآ أُشْرِكَ بِهِۦٓ ۚ إِلَيْهِ أَدْعُوا۟ وَإِلَيْهِ مَـَٔابِ (٣٦)
Those whom We have given the Book rejoice in what has been sent down to you. Some of the factions deny a part of it. Say, ‘Indeed I have been commanded to worship Allah and not to ascribe any partner to Him. To Him do I summon [all mankind] and to Him will be my return.’ (36)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا عَرَبِيًّا ۚ وَلَئِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَ أَهْوَآءَهُم بَعْدَ مَا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ مَا لَكَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا وَاقٍ (٣٧)
Thus We have sent it down as a dispensation in Arabic; and should you follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you, you shall have against Allah neither any guardian nor defender. (37)
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا رُسُلًا مِّن قَبْلِكَ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا وَذُرِّيَّةً ۚ وَمَا كَانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتَابٌ (٣٨)
Certainly We have sent apostles before you, and We appointed for them wives and descendants; and an apostle may not bring a sign except by Allah’s leave. There is a written [ordinance] for every term: (38)
يَمْحُوا۟ ٱللَّهُ مَا يَشَآءُ وَيُثْبِتُ ۖ وَعِندَهُۥٓ أُمُّ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (٣٩)
Allah effaces and confirms whatever He wishes and with Him is the Mother Book. (39)
وَإِن مَّا نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ وَعَلَيْنَا ٱلْحِسَابُ (٤٠)
Whether We show you a part of what We promise them, or take you away [before that], your duty is only to communicate, and it is for Us to do the reckoning. (40)
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا نَأْتِى ٱلْأَرْضَ نَنقُصُهَا مِنْ أَطْرَافِهَا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ لَا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٤١)
Have they not seen how We visit the land diminishing it at its edges? Allah judges, and there is none who may repeal His judgement, and He is swift at reckoning. (41)
وَقَدْ مَكَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْمَكْرُ جَمِيعًا ۖ يَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ۗ وَسَيَعْلَمُ ٱلْكُفَّـٰرُ لِمَنْ عُقْبَى ٱلدَّارِ (٤٢)
Those who were before them [also] schemed; yet all devising belongs to Allah. He knows what every soul earns. Soon the faithless will know in whose favour the outcome of that abode will be. (42)

(Commentary)

This is a continuation of the previous verses and a response to their saying: لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ — "Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?" (13:27).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ — Those to whom We have given the Book rejoice in what has been sent down to you (13:36) until the end of the verse. It appears that "those to whom the Book was given" refers to the Jews and the Christians, or perhaps them along with the Magians (المجوس), as this is the customary usage in the Quranic idiom. This Surah is Meccan, and history confirms that the Jews did not initially oppose the Arabian Prophethood at the beginning of the Mission (البعثة) and prior to it with the same level of animosity that the events following the Migration (الهجرة) later provoked. Indeed, a group of them entered Islam at the beginning of the Migration and bore witness to the Prophethood of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the fact that he was foretold in their books, as the Exalted says: وَشَهدَ شاهِدٌ مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ عَلى مِثْلِهِ فَآمَنَ وَاسْتَكْبَرْتُمْ — ...and a witness from the Children of Israel has testified to its like and believed, while you were regained with pride? (46:10).

Furthermore, there were among the Christians at that time a people upon the truth who did not oppose the call of Islam, such as the group of Christians from Abyssinia, according to what has been narrated regarding the story of the migration to Abyssinia, and others besides them. Regarding such people, the Exalted has said: الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ هُمْ بِهِ يُؤْمِنُونَ — Those to whom We gave the Book before it are the ones who believe in it (28:52), and He said: وَمِنْ قَوْمِ مُوسى أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ — Among the people of Moses is a nation who guide [others] by the truth and do justice thereby (7:159). Similarly, the Magians were awaiting deliverance through the appearance of a savior who would spread truth and justice, and they did not oppose the truth with the same vehemence as the polytheists (المشركون).

Thus, it appears that they are the ones intended by the verse, especially the righteous among the Christians—those who maintained that the Messiah was a human messenger, such as the Negus (النجاشي) and his companions. This is supported by the conclusion of the verse: قُلْ إِنَّما أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ اللهَ وَلا أُشْرِكَ بِهِ إِلَيْهِ أَدْعُوا — Say, ‘I have been commanded only to worship Allah and not to ascribe any partner to Him. To Him do I invite [all mankind]’ (13:36), for this address is most appropriate when directed toward the Christians.

His saying: وَمِنَ الْأَحْزابِ مَنْ يُنْكِرُ بَعْضَهُ — ...and of the factions there are those who deny part of it (13:36). The definite article (اللام) here is for specific reference (للعهد), meaning: and among the factions of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب) are those who deny part of what has been sent down to you—namely, that which indicates monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the negation of the Trinity, as well as other matters that contradict the distorted knowledge and laws held by the People of the Book.

His saying: قُلْ إِنَّما أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ اللهَ وَلا أُشْرِكَ بِهِ — Say, ‘I have been commanded only to worship Allah and not to ascribe any partner to Him’ (13:36) is evidence that the "part" they deny pertains to the oneness of worship or obedience. Allah commanded him to address them by seeking agreement upon this, saying: قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ تَعالَوْا إِلى كَلِمَةٍ سَواءٍ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللهَ وَلا نُشْرِكَ بِهِ شَيْئاً وَلا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ — Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you: that we will worship no one but Allah, that we will not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us will take others as lords besides Allah.’ (3:64).

Then He completed the statement by saying: إِلَيْهِ أَدْعُوا وَإِلَيْهِ مَآبِ — ...To Him do I invite [all mankind] and to Him is my return (13:36). Thus, the beginning of the statement expressed his objective for himself and others, while its end expressed his conduct (سيرته). That is: I have been commanded to worship Allah alone in my actions and my call, and upon that I proceed among the people; I do not invite except to Him, and I do not return in any of my affairs except to Him. Thus, the conclusion of the verse carries the same meaning as His saying: قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللهِ عَلى بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي وَسُبْحانَ اللهِ وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ — Say, ‘This is my way. I summon to Allah with insight—I and he who follows me. Immaculate is Allah, and I am not one of the polytheists.’ (12:108).

It is also possible that the intended meaning of وَإِلَيْهِ مَآبِ is the Resurrection (المعاد), in which case it serves as a justification (التعليل), meaning: I invite to Him alone because my return is to Him alone.

Some commentators have interpreted "the Book" (الكتاب) in the verse as the Quran, "those to whom the Book was given" as the companions of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and "the factions" (الأحزاب) as the desert Arabs (الأعراب) who leagued against the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and plotted against him from among the Quraysh and other tribes.

However, this is contrary to the customary Quranic usage of the phrase الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ (those to whom the Book was given). Furthermore, such an interpretation would result in the verse containing a redundant meaning.

Others have mentioned that the intended meaning refers specifically to the Jews, the Book being the Torah, and the denial by some of their factions of "part" of the Quran refers to that which does not agree with their distorted laws. This is despite the fact that all of them deny whatever does not agree with what they have, doing so without joy, while the rest were both rejoicing and denying. They have engaged in lengthy discussions regarding this.

It is narrated from some that the relative pronoun (الموصول) refers to the Muslims in general, and "the factions" refers to the Jews, Christians, and Magians. From others, it is narrated that the implied meaning of وَإِلَيْهِ مَآبِ is "to Him is my return and your return." These are opinions for which there is no evidence in the wording, and there is no benefit in prolonging the discussion about them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ أَنْزَلْناهُ حُكْماً عَرَبِيًّا وَلَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتَ أَهْواءَهُمْ بَعْدَ ما جاءَكَ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ ما لَكَ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا واقٍ — Thus have We sent it down as a dispensation in Arabic. And should you follow their desires after the knowledge that has come to you, you shall have against Allah no guardian or defender. (13:37). The demonstration in His saying كَذلِكَ (Thus) refers to the Book mentioned in the previous verse, which is the genus of the Book revealed to the past prophets, such as the Torah and the Gospel.

The intended meaning of "dispensation" (الحُكم) is the decree (القضاء) and the firm resolve (العزيمة), for that is the function of the Book sent down from heaven containing the Divine Law (الشريعة), as He said: وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ — ...and He sent down with them the Book with the truth to judge between the people concerning that about which they differed. (2:213). Thus, the Book is a Divine decree (حكم إلهي) in one respect and a judge (حاكم) among the people in another. This is the intended meaning of al-hukm, not "wisdom" (الحكمة) as has been suggested.

His saying عَرَبِيًّا (in Arabic) is an attribute of the "dispensation" (حكم) and an indication that the Book is in the Arabic tongue, which was his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله tongue, following the way of Allah (سنة الله) that has passed among His servants. The Exalted said: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسانِ قَوْمِهِ — We did not send any apostle except with the language of his people... (14:4). This—as is not hidden—is evidence that those mentioned in the previous verse are the Jews and Christians, and that these verses address their state, just as the verses preceding them addressed the state of the polytheists.

Based on this, the intended meaning of His saying وَلَئِنِ اتَّبَعْتَ أَهْواءَهُمْ (And should you follow their desires) etc., is a prohibition against following the desires of the People of the Book. Much regarding this has been mentioned in the Quran. The core of it was that they used to propose to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله a sign other than the Quran, just as the polytheists proposed, and they hoped he would follow them in the laws they possessed due to their deeming abrogation (النسخ) — the replacement of a previous divine ruling with a new one — in laws to be impossible. These two matters, especially the first, are the primary subjects addressed by these verses.

The meaning is: Just as We sent down to those who were given the Book their book, We have sent down this Quran to you in your tongue, containing a decree or acting as a judge among the people. And should you follow the desires of the People of the Book—such that you wish for a sign other than the Quran to be sent down to you as they propose, or you compromise with them and incline toward following some of the abrogated or distorted laws they possess—We would seize you with punishment, and you would have no guardian (ولي) to take charge of your affair besides Allah, nor any defender (واق) to protect you from Him. Thus, the address is to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and he is the one intended by it, not the community (الأمة) as some have mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلاً مِنْ قَبْلِكَ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُمْ أَزْواجاً وَذُرِّيَّةً وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتابٌ — Certainly We have sent apostles before you, and We appointed for them wives and descendants; and an apostle may not bring a sign except by Allah’s leave. For every term there is a writing. (13:38). When He prohibited the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله from following their desires regarding the sign they proposed he bring other than the Quran, He reminded him of the reality of the situation to despair him of any hope in that regard and to resolve him to rely upon Allah and return all matters to Him.

This reality is that the established way of Allah (سنة الله) regarding the messengers is that they are human beings, proceeding according to the customary way familiar among people, without exceeding it to possess anything belonging to the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge. They do not possess an unseen power that can effect whatever it wishes, nor are they capable of everything they desire or is desired of them, such that they could bring any sign they wished, except if Allah grants them permission. The messenger, being a human like others, has no authority in the matter; rather, all authority belongs to Allah.

He is the One who sends down the sign if He wills. However, He, the Glorified, only sends down signs when Divine wisdom (الحكمة الإلهية) necessitates it. Times are not identical or equal in their rulings and interests; otherwise, wisdom would be nullified and the order of creation would be disrupted. Rather, for every time there is a wisdom that suits it and a ruling that suits it; thus, for every time there is a sign specific to it.

This is what the verse indicates. His saying: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلاً مِنْ قَبْلِكَ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُمْ أَزْواجاً وَذُرِّيَّةً is an indication of the established way regarding the messengers in their ordinary humanity. His saying: وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ is an indication of their lack of independent unseen power to do whatever they wish, unless Divine permission (الإذن الإلهي) supports them.

His saying: لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ — meaning a limited time — كِتابٌ — meaning a decreed and written ruling (حكم مقضي مكتوب) specific to it. This is an indication of what is alluded to by the exception of permission and the way of Allah acting within it. The implied meaning is: Allah, the Glorified, is the One who sends down what He wills and permits what He wills, but He does not send down nor permit every sign at every time, for every time has a writing (كتاب) He has written, and nothing occurs within it except what is in it.

From what has preceded, it becomes clear that the view mentioned by some—that the phrase لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتابٌ is a case of inversion (القلب) and its original form is "for every writing there is a term" (لكل كتاب أجل), meaning that every book sent down from Allah has a specific time in which it is revealed and acted upon (the Torah having a time, the Gospel a time, and the Quran a time)—is a view not worth considering.

His saying, the Exalted: يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ — Allah effaces or confirms whatever He wishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book. (13:39). To efface (المحو) something is to remove its trace and mark. It is said: "I effaced the writing" when you remove the lines and marks within it. The Exalted said: وَيَمْحُ اللهُ الْباطِلَ وَيُحِقُّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ — ...and Allah will efface falsehood and confirm the truth by His words. (42:24), meaning He removes the traces of falsehood, just as He said: فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفاءً — ...As for the scum, it leaves as dross... (13:17). And He said: وَجَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ آيَتَيْنِ فَمَحَوْنا آيَةَ اللَّيْلِ وَجَعَلْنا آيَةَ النَّهارِ مُبْصِرَةً — We made the night and the day two signs. Then We effaced the sign of the night and We made the sign of the day a source of visibility... (17:12), meaning We removed the trace of visibility from the night. Thus, effacement (المحو) is close in meaning to abrogation (النسخ); it is said: "The sun abrogated the shadow" when it removes its trace and mark.

Effacement in the verse is contrasted with confirmation (الإثبات), which is to establish a thing in its place such that it does not move or waver. It is said: "I confirmed the peg in the ground" when you fix it such that it does not move or leave its center. Thus, effacement is the removal of a thing after its establishment with its mark, and it is frequently used in the context of writing.

The occurrence of His saying يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ after His saying لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتابٌ, and its connection to it on one side and to His saying وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ on the other, makes it evident that the intended meaning is the effacement and confirmation of books within their times and terms. Thus, the book that Allah confirmed in the first term, He may efface in the second term if He wills, and confirm another book; so a book continues to be effaced and another confirmed.

If we consider what is in the Book as a sign (آية), and everything is a sign, it is correct to say that He continues to efface a sign and confirm a sign, as indicated by His saying: ما نَنْسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنْسِها نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِنْها أَوْ مِثْلِها — For any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring another which is better than it, or one similar to it. (2:106), and His saying: وَإِذا بَدَّلْنا آيَةً مَكانَ آيَةٍ — When We change a verse in place of another verse... (16:101).

Thus, His saying يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ, despite its generality, serves as a justification (التعليل) for His saying لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتابٌ. The meaning is that every time has a book specific to it, so they differ. The variation of books according to the variation of times and terms only appears from the side of Divine disposal through His will, not from the side of their own variation in themselves—as if a book were inherently determined for each term such that it could not change. Rather, Allah, the Glorified, is the One who determines that by substituting one book for another, effacing one and confirming another.

His saying: وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ — ...and with Him is the Mother of the Book (13:39), meaning its source (أصله), for the "mother" (الأم) is the source from which a thing originates and to which it returns. This is a rebuttal of a potential objection and an explanation of the reality of the matter. For the variation in the state of the book written for a term—through effacement and confirmation, i.e., the changing of the written ruling and the decreed statement from time to time—might suggest that matters and decrees do not have a fixed form with Allah, the Glorified. It might suggest that His ruling merely follows external causes and factors that necessitate it, like our own rulings and decrees as conscious creatures. Or it might suggest that His ruling is haphazard (جزافي), having no inherent determination, and that nothing external influences its determination—as simple-minded people might imagine that one who possesses absolute ownership (مِلك) and absolute authority (سلطنة) may intend whatever he wills and do whatever he wants with absolute freedom, without observing any constraint, condition, or system. Thus, no fixed form for any of His acts or decrees would exist with Him. Yet the Exalted has said: ما يُبَدَّلُ الْقَوْلُ لَدَيَّ — The word is not altered with Me... (50:29), and He said: وَكُلُّ شَيْءٍ عِنْدَهُ بِمِقْدارٍ — ...and everything is with Him by measure. (13:8), among other verses.

So He rebutted this objection with His saying: وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ, meaning the source of the genus of the Book and the fixed command to which these books—which are effaced and confirmed according to times and terms—return. If the Mother of the Book itself accepted effacement and confirmation, it would be like them, not their source. And if there were no source for it, then effacement and confirmation in the acts of the Exalted would either be dependent on external matters that necessitate it—in which case He, the Exalted, would be compelled and overcome by external factors and causes like us, whereas "Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment"—or it would be dependent on nothing at all, which is haphazardness (الجزاف) that would disrupt the order of creation and the single general governance that links things together. His presence is far above such a thing. The Exalted said: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ ما خَلَقْناهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ — We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with the truth... (44:38-39).

In summary, the content of the verse is that for Allah, the Glorified, in every time and term, there is a writing (كتاب)—that is, a ruling and a decree—and that He effaces what He wills of these books, rulings, and decrees, and confirms what He wills. That is, He changes the decree fixed for one time and places in the second time another decree in its stead. However, with Him, in relation to every time, there is a decree that does not change and does not accept effacement or confirmation; it is the source (الأصل) to which the other decrees return and from which they originate, and He effaces and confirms according to what it necessitates.

From the verse, several points become clear:

First: The law of effacement and confirmation is general for all events that are subject to terms and times, which includes everything in the heavens and the earth and what is between them. The Exalted said: ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى — We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them except with the truth and for a stated term. (46:3).

This is due to the generality of His saying: يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ. The fact that the specific context (المورد) concerns the signs of Prophethood does not necessitate the restriction of the verse, for the context does not restrict the general application.

By this, the invalidity of several views becomes apparent: (1) that this refers only to legal rulings, which is abrogation (النسخ); (2) that it refers to permissible acts recorded in the scrolls of deeds, which Allah effaces and replaces with obedience or disobedience for which there is recompense; (3) that it is the effacing of the sins of believers out of grace and the confirmation of sins for disbelievers as punishment; (4) that it is in matters where supplication (الدعاء) and charity (الصدقة) have an effect, such as trials, calamities, and narrowness of livelihood; (5) that effacement is the removal of sins through repentance (التوبة) and confirmation is the changing of evil deeds into good deeds; (6) that it is the effacing of whatever generations Allah wills and the confirmation of other generations after them; (7) that it is the effacing of the moon and the confirmation of the sun, which is the effacing of the sign of the night and making the sign of the day a source of visibility; (8) that it is the effacing of this world and the confirmation of the Hereafter; (9) that it is in the souls during sleep, when Allah takes them and then sends back those He wills and withholds those He wills; (10) that it is in the lifespans (الآجال) written on the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر), where Allah effaces what He wills of them and confirms what He wills.

These and similar views are opinions for which there is absolutely no evidence in the wording to restrict the noble verse. The verse possesses an undeniable generality, and necessary observation (المشاهدة الضرورية) accords with it. For the law of change is operative in all parts of the manifest world; there is nothing that, when compared across two points in time in its existence, does not show change in its essence, attributes, and actions. At the same time, if these things are considered in themselves and according to their occurrence, they are found to be fixed and unchanging, for a thing does not change from the way it has occurred.

Thus, manifest things have two aspects: an aspect of change that entails death and life, cessation and subsistence, and types of transformation and substitution; and an aspect of fixity that does not change from what it is.
