These are either the Book of Effacement and Confirmation and the Mother of the Book themselves, or two matters resulting from the two Books. In any case, the verse is applicable to both these aspects.

Second: For Allah, the Glorified, there is in everything a fixed decree (قضاء ثابت) that does not change. By this, the invalidity of the view mentioned by some—that every decree accepts change—becomes apparent. They argued for this using scattered narrations and supplications indicating that, as well as verses and reports stating that supplication and charity repel an evil decree. The response is that such change occurs in the non-finalized decree (القضاء غير المحتوم).

Third: Decree (القضاء) is divided into changeable and unchangeable. The remainder of the discussion on this verse will be completed shortly, Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ ما نُرِيَنَّكَ بَعْضَ الَّذِي نَعِدُهُمْ أَوْ نَتَوَفَّيَنَّكَ فَإِنَّما عَلَيْكَ الْبَلاغُ وَعَلَيْنَا الْحِسابُ — Whether We show you part of what We promise them, or take you away, your duty is only to deliver [the message], and with Us is the reckoning. (13:40). إِنْ ما consists of the conditional in (إن) and the extra ma (ما) for emphasis, the evidence for which is the entry of the emphatic nun (نون التأكيد) into the verb following it.

The verse clarifies the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله function, which is to occupy himself solely with the matter of warning and delivery (التبليغ). He should not follow their desires regarding the descent of a sign upon him as they proposed, to the extent that he should not even wait for the result of his delivery or the arrival of the punishment Allah promised them.

The verse also indicates that Divine reckoning (الحساب الإلهي) occurs in this world just as it occurs in the Hereafter.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها — Have they not seen that We visit the land, reducing it from its edges? (13:41) etc. This is a discourse intended for taking heed (العبرة), presented after the threat of destruction was given to them. From this, it is known that "visiting the land and reducing it from its edges" is a metaphor for the reduction of its inhabitants through death and destruction. Thus, the verse is similar to His saying: بَلْ مَتَّعْنا هؤُلاءِ وَآباءَهُمْ حَتَّى طالَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْعُمُرُ أَفلا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها أَفَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ — Rather We enjoyed these and their fathers until old age was far advanced for them. Do they not see how We visit the land, reducing it from its edges? Is it they who will be the victors? (21:44).

The view of some—that the meaning is: "Have the people of Mecca not seen that We visit their land and reduce it from its edges by the Muslims conquering the villages one by one, so they should fear that We will conquer their city and take vengeance upon them"—is refuted by the fact that the Surah is Meccan, and those conquests only occurred after the Migration. Furthermore, the verses, with their threat, look toward their destruction in the Battle of Badr and elsewhere, not toward the conquest of Mecca.

His saying: وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ وَهُوَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ — ...Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment, and He is swift at reckoning. (13:41). He intends by this that victory belongs to Allah, the Glorified, for He judges, and there is no one against His judgment to reverse it so as to overcome Him by prevention or rejection. He, the Glorified, reckons every deed as soon as it occurs without delay, such that no one else can interfere with it. Thus, His saying وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ etc., carries the same meaning as His saying at the end of the verse from Surah al-Anbiya mentioned above: أَفَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ — ...Is it they who will be the victors? (21:44).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَدْ مَكَرَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَلِلَّهِ الْمَكْرُ جَمِيعاً — Those who were before them [also] plotted; but all plotting belongs to Allah... (13:42) until the end of the verse. That is: those before them also plotted, but their plotting did not benefit them, and they were unable to prevent Us from visiting the land and reducing it from its edges. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, owns all plotting; He nullifies it and returns it upon its perpetrators. So let them take heed.

His saying: يَعْلَمُ ما تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ — ...He knows what every soul earns... (13:42) serves as a justification for His ownership of all plotting. For plotting only succeeds when the one plotted against is ignorant of it; but if He knows it, then its nullification is with Him.

His saying: وَسَيَعْلَمُ الْكُفَّارُ لِمَنْ عُقْبَى الدَّارِ — ...and the faithless will soon know for whom is the ultimate abode. (13:42). This is a termination of the argument by asserting that the matter of things reaching their ultimate ends is among the necessary, objective realities that will not fail to occur. They will witness it with their own eyes, so there is no need for lengthy or elaborate notification; they will soon know.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated that Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim reported from Mujahid who said: "When the verse وَما كانَ لِرَسُولٍ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِآيَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ (and an apostle may not bring a sign except by Allah’s leave) was revealed, the Quraysh said: 'We do not see you, O Muhammad, possessing anything, and the matter has already been concluded.' So this verse was revealed as a threat and a warning to them: يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ (Allah effaces or confirms whatever He wishes)—meaning if We wish, We can bring about from Our command whatever We wish."

I say: The verse, as previously explained, is foreign to this meaning. At the end of this hadith, it says: "And Allah brings about in every Ramadan, so Allah effaces what He wills and confirms of the people's provisions, their calamities, what He gives them, and what He apportions for them." In another narration from Jabir from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding the verse, he said: "He effaces from the provision and increases in it, and He effaces from the term (الأجل) and increases in it." This is by way of giving examples, while the verse is more general.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was asked about His saying: يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ. He said: "That is every Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر); He raises and lowers and provides—except for life, death, wretchedness (الشقاوة), and happiness (السعادة), for those do not change."

I say: This narration, besides contradicting the very numerous narrations reported from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام, and the companions, also contradicts the generality of the verse and the proof of reason. Similar to it is what is narrated from Ibn Umar from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Allah effaces what He wills and confirms—except for wretchedness, happiness, life, and death."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh and Ibn Asakir narrated from Ali that he asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about this verse, and he said to him: "I shall certainly delight your eye with its interpretation, and I shall certainly delight the eye of my community after me with its interpretation: Charity (الصدقة) given correctly, kindness to parents, and performing good deeds transform wretchedness into happiness, increase the lifespan, and protect against evil ends."

I say: This narration does no more than mention some of the instances (مصاديق) of the verse.

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Hisham ibn Salim, Hafs ibn al-Bakhtari, and others, from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام regarding this verse: يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ. He said: "And is anything effaced except what was confirmed? And is anything confirmed except what was not?"

I say: al-Ayyashi also narrated it in his Tafsir from Jamil from him (عليه السلام).

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Fudayl ibn Yasar who said: "I heard Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام saying: 'Among matters are those that are finalized (محتومة), which will inevitably occur. And among matters are those that are suspended (موقوفة) with Allah; He brings forward in them what He wills, and He effaces what He wills and confirms of them what He wills. He has not informed anyone of those—meaning the suspended ones. As for what the messengers brought, they will occur; He does not prove Himself, His prophet, or His angels wrong.'"

I say: It is narrated through another chain, and similarly in al-Kafi with its chain from Fudayl from him عليه‌السلام with the same meaning.

In the same source, from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام who said: "Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام used to say: 'Were it not for a verse in the Book of Allah, I would have told you of what has been and what will be until the Day of Resurrection.' I said to him: 'Which verse?' He said: 'Allah said: يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ.'"

I say: Its meaning is that what appears from the verse is that Allah, the Glorified, only wants His creation to live in ignorance of future events so that they may fulfill the duties of their lives guided by ordinary causes and driven by fear and hope. The full manifestation of future events would spoil this Divine purpose. Thus, it is the reason for refraining from speaking [of the future], not the fear that Allah would prove him wrong through al-bada' (البداء) — the manifestation of a Divine decree contrary to what was previously apparent — for he is secure from that. Thus, there is no contradiction between this narration and the one before it.

In the same source, from Fudayl ibn Yasar from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام who said: "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted, wrote a book in which is what was and what is to be. He placed it before Him; so what He willed of it He brought forward, and what He willed He delayed, and what He willed He effaced, and what He willed occurred, and what He did not will did not occur."

In the same source, from Ibn Sinan from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام who said: "Indeed, Allah brings forward what He wills and delays what He wills, and effaces what He wills and confirms what He wills, and with Him is the Mother of the Book." And he said: "Every matter that Allah intends is in His knowledge before He establishes it. Nothing appears (يبدو) to Him except that it was already in His knowledge; indeed, nothing appears to Allah out of ignorance."

I say: The narrations regarding al-bada' from them عليه‌السلام are numerous and widely transmitted (مستفيضة), so no weight is given to what has been reported from some that it is a solitary report (خبر واحد).

The narration, as you see, negates al-bada' in the sense of His, the Exalted, gaining knowledge of something He was previously ignorant of—meaning a change in His knowledge in His essence, as might happen with us; Allah is far exalted above that. Rather, it is the manifestation of a command from Him, the Exalted, a second time after what was manifest from Him was contrary to it the first time. Thus, it is the effacing of the first and the confirmation of the second, while Allah, the Glorified, is knowledgeable of both.

This is something that no person of intellect can deny. For matters and events have an existence according to what their deficient causes—whether a cause, a condition, or an obstacle—necessitate, which might fail to occur. And they have an existence according to what their complete causes and reasons necessitate, which is fixed, not suspended, and does not fail to occur. The two Books—I mean the Book of Effacement and Confirmation and the Mother of the Book—are either two matters followed by these two stages of the existence of things (one of which accepts effacement and confirmation and the other accepts only fixity), or they are those two stages themselves. In any case, the manifestation of a command or a will from Him, the Exalted, after what was manifest was contrary to it, is clear and should not be doubted.

What I believe is that the dispute over the affirmation of al-bada', as appears from the traditions of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), and its negation, as appears from others, is a verbal dispute (نزاع لفظي). For this reason, we have not dedicated a separate section to this discussion as is the custom of authors. One evidence that the dispute is verbal is their arguing for the negation of al-bada' from Him, the Exalted, on the grounds that it entails a change in His knowledge—whereas that is a necessary consequence of al-bada' in the sense it is interpreted regarding us, not al-bada' in the sense it is interpreted in the reports regarding Him, the Exalted.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Hakim narrated from Abu al-Darda' that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ with the lightened form (مخففة).

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said regarding His saying نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها (reducing it from its edges): 'The passing away of the scholars.'"

In al-Majma', from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام: "We reduce it by the passing away of its scholars, its jurists, and its best people."

In al-Kafi, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Ali from the one he mentioned from Jabir from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام who said: "Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام used to say: 'What makes my soul generous regarding the speed of death or killing among us is the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا نَأْتِي الْأَرْضَ نَنْقُصُها مِنْ أَطْرافِها (Have they not seen that We visit the land, reducing it from its edges?).' He said: 'The loss of the scholars.'"

I say: It is as if the meaning is that it makes my soul generous that Allah, the Exalted, attributed the taking of the scholars to Himself and not to anyone else, so death or killing becomes pleasant for me.

Ayah

وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَسْتَ مُرْسَلًا ۚ قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِٱللَّهِ شَهِيدًۢا بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ وَمَنْ عِندَهُۥ عِلْمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ (٤٣)
The faithless say, ‘You have not been sent [by Allah].’ Say, ‘Allah suffices as a witness between me and you, and he who possesses the knowledge of the Book.’ (43)

(Commentary)

This verse is the conclusion of the Surah and connects the discourse back to its opening in His saying: المر تِلْكَ آيَاتُ الْكِتَابِ ۗ وَالَّذِي أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ الْحقُّ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ "Alif, Lam, Mim, Ra. These are the signs of the Book, and what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth, but most people do not believe" (13:1). It is a third assault against those who deny the truth of the Book of Allah, wherein He calls to witness that Allah bears witness to the Messengership, as does whoever has attained knowledge of this Book.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَسْتَ مُرْسَلًا "The faithless say, ‘You have not been sent [by Allah]’" (13:43). The structure of the discourse in this Surah is based on their denial of the truth of the Book and their refusal to consider it a divine sign (آية) of the Messengership. For this reason, they repeatedly proposed signs other than it, as Allah the Exalted recounted throughout the verses time and again, answering them with that which refuted their claims. It is as if, when they despaired of obtaining what they proposed, they denied the very basis of the Messengership due to their lack of submission to what Allah had sent down as a sign and His lack of response to the signs they proposed. Thus, they would say: "You have not been sent."

Therefore, Allah prompted His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with the proof (الحجة) against them regarding his Messengership by saying: قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيدًا بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ وَمَنْ عِندَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ "Say, ‘Allah suffices as a witness between me and you, and he who possesses the knowledge of the Book’" (13:43). This is a decisive proof; it is neither rhetorical speech nor a referral to something for which there is no way to attain knowledge.

His saying: قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيدًا بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ "Say, ‘Allah suffices as a witness between me and you’" is an invocation of Allah, the Glorified, as a witness, for He is the Guardian of the matter of sending Messengers. This is a witness of delivery (شهادة تأدية) — the actual communication of the testimony — and not merely a witness of endurance (شهادة تحمل) — the mere internal possession of knowledge. Indeed, verses such as His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّكَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ عَلَى صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ "Indeed you are one of the apostles, on a straight path" (36:3-4) are among the signs of the Quran. Its being a miraculous sign (آية معجزة) from Allah is a necessity (ضروري), its being His speech and word is a necessity, and its inclusion of the confirmation of the Messengership through its denotative correspondence (دلالة المطابقة) — which relies on necessary knowledge — is also a necessity. We mean nothing by "witness of delivery" except this.

Among the commentators, those who interpreted His testimony, the Exalted, as meaning that He has manifested such proofs and arguments for my Messengership that there is no need for the testimony of another witness, and then said: "Naming this 'testimony' — even though it is an act (فعل) while testimony is a word (قول) — is metaphorical (مجاز) since it serves the same purpose, or rather, it is stronger than it," have sought the objective through the wrong path.

This is because the proofs and arguments indicating the truth of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) Messengership are either the Quran — which is the eternal miraculous sign — or other supernatural events and miracles. The verses of this Surah — as you can see — do not answer the disbelievers regarding what they proposed of this second category. There is no meaning, then, in invoking as a witness that which they were not answered with. As for the Quran, it is clear that relying upon it is from the perspective that it is a miracle confirming the Messengership through its indication of it; that is, it is the speech of the Exalted witnessing to the Messengership. If this is the case, what is the meaning of turning away from its being His speech indicating the truth of the Messengership — which is a verbal testimony (شهادة لفظية) from the Exalted in the literal sense of testimony — to its being a practical proof (دليل فعلي) from Him, metaphorically called testimony?

Furthermore, the claim that His act is a stronger indication of that than His word is untenable.

Thus, it is established that the meaning of His saying: اللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ "Allah is a witness between me and you" is that the confirmation of the Messengership occurring in the Quran is a divine testimony to that effect.

As for making the testimony a "witness of endurance" (شهادة تحمل), it corrupts the meaning at its root. What meaning is there in referring a disputed matter to the knowledge of Allah and taking that as a proof against an opponent when he has no way to access what is in the knowledge of Allah regarding his affair? Is it as he says, or is it a fabrication he invents against Allah?

His saying: وَمَنْ عِندَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ "and he who possesses the knowledge of the Book" means: and he who possesses the knowledge of the Book suffices as a witness between me and you. Some have mentioned that the "Book" refers to the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), which would necessitate that the relative pronoun (الموصول) refers to Allah, the Glorified. It would be as if it were said: "Allah, who possesses the knowledge of the Book, suffices as a witness..."

There are two issues with this: First, it contradicts the apparent meaning of the conjunction (العطف). Second, it would be the conjunction of the Essence (الذات) with its attribute to the Essence itself, which is unseemly and impermissible in eloquent speech. For this reason, when al-Zamakhshari in al-Kashshaf cited this view from al-Hasan, saying: "From al-Hasan: 'No, by Allah, He means none but Allah,'" he followed it by saying: "The meaning is: He who is worthy of worship and He who alone knows the knowledge of what is in the Tablet suffices as a witness between me and you." Thus, he resorted to a device to rectify it by substituting the name of majesty بِاللَّهِ (by Allah) with "He who is worthy of worship" and substituting مَنْ (who) with "He who [knows]," so that the conjoined and the conjoined-to become two attributes. It would then be in the sense of conjoining one of the attributes of the Essence to the other, and linking the ruling to the Essence by virtue of its possessing both attributes, as they both play a role in it. Understand this.

However, it is well known that substituting one word for another to make a meaning sound correct does not necessitate that the original wording is correct; otherwise, the rules of linguistics would be nullified.

Furthermore, reflecting on what was previously mentioned regarding the meaning of this testimony — that it refers to the Quran’s confirmation of the Messengership of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) — shows that the placement of the name of majesty in this position is not to hint at its descriptive meaning, but rather to attribute the testimony to the Sacred Essence (الذات المقدسة) which encompasses all attributes of perfection. This is because His testimony is the greatest of testimonies. The Exalted said: قُلْ أَيُّ شَيْءٍ أَكْبَرُ شَهَادَةً ۖ قُلِ اللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ "Say, ‘What thing is greatest as a witness?’ Say, ‘Allah! [He is] witness between me and you’" (6:19).

Others mentioned that the "Book" refers to the Torah and the Gospel, or specifically the Torah. The meaning would be: "The scholars of the Book suffice as witnesses between me and you," because they know what Allah promised the Prophets and they read my description in the Book.

The problem with this is that what is stipulated in the verse is "testimony" (الشهادة), not mere "knowledge" (العلم). The Surah is Meccan (مكية), and none of the scholars of the People of the Book had believed at that time, as has been said, nor had they testified to the Messengership in any way. There is no meaning in arguing by relying on a testimony that no one had yet provided.

It has been said: It refers to the group who embraced Islam from the scholars of the People of the Book, such as Abdullah ibn Salam, Tamim al-Dari, al-Jarud, and Salman al-Farisi. It was also said it refers specifically to Abdullah ibn Salam. This was refuted on the basis that the Surah is Meccan, and these individuals only embraced Islam in Medina.

Those who claim it is Abdullah ibn Salam have exerted great effort in defending this view. Some said: The fact that the Surah is Meccan does not preclude some of its verses from being Medinan (مدنية); so why is it not possible for this verse to be Medinan while the Surah is Meccan?

To this, we say: First, mere possibility does not establish a fact unless there is a sound report (نقل صحيح) that can be relied upon. Furthermore, the majority have explicitly stated that it is Meccan, as reported in al-Bahr.

Second: That [mixing of verses] only occurs in some verses placed within the verses of the revealed Surahs. As for a verse like this, which is the conclusion and looks back to the opening of the Surah, it is not so; for there is no meaning in deferring a part of a discourse whose parts are interconnected for an indefinite period.

Some said: The fact that the verse is Meccan does not preclude the speech from being an announcement of what will be testified to in the future.

The problem with this is that it renders the proof weak and invalid. What meaning is there in arguing against a people who say, "You have not been sent," by saying: "Believe in it today because some scholars of the People of the Book will testify to it in the future"?

Some said: This testimony is a "witness of endurance" (شهادة تحمل) which does not necessitate the faith of the witness at the time of the testimony. Thus, it is possible for the verse to be Meccan and refer to Abdullah ibn Salam or other scholars of the Jews and Christians, even if they had not believed at the time the verse was revealed.

The problem here is that the meaning then reverts to arguing by the knowledge of the scholars of the People of the Book even if they did not acknowledge it or believe. If that were the case, it would have been more appropriate to invoke the knowledge of the disbelievers themselves, for the proof had been completed against them by the Quran being the word of Allah, and that could only be through their knowledge of it. What would be the reason for turning away from them to others when they all share in disbelieving in the Messengership and denying it? Furthermore, it was previously stated that the testimony in the verse is nothing but a "witness of delivery" (شهادة أداء), not merely endurance.

Some — namely Ibn Taymiyyah, who came up with something quite strange — said that the verse is Medinan by consensus. This is as you see [unfounded].

Others mentioned that the "Book" refers to the Noble Quran. The meaning is that whoever has carried this Book, realized its knowledge, and become distinguished by it, testifies that it is from Allah and that I am sent with it. Thus, the end of the Surah returns to its beginning in His saying: تِلْكَ آيَاتُ الْكِتَابِ ۗ وَالَّذِي أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ الْحَقُّ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ "These are the signs of the Book, and what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth, but most people do not believe" (13:1). Its end reflects its beginning and what is in its middle, such as His saying: أَفَمَن يَعْلَمُ أَنَّمَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ الْحَقُّ كَمَنْ هُوَ أَعْمَىٰ ۚ إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ "Is he who knows that what has been sent down to you from your Lord is the truth like him who is blind? Only those who possess intellect take admonition" (13:19).

This is, in reality, a victory and support from the Exalted for His Book in the face of the disparagement and belittlement of the faithless, who said: لَوْلَا أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ آيَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِ "Why has not a sign been sent down to him from his Lord?" (13:7, 13:27) time and again, and "You have not been sent." They gave no weight to his affair and paid no heed to him. Allah answered their words time and again without addressing the matter of the Quran or mentioning that it is the greatest sign of the Messengership, though that was necessary. Thus, His saying: قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيدًا بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ وَمَنْ عِندَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ "Say, ‘Allah suffices as a witness between me and you, and he who possesses the knowledge of the Book’" is the fulfillment of this necessary objective without which the clarification would be incomplete. This is one of the best evidences that the verse, like the rest of the Surah, is Meccan.

This supports what a group has mentioned and what has been reported in narrations through the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), that the verse was revealed concerning Ali (عليه السلام). If the saying وَمَنْ عِندَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ "and he who possesses the knowledge of the Book" were to apply to anyone who believed in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) at that time, it would be him. He was the most knowledgeable of the Ummah regarding the Book of Allah, and sound narrations to that effect are numerous. Even if nothing had been reported concerning him except the saying of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the Hadith of the Two Weighty Things (الثقلين) — which is mutawatir (متواتر) — successively narrated by so many chains that its authenticity is beyond doubt — through the paths of both groups: "They shall never separate until they return to me at the Pond," it would have been sufficient.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Basa'ir, with its chain of narration from Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), who said regarding the verse: "It is Ali (عليه السلام)."

I say: It was also narrated with chains from Jabir, Burayd ibn Mu'awiyah, and Fudayl ibn Yasar from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام); and with its chain from Abdullah ibn Bukayr and Abdullah ibn Kathir al-Hashimi from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام); and with its chain from Salman al-Farisi from Ali (عليه السلام).

In al-Kafi, with its chain from Burayd ibn Mu'awiyah regarding the verse, he said: "He meant us, and Ali is the first of us, the most excellent of us, and the best of us after the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

In al-Ma'ani, with its chain from Khalaf ibn Atiyyah al-Awfi from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: "I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about the saying of Allah, Great is His Praise: قَالَ الَّذِي عِندَهُ عِلْمٌ مِّنَ الْكِتَابِ 'He who possessed some knowledge of the Book said' (27:40). He said: 'That was the successor (وصي) of my brother Solomon son of David.' I said to him: 'O Messenger of Allah, what about the saying of Allah: قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيدًا بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ وَمَنْ عِندَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ "Say, ‘Allah suffices as a witness between me and you, and he who possesses the knowledge of the Book’"?' He said: 'That is my brother Ali ibn Abi Talib.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abdullah ibn Ata, who said: "I said to Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام): 'This son of Abdullah ibn Salam ibn Imran claims that his father is the one about whom Allah says: قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيدًا بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ وَمَنْ عِندَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ.' He said: 'He has lied; it is Ali ibn Abi Talib.'"

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Shahr Ashub, who said: From Muhammad ibn Muslim, Abu Hamzah al-Thumali, and Jabir ibn Yazid from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام); and Ali ibn Faddal and Fudayl ibn Dawud from Abu Basir from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام); and Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Kalbi and Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl from al-Rida (عليه السلام). It has also been narrated from Musa ibn Ja'far (عليه السلام), from Zayd ibn Ali, from Muhammad ibn al-Hanafiyyah, from Salman al-Farisi, from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, and Ismail al-Suddi, that they said regarding His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيدًا بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ وَمَنْ عِندَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ: "It is Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام)."

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from al-Tha'labi in his Tafsir, with his chain from Mu'awiyah from al-A'mash from Abu Salih from Ibn Abbas; and it was narrated from Abdullah ibn Ata from Abu Ja'far: that it was said to him: "They claim that the one who possesses the knowledge of the Book is Abdullah ibn Salam." He said: "No, that is Ali ibn Abi Talib." It was also narrated that Sa'id ibn Jubayr was asked: "Is وَمَنْ عِندَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ Abdullah ibn Salam?" He said: "No, and how could it be? This Surah is Meccan."

I say: It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Nahhas in his Nasikh from Ibn Jubayr.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, also from the jurist Ibn al-Maghazili al-Shafi'i, with his chain from Ali ibn Abis, who said: "Abu Maryam and I entered upon Abdullah ibn Ata. He said: 'O Abu Maryam, tell Ali the narration you told me from Abu Ja'far.' He said: 'I was sitting with Abu Ja'far when the son of Abdullah ibn Salam passed by him. I said: "May Allah make me your ransom, this is the son of the one who possesses the knowledge of the Book." He said: "No, but it is your companion Ali ibn Abi Talib, concerning whom verses from the Book of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, were revealed: وَمَنْ عِندَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ, أَفَمَن كَانَ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِ وَيَتْلُوهُ شَاهِدٌ مِّنْهُ 'Is he who stands on a manifest proof from his Lord, and whom a witness from him follows' (11:17), and إِنَّمَا وَلِيُّكُمُ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ 'Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle...' (5:55)."'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Mardawayh extracted through the path of Abd al-Malik ibn Umayr that Muhammad ibn Yusuf ibn Abdullah ibn Salam said: "Abdullah ibn Salam said: 'Allah has sent down in the Quran: قُلْ كَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيدًا بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ وَمَنْ عِندَهُ عِلْمُ الْكِتَابِ.'"

I say: What is similar in meaning was narrated from Ibn Mardawayh from Zayd ibn Aslam from his father and from Jundub. You have already seen the status of such narrations in what preceded. It has also been narrated from Ibn al-Mundhir from al-Sha'bi: "Nothing was revealed in the Quran concerning Abdullah ibn Salam."

Completed, and praise be to Allah.
